AI.tldr: C3R is a drop-in control layer that certifies per-domain contamination budgets using inferred domain posteriors and risk-controlling prediction sets, guaranteeing reductions on hardest domains while abstaining rather than silently violating, with strong empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Standard retrieval metrics and conformal risk control methods fail to handle mixed-domain corpora, under-covering worst domains and silently violating contamination budgets when evidence comes from wrong domains.
AI.method: C3R uses a two-split scheme built on risk-controlling prediction sets with a finite-sample transfer bound that crosses from inferred to true domain, supports heterogeneous budgets, and inverts for deployment.
AI.result: Across 1,000 resampled calibrations, the certificate never violates (stability) while marginal control violates in every draw. Soft demotion retains more recall than strongest calibrated cascade at equal certified contamination. Results replicate across open testbeds including federal regulations and LLM-judged probes.
AI.conclusion: C3R provides a frozen-stack, reranker-agnostic control layer that effectively certifies per-domain contamination, abstains when uncertain, and guarantees reduction on hardest domains.

title: RegNetAgents: A Multi-Agent Framework for Cross-Network Regulatory Driver Identification in Cancer Genomics
summary: We introduce RegNetAgents, an AI-oriented multi-agent framework for structured, query-driven regulatory candidate identification across heterogeneous gene regulatory networks. The system enables unified analysis of bulk tumor and single-cell-derived ARACNe networks by integrating TCGA-derived cancer networks with large-scale single-cell regulatory networks from the GREmLN project. For a given focal gene, the framework performs dual-network classification, cancer gene filtering using OncoKB annotations, and mode-of-action (MoA) assignment for tumor-derived regulatory relationships. Candidates are ranked by evidence consistency across networks (Both, TCGA-only, GREmLN-only). The system is implemented as a multi-agent LangGraph DAG workflow, accessible through a unified Python API and Model Context Protocol (MCP) client, operating as a downstream analytical layer over precomputed regulatory networks rather than a network inference method. Across eleven breast cancer (BRCA) and twelve colorectal cancer (COAD) focal genes, RegNetAgents identifies candidate regulators significantly enriched for OncoKB-annotated cancer genes. TCGA-derived candidates show strong enrichment (Stouffer Z = 6.69 for BRCA and 6.95 for COAD), while GREmLN-derived candidates also demonstrate significant enrichment (Z = 5.51 for BRCA and 7.06 for COAD; all p < 0.0001). No enrichment is observed in housekeeping or non-driver control gene sets, supporting signal specificity. An extended module enables structured evaluation of oncogenic potential, druggability, clinical relevance, and network vulnerability, supporting end-to-end interpretation from candidate identification to biological hypothesis generation. RegNetAgents establishes an interpretable AI framework for cross-network regulatory candidate identification in cancer genomics.

AI.tldr: RegNetAgents is an AI multi-agent framework that identifies regulatory candidates across bulk tumor and single-cell gene regulatory networks (TCGA and GREmLN). It classifies dual-network relationships, filters cancer genes via OncoKB, and assigns mode-of-action. Tested on 11 BRCA and 12 COAD focal genes, it shows significant enrichment for known cancer genes (Stouffer Z up to 7.06, p<0.0001) with no signal in control sets, supporting specificity.
AI.motivation: To enable structured, query-driven regulatory candidate identification across heterogeneous (bulk and single-cell) gene regulatory networks, overcoming the fragmentation of cancer genomics data sources.
AI.method: Multi-agent framework (LangGraph DAG) integrating TCGA and GREmLN ARACNe networks, with dual-network classification, OncoKB-based cancer gene filtering, and mode-of-action assignment. Implemented as Python API and MCP client.
AI.result: Significant enrichment of OncoKB cancer genes among identified regulators (BRCA: Z=6.69 TCGA, Z=5.51 GREmLN; COAD: Z=6.95 TCGA, Z=7.06 GREmLN; all p<0.0001). No enrichment in housekeeping or non-driver controls.
AI.conclusion: RegNetAgents provides an interpretable, cross-network AI framework for cancer regulatory candidate identification, supporting end-to-end hypothesis generation from candidate discovery to biological interpretation.

title: Adaptive Score-Based VAMP: Self-Tuning Hyperparameters via Tilted EM
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures. A short version of this paper has been submitted to the 2026 ESS Symposium (ESSS 2026)
summary: Approximate-message-passing methods offer fast Bayesian inference for high-dimensional inverse problems, but their performance and state-evolution predictions rely on correctly specified module parameters. This paper develops an adaptive version of score-based vector approximate message passing (SC-VAMP). Each parameterized factor is updated by a local tilted expectation-maximization (EM) step that reuses the tilted moments already computed by the single-input single-output module interface. Under standard large-system state-evolution assumptions and identifiability conditions, the matched parameters form a Bayes-optimal population fixed point of the adaptive recursion. The argument is written separately for prior modules and likelihood/LMMSE modules, the latter using the Gaussian cavity induced by the VAMP transformed-error model. Numerical results for linear and one-bit Bernoulli-Gaussian compressed sensing show that the proposed updates recover near-oracle performance from strongly mismatched initializations.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an adaptive version of SC-VAMP that uses local tilted EM steps to update module parameters, achieving near-oracle performance even from mismatched initializations in high-dimensional Bayesian inference.
AI.motivation: Approximate-message-passing methods rely on correctly specified module parameters for optimal performance, but these are often unknown or mismatched in practice.
AI.method: Each parameterized factor is updated via local tilted EM steps reusing tilted moments from the SISO module interface; separate analyses are provided for prior modules and likelihood/LMMSE modules using the Gaussian cavity from VAMP's transformed-error model.
AI.result: Under standard large-system assumptions and identifiability conditions, the adaptive updates converge to a Bayes-optimal population fixed point. Numerical experiments on linear and one-bit Bernoulli-Gaussian compressed sensing show near-oracle performance recovery from strongly mismatched initializations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive SC-VAMP effectively learns unknown module parameters online, restoring near-optimal Bayesian inference performance without requiring correct prior specification.

title: DCVC-MB: Neural B-Frame Video Compression using State Space Models
summary: In this paper we propose DCVC-Mamba (DCVC-MB), a neural video codec framework for B-frame coding. Our approach incorporates an IBP frame strategy for low-delay B-frame coding, a spatio-temporal fusion model based on state-space models for bidirectional temporal prediction, and an entropy-aware skipping mechanism that selectively omits coding certain latents to reduce entropy coding times. In addition to our model contributions we also implement two inference-time strategies that enhance compression performance. Experimental evaluation shows that DCVC-MB compares favorably to existing NVCs and traditional codecs. The method demonstrates BD-rate reductions of up to $8.98\%$ on average compared to prior neural video codecs, and improvements of up to $30.45\%$ and $1.81\%$ over the VTM-19.0-LDP and VTM-19.0-RA(Inter-GoP=16) benchmarks, respectively, contributing to advances in neural video compression.

AI.tldr: DCVC-Mamba (DCVC-MB) is a neural video codec for B-frame coding using IBP frame strategy, state-space models for bidirectional prediction, entropy-aware skipping, and inference-time strategies, achieving significant BD-rate reductions over prior NVCs and traditional codecs like VTM-19.0.
AI.motivation: To improve neural video compression performance for B-frame coding by addressing limitations in temporal prediction, entropy coding efficiency, and bidirectional modeling.
AI.method: Proposes DCVC-MB with (1) IBP frame strategy for low-delay B-frame coding, (2) spatio-temporal fusion model using state-space models (SSMs) for bidirectional temporal prediction, (3) entropy-aware skipping mechanism to reduce entropy coding time, and (4) two inference-time strategies to boost compression.
AI.result: Achieves average BD-rate reductions of 8.98% over prior NVCs, and 30.45% / 1.81% over VTM-19.0-LDP and VTM-19.0-RA(Inter-GoP=16) benchmarks respectively.
AI.conclusion: DCVC-MB advances neural video compression, outperforming both existing neural and traditional video codecs.

title: Quasi-Belief Propagation and Neural-Network Check Node Processing for BCH Codes
summary: This paper proposes a quasi-BP decoding scheme for BCH codes that preserves the parallelizable structure of belief propagation while exploiting code automorphisms and optimized redundant parity-check matrices. To eliminate the computationally expensive $\tanh$ and $\tanh^{-1}$ functions in check node updates, we further introduce a neural-network-based variant that replaces them with a lightweight convolutional neural network trained under a triple-constraint loss function enforcing non-negativity and order consistency. Simulation results for three BCH codes demonstrate that quasi-BP decoding achieves competitive frame error rate performance, with a gap within 0.25 decibels compared with belief propagation decoding of an LDPC code of similar blocklength. The neural-network-based variant incurs negligible performance loss while enabling stable deployment with arithmetic operations on hardware accelerators. Concatenation with an ordered statistics decoding variant further bridges the gap to the maximum-likelihood bound. Hence, the proposed schemes offer a viable path toward high-throughput, low-latency decoding of BCH codes in next-generation communication systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a quasi-BP decoding scheme for BCH codes that maintains parallelizability of belief propagation while using code automorphisms and optimized parity-check matrices, plus a neural-network variant that replaces tanh/tanh⁻¹ functions with a lightweight CNN trained under triple-constraint loss. Results show competitive performance within 0.25 dB of LDPC BP decoding, with the NN variant enabling efficient hardware deployment.
AI.motivation: To achieve high-throughput, low-latency decoding of BCH codes for next-generation communication systems by addressing the computational bottlenecks (tanh/tanh⁻¹ functions) in traditional belief propagation decoding.
AI.method: Quasi-BP decoding scheme preserving parallelizable BP structure, exploiting code automorphisms and optimized redundant parity-check matrices. A neural-network variant replaces tanh/tanh⁻¹ functions with a lightweight CNN trained under a triple-constraint loss function (enforcing non-negativity and order consistency).
AI.result: Quasi-BP decoding achieves FER performance within 0.25 dB of LDPC BP decoding. The NN variant shows negligible performance loss with stable arithmetic operations on hardware accelerators. Concatenation with ordered statistics decoding variant further closes the gap to the ML bound.
AI.conclusion: The proposed schemes offer a viable path toward high-throughput, low-latency decoding of BCH codes, suitable for next-generation communication systems.

title: Domain Adaptation of Mismatched Proximal Denoiser for Plug-and-Play Image Reconstruction
summary: Plug-and-play proximal gradient descent (PnP-PGD) enables flexible image reconstruction by using denoisers as implicit priors. In practice, these denoisers are often deployed outside their training domains. Existing analyses establish convergence under structural assumptions on the deployed denoiser, such as requiring it to be a proximal map or a contraction. However, they do not measure how domain mismatch affects convergence of PnP-PGD. We define this effect as \emph{proximal mismatch}: the discrepancy between a deployed denoiser $\widehat{\mathsf D}$ and a target-domain reference map $\mathsf D_\star=\operatorname{prox}_{R_\star}$ associated with the underlying regularizer $R_\star$. Under this mismatch, each denoising update becomes an inexact proximal step for the target objective. We further derive a stationarity bound that decays at a rate of $\mathcal{O}(1/K)$, with an additive term proportional to the average squared proximal mismatch. This result motivates adaptation via proximal matching rather than MSE-based adaptation alone. We study this approach with two established denoiser families: learned proximal networks and gradient-step denoisers. Experiments on Gaussian deblurring and super-resolution under substantial domain shift show that proximal matching adaptation improves reconstruction quality significantly over MSE-based adaptation, yielding the largest numerical gains in the few-shot regime.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how domain mismatch (proximal mismatch) affects convergence of Plug-and-Play Proximal Gradient Descent (PnP-PGD), deriving an O(1/K) stationarity bound and proposing proximal matching adaptation over MSE-based adaptation, with experiments showing significant reconstruction improvements under domain shift.
AI.motivation: Existing PnP-PGD convergence analyses require structural assumptions (proximal map, contraction) but do not measure how domain mismatch affects convergence. The authors aim to characterize this effect and propose better adaptation strategies.
AI.method: Defines proximal mismatch as discrepancy between deployed denoiser and target-domain reference map. Derives stationarity bound O(1/K) with additive term proportional to average squared proximal mismatch. Studies proximal matching adaptation on learned proximal networks and gradient-step denoisers.
AI.result: Proximal matching adaptation improves reconstruction quality significantly over MSE-based adaptation, with largest gains in the few-shot regime, tested on Gaussian deblurring and super-resolution under substantial domain shift.
AI.conclusion: Proximal mismatch is a key factor affecting PnP-PGD convergence under domain shift, and proximal matching is a more effective adaptation strategy than MSE-based approaches.

title: MEMORA: Embodied Action Memory from Egocentric Videos for Reasoning and Planning
comment: 43 pages, 9 figures. Oral presentation at the Robotics: Science and Systems 2026 Workshop on Foundation Models for Robot Planning (FM4RoboPlan)
summary: Long-horizon robot planning requires more than predicting what actions will do next; it also requires memory of the embodied experience that makes future goals interpretable. People do not plan from the present scene alone: they draw on remembered places, object-state changes, prior procedures, and regularities revealed through repeated action. We formulate Embodied Action Memory (EAM) as the capability to form, maintain, and use such experience as a persistent memory state for later decisions. MEMORA realizes EAM with a formation-consolidation-retrieval lifecycle and four typed stores: Environment Memory, Entity Memory, Activity Memory, and Inferred Knowledge. Online editing maintains object identities and state histories as new observations arrive; offline consolidation abstracts repeated experience into reusable procedures and participant-specific regularities. MEMORA-Bench evaluates this lifecycle on 45 hours of EPIC-KITCHENS-100 extension video across 18 participants through memory-grounded planning, including previously unseen goals, and a complementary memory-assessment task. Across four open-weight language models, full MEMORA--combining editing, typed stores, and consolidation--achieves the strongest aggregate results among the evaluated memory conditions. It improves memory-assessment accuracy by up to 20.5 points over the strongest controlled baseline and improves out-of-distribution Robot-Grounded Plan score by up to 16.6% relative. A qualitative two-task robot deployment study further illustrates how memory-grounded language plans can interface with downstream control, while the overall results show that editable, consolidated memory can supply remembered context for robot planning. Project page: https://yuzihaowashu.github.io/MEMORA/

AI.tldr: MEMORA introduces Embodied Action Memory (EAM) with a formation-consolidation-retrieval lifecycle using four typed stores (Environment, Entity, Activity, Inferred Knowledge) to improve long-horizon robot planning. Evaluated on 45 hours of EPIC-KITCHENS-100 video across 18 participants, it achieves up to 20.5% higher memory-assessment accuracy and 16.6% better out-of-distribution plan scores over baselines.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon robot planning requires more than predicting immediate actions; it needs persistent memory of embodied experience (places, object states, procedures, regularities) to make future goals interpretable, which current systems lack.
AI.method: MEMORA implements a three-phase lifecycle (formation, consolidation, retrieval) with four typed memory stores: Environment Memory (places), Entity Memory (object identities/state histories), Activity Memory (procedures), and Inferred Knowledge (regularities). Online editing updates object states; offline consolidation abstracts repeated experiences into reusable procedures.
AI.result: Across four open-weight language models, MEMORA achieves up to 20.5% higher memory-assessment accuracy and 16.6% relative improvement in out-of-distribution Robot-Grounded Plan scores over the strongest baseline. A qualitative robot deployment study shows feasible integration with downstream control.
AI.conclusion: Editable, consolidated memory (typed stores with lifecycle management) effectively supplies remembered context for robot planning, demonstrating that structured persistent memory improves long-horizon task performance.

title: QFireNet: A Quantum-Enhanced U-Net for Wildfire Segmentation from Sentinel-2 Imagery
summary: Wildfire detection from satellite imagery is a semantic image segmentation problem that has proven to be difficult due to challenges such as class imbalance, feature complexity, and atmospheric interference. In this paper, we build on the foundational U-Net image segmentation model to develop a quantum-hybrid solution in hopes of more effectively modeling the high-dimensional spectral feature space of the Sen2Fire dataset. We inject a variational quantum circuit in the bottleneck portion of U-Net, specifically the QuFeX and QB-Net ansatzes. We test a classical Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) for further comparative analysis of the model, and we also explore classical improvements to the U-Net model and its training process, including a compression of parameters, alternative loss functions, and uniform mixing of input data. Our primary finding is that under matched conditions, both QB-Net (with an $F_1$ score of 31.18) and QuFeX ($F_1 = 30.79$) outperformed the classical U-Net baseline results ($F_1 = 28.71$). Additionally, the classical FPN achieved a comparable score of 31.13. A crucial finding was that data mixing removed a significant domain shift between the geographically-separated train and test sets, which boosted the classical FPN $F_1$ score to 39.76. We validate the architecture's robustness and generalizability to the wildfire detection problem via cross-dataset transfer on the California Burned Areas (CaBuAr) dataset. Overall, we find that quantum machine learning has potential to provide an advantage in the problem of wildfire image segmentation, and further experiments will continue to validate and expand upon this finding.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes quantum-hybrid U-Net models (QB-Net and QuFeX) for wildfire detection from satellite imagery, achieving improved F1 scores over classical baselines, with data mixing significantly boosting performance by addressing domain shift.
AI.motivation: Wildfire detection from satellite imagery is difficult due to class imbalance, feature complexity, and atmospheric interference. The authors aim to leverage quantum machine learning to better model the high-dimensional spectral feature space.
AI.method: The authors inject variational quantum circuits (QuFeX and QB-Net ansatzes) into the bottleneck of U-Net, compare with classical FPN, and explore classical improvements like parameter compression, alternative loss functions, and uniform data mixing.
AI.result: QB-Net (F1=31.18) and QuFeX (F1=30.79) outperformed classical U-Net (F1=28.71). Classical FPN scored 31.13. Data mixing boosted FPN to 39.76 by removing domain shift. Cross-dataset validation on CaBuAr confirmed robustness.
AI.conclusion: Quantum machine learning shows potential for advantage in wildfire image segmentation, with further experiments needed to validate and expand the findings.

title: DialogueVPR: Towards Conversational Visual Place Recognition
summary: Inspired by how humans communicate spatial information, language-guided geo-localization has gained significant traction for its intuitive and practical value. Despite this progress, most methods still rely on a static, one-shot retrieval paradigm, which fails to handle the ambiguity and incompleteness inherent in real-world natural language descriptions. We propose a paradigm shift to reasoning retrieval and introduce Dialogue Place Recognition (DlgPR), which casts localization as an interactive, dialogue-driven reasoning process. To support this new task, we present DlgQuest-Cities, the first large-scale dialogue-based benchmark for place recognition, and a unified reasoning framework that couples a cross-modal multi-level retriever with an intelligent questioner, DQ-pilot. DQ-pilot is trained in a curriculum: supervised fine-tuning on a curated DQ-cities-20k subset followed by reinforcement refinement on a harder DQ-cities-10k split via GRPO. Two task-aligned metrics guide learning: a Discriminative Difficulty Index (DDI) for curriculum sampling and a Positional Retrieval Gain (PRG) reward that directly measures retrieval improvement induced by a question. Experiments show this reasoning-based approach significantly outperforms baselines. The code and model are available at https://github.com/Graysonggg/DlgPR.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a paradigm shift from static one-shot retrieval to interactive dialogue-driven reasoning for language-guided geo-localization. They introduce DlgPR (Dialogue Place Recognition), a new benchmark DlgQuest-Cities, and a unified framework with a cross-modal multi-level retriever and DQ-pilot questioner trained via curriculum learning with GRPO reinforcement refinement.
AI.motivation: Existing language-guided geo-localization methods rely on a static, one-shot retrieval paradigm that cannot handle the ambiguity and incompleteness of real-world natural language descriptions, unlike human communication which uses interactive dialogue.
AI.method: 1) Introduces DlgPR, a dialogue-driven localization paradigm. 2) Builds DlgQuest-Cities benchmark with curated subsets (DQ-cities-20k, DQ-cities-10k). 3) Proposes a unified reasoning framework: cross-modal multi-level retriever + DQ-pilot questioner. 4) Trains DQ-pilot via curriculum: SFT on 20k subset, then GRPO reinforcement refinement on harder 10k split. 5) Uses DDI for curriculum sampling and PRG reward to measure retrieval improvement from questions.
AI.result: The proposed reasoning-based approach significantly outperforms baselines on the dialogue-based place recognition task.
AI.conclusion: Dialogue-driven reasoning retrieval is more effective than static one-shot retrieval for real-world language-guided geo-localization, supported by new benchmarks and metrics (DDI, PRG).

title: Efficient Quantum Algorithm for Phase Optimization of 1-Bit RIS-Assisted MIMO Communication System
summary: We propose a Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm with a deterministic linear ramp schedule (QAOA-LR) for phase optimization of a 1-bit RIS-assisted MIMO communication system. Each RIS element is restricted to a binary phase shift of 0 or π, turning the passive beamforming design problem with N elements into a combinatorial optimization problem over 2^N configurations. Instead of running a classical optimizer, QAOA-LR uses a fixed linear ramp to set the variational parameters across p layers and finds the best scale via a simple one-dimensional grid search over a single parameter. Monte Carlo simulations over Rayleigh-fading MIMO channels confirm that QAOA-LR closely tracks the optimum maximum-likelihood (ML) solution. Furthermore, the proposed algorithm reduces the computational complexity compared with classical optimization, and real hardware experiments on the IBM Quantum processor confirm near-ML capacity performance with polynomial scaling of quantum processing unit execution time as the number of RIS elements increases.

AI.tldr: Proposes QAOA with a deterministic linear ramp schedule (QAOA-LR) for binary phase optimization in 1-bit RIS-assisted MIMO systems, reducing complexity while achieving near-ML performance, validated via simulations and IBM Quantum processor experiments.
AI.motivation: To efficiently solve the combinatorial optimization problem of passive beamforming in a 1-bit RIS-assisted MIMO system (2^N configurations), avoiding the high complexity of classical optimizers.
AI.method: Uses QAOA with a fixed linear ramp schedule for variational parameters across p layers; optimizes only a single scale parameter via 1D grid search.
AI.result: Monte Carlo simulations show QAOA-LR closely tracks the optimal ML solution; hardware experiments on IBM Quantum processor confirm near-ML capacity with polynomial scaling of execution time.
AI.conclusion: QAOA-LR is an efficient quantum algorithm for RIS phase optimization, offering near-optimal performance with reduced computational complexity and practical scalability on real quantum hardware.

title: SD-MAR: Multi-image Analytical Reasoning via Synthetic Data and Reinforcement Learning
summary: Vision Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate strong perceptual abilities but remain limited in tasks requiring analytical reasoning across multiple visual states, such as multi-image comparison, change detection, and multi-step visual inference. These capabilities are critical for real-world multimodal applications where reasoning must be grounded in systematic differences between visual contexts. However, existing benchmarks rarely require both explicit visual comparison and analytical reasoning, leaving this capability underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce SD-MAR (Synthetic Data for Multi-image Analytical Reasoning), a framework for training and evaluating VLMs on multi-image analytical reasoning. SD-MAR constructs paired visual scenarios through controlled perturbations and generates reasoning tasks spanning semantic change attribution and quantitative comparison. We further train VLMs using GRPO-lite with Backward Discounted Allocation (BDA), a reinforcement learning approach that removes KL regularization to encourage stronger policy optimization while allocating greater credit to the later reasoning steps where analytical conclusions are formed. Experiments on Qwen2.5-VL-7B and InternVL3-8B show that GRPO-lite fine-tuning on SD-MAR improves in-domain accuracy by up to 36.95%, with Qwen2.5-VL-7B outperforming GPT-4.1 on the SD-MAR benchmark. Importantly, out-of-domain generalization is preserved or improved: performance remains within 1% on MME, MMMU-Pro, and MathVista, while improving by up to 4% on MMBench. LLM-as-judge evaluation further demonstrates consistent improvements in logical coherence and explanation quality across both models.

AI.tldr: Introduces SD-MAR, a framework for multi-image analytical reasoning in VLMs, trained with GRPO-lite (BDA) reinforcement learning, achieving up to 36.95% accuracy gains and strong generalization.
AI.motivation: VLMs lack analytical reasoning across multiple visual states (multi-image comparison, change detection), and existing benchmarks don't evaluate this capability.
AI.method: SD-MAR generates paired visual scenarios via controlled perturbations with reasoning tasks. Uses GRPO-lite with Backward Discounted Allocation (BDA) — RL without KL regularization that credits later reasoning steps more.
AI.result: Up to 36.95% in-domain accuracy improvement on Qwen2.5-VL-7B and InternVL3-8B; Qwen2.5-VL-7B surpasses GPT-4.1 on SD-MAR; generalization within 1% on MME/MMMU-Pro/MathVista, +4% on MMBench; better logical coherence.
AI.conclusion: SD-MAR + GRPO-lite (BDA) effectively improves multi-image analytical reasoning in VLMs while preserving or enhancing out-of-domain generalization and explanation quality.

title: Identification Codes and Post-Shannon Communication: Theory, Architectures, and Emerging Applications
summary:
Identification (ID) coding, introduced by Ahlswede and Dueck, extends Shannon's classical communication paradigm by replacing message reconstruction with hypothesis testing. Instead of decoding the transmitted message, the receiver only decides whether a particular message was sent. A fundamental result of ID theory is the double-exponential growth in the number of identifiable messages with respect to (w.r.t.) the blocklength. This scaling behavior enables fundamentally new communication architectures for large-scale distributed systems and forms a key building block of post-Shannon communication.
  While ID cannot replace classical communication in general, it is particularly well-suited for scenarios in which full message reconstruction is unnecessary, such as monitoring, alarming, and control systems.
  In this survey, we review the theoretical foundations of ID coding and discuss emerging communication architectures and application domains based on this paradigm. Particular emphasis is placed on practical use cases, including monitoring systems, special-purpose data storage, joint identification and sensing (JIDAS), semantic communications, mobile-network control systems and networked consensus testing systems. We further highlight recent system concepts, industrial perspectives, and implementation examples that illustrate how ID-based principles can be realized in practical communication systems.

AI.tldr: This survey reviews Identification (ID) coding theory—a post-Shannon paradigm where receivers only detect whether a specific message was sent rather than decoding the full message—highlighting its double-exponential coding capacity and practical applications in monitoring, control, sensing, semantic communications, and networked systems.
AI.motivation: ID coding extends Shannon's paradigm by replacing full message reconstruction with hypothesis testing, which is particularly useful for large-scale distributed systems where full message decoding is unnecessary.
AI.method: The paper surveys theoretical foundations of ID coding, including the double-exponential growth in identifiable messages, and reviews emerging architectures and use cases.
AI.result: ID enables double-exponential growth of identifiable messages w.r.t. blocklength, making it highly scalable for large-scale systems. Practical domains include monitoring, data storage, JIDAS, semantic communications, mobile-network control, and consensus testing.
AI.conclusion: ID coding is a promising post-Shannon paradigm for scenarios where full message reconstruction is not needed, with growing practical implementations and industrial relevance.

title: Deep Scene-Driven Ordering of Hadamard Basis for Single-Pixel Spectral Imaging
summary: Spectral images are highly valuable for various applications, including environmental monitoring and precision agriculture. However, the high cost of specialized sensors limits the wide use of this technology in numerous applications. Current alternatives to acquire high spatial-spectral resolution spectral images, like Single-Pixel Imaging (SPI) enhanced with Deep Optical Coding Design (DOCD), have limitations due to their non-feedback optical designs, leading to limited image quality, with optimal performance achieved only for the specific scenes used during training. This work reformulates the DOCD framework to handle the scene-driven ordering of the Hadamard basis within the SPI architecture for spectral imaging. Taking into account that SPI usually acquires hundreds of snapshots, our approach introduces a scene-driven ordering of the Hadamard matrix for flexible SPI modulation pattern selection based on scene characteristics in an end-to-end optimization. Simulations on spectral datasets and real test-bed acquisitions demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in improving the quality of VIS and NIR spectral images compared to fixed designs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a scene-driven optimization method for ordering Hadamard basis patterns in Single-Pixel Imaging (SPI) for spectral imaging, improving spectral image quality compared to fixed designs.
AI.motivation: High cost of specialized sensors limits spectral imaging adoption; current Deep Optical Coding Design (DOCD) methods are non-feedback and achieve optimal performance only for training scenes, lacking adaptability.
AI.method: Reformulates DOCD framework for scene-driven Hadamard basis ordering within SPI architecture, enabling flexible modulation pattern selection based on scene characteristics via end-to-end optimization.
AI.result: Simulations on spectral datasets and real test-bed acquisitions show improved VIS and NIR spectral image quality compared to fixed designs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scene-driven approach effectively enhances spectral imaging quality in SPI systems, overcoming limitations of non-feedback optical designs.

title: Stochastic Filtering for Quorum Sensing in Robot Swarms under Anonymous Communication
summary: Quorum Sensing (QS) is a key capability for robot swarms, useful for coordination of activities at the group level. Effective communication is instrumental for individuals to estimate the quorum level of the entire swarm. Anonymous communication protocols where individuals exchange local information without revealing unique identities are helpful to support quorum estimates by sampling information from neighbours and maintain scalability of the QS process. However, because anonymous protocols cannot distinguish message sources, repeated messages from the same sender may be double-counted, thereby biasing collective quorum estimates. In this study, we introduce a stochastic filtering protocol inspired by $k$-priority sampling to improve estimate stability (\ANTk), and we compare it with a baseline anonymous protocols (\AN) and a randomised variant designed to improve accuracy (\ANT). We find that the baseline protocol \AN provides a parsimonious and fast solution, but remains highly inaccurate due to double-counting bias. The \ANT variant improves accuracy but suffers from information inertia, resulting in slower convergence. Finally, actively filtering the message buffer via the \ANTk protocol successfully decreases temporary errors and stabilises the estimate, at the cost of an increased time of recovery from errors.

AI.tldr: This paper studies quorum sensing protocols for robot swarms, introducing a stochastic filtering protocol (ANTk) inspired by k-priority sampling to improve estimate stability over baseline anonymous (AN) and randomized (ANT) protocols, finding trade-offs between accuracy, speed, and error recovery.
AI.motivation: To improve quorum estimation in robot swarms using anonymous communication protocols, where double-counting of repeated messages from the same sender biases collective estimates.
AI.method: Introduces a stochastic filtering protocol (ANTk) based on k-priority sampling, compared against a baseline anonymous protocol (AN) and a randomized variant (ANT) in robot swarm simulations.
AI.result: AN is fast but inaccurate due to double-counting; ANT improves accuracy but converges slowly due to information inertia; ANTk stabilizes estimates and reduces temporary errors at the cost of slower error recovery.
AI.conclusion: There is a fundamental trade-off between accuracy, speed, and error recovery in anonymous quorum sensing protocols, with ANTk offering the best stability among the compared approaches.

title: Branching Policy Optimization: Sandbox-Native Language Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning has emerged as the dominant paradigm for training large language model (LLM) agents that interact with executable sandboxes. State-of-the-art algorithms such as PPO, RLOO, and GRPO inherit their rollout topology from RLHF: for each prompt, N independent trajectories are sampled from the initial state, and an advantage is computed by subtracting a group baseline. This design ignores a defining property of agent sandboxes. They are deterministic, snapshottable, and resumable from any intermediate state. We argue that this property enables a fundamentally different rollout topology: rather than N independent trees of depth T, one can construct a single tree of N leaves whose siblings share prefixes, and therefore share variance. We instantiate this idea as Branching Policy Optimization (BPO), a sandbox-native RL algorithm that (i) adaptively snapshots the sandbox at high-entropy decision points along a backbone trajectory, (ii) forks K alternative actions per branch point and rolls out each to termination, and (iii) computes per-step advantages from sibling returns rather than from independent prompts. We prove this estimator is unbiased and has strictly lower variance than the trajectory-level baseline, with the reduction equal to the prefix-explained portion of return variance. On WebShop, ALFWorld, and SWE-bench Verified with Qwen2.5-7B and Llama-3.1-8B backbones, BPO improves success by 3.6--6.1 absolute points over GRPO and RLOO at matched compute, halves gradient-norm variance, and matches the best baseline using 38% fewer policy updates.

AI.tldr: BPO leverages deterministic, snapshottable sandbox environments to replace N independent trajectories with a single branching tree, reducing variance and improving sample efficiency in RL for LLM agents.
AI.motivation: Existing RL algorithms (PPO, RLOO, GRPO) for LLM agents use independent trajectory sampling that ignores the deterministic, snapshottable, and resumable nature of executable sandboxes, leading to suboptimal variance and efficiency.
AI.method: BPO adaptively snapshots sandboxes at high-entropy decision points along a backbone trajectory, forks K alternative actions per branch point, rolls each to termination, and computes per-step advantages from sibling returns (shared prefixes) rather than independent baselines.
AI.result: On WebShop, ALFWorld, and SWE-bench Verified with Qwen2.5-7B and Llama-3.1-8B: 3.6-6.1 absolute point improvement over GRPO/RLOO at matched compute, 50% reduction in gradient-norm variance, and matches best baseline with 38% fewer policy updates.
AI.conclusion: BPO demonstrates that rethinking rollout topology to exploit sandbox properties yields unbiased, lower-variance advantage estimation and significant practical gains in LLM agent training efficiency.

title: Interpretable Language Model for Closed-Loop Type 1 Diabetes Control
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Automation Science and Engineering conference (IEEE CASE 2026)
summary: Type 1 Diabetes (T1D) is a chronic, life-threatening autoimmune condition characterized by the complete destruction of insulin-producing pancreatic beta cells. While Artificial Pancreas Systems (APS) powered by Reinforcement Learning (RL) have shown promise in automating insulin delivery, their ``black-box'' nature makes it hard for patients and doctors to trust them fully. This paper presents LLM-T1D, a promising approach that combines the precision of RL with the clear, human-like reasoning of Large Language Models (LLMs) to create a more transparent and reliable insulin pump controller. By training an expert RL system and distilling its knowledge into fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1 8B and Qwen3 8B models, we developed a controller that not only surpasses the RL system's performance but also explains its decisions in plain, understandable language. Tested on the FDA-approved UVA/Padova T1D simulator, the LLM controllers deliver excellent blood sugar control (73.5% Time in Range) while maintaining strict formal safety verification against hallucinations.

AI.tldr: LLM-T1D combines Reinforcement Learning with fine-tuned LLMs (LLaMA 3.1 8B, Qwen3 8B) to create a transparent, high-performing insulin pump controller for Type 1 Diabetes, achieving 73.5% Time in Range on the FDA-approved UVA/Padova simulator with formal safety guarantees.
AI.motivation: RL-based Artificial Pancreas Systems lack transparency, making it hard for patients and doctors to trust them fully. The paper aims to create a controller that is both high-performing and interpretable.
AI.method: Train an expert RL system, then distill its knowledge into fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1 8B and Qwen3 8B LLMs, enabling both control and natural language explanations of decisions.
AI.result: LLM controllers surpass the RL system's performance, achieving 73.5% Time in Range blood glucose control with strict formal safety verification against hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: LLM-T1D demonstrates that combining RL precision with LLM reasoning can yield a transparent, trustworthy, and safe insulin pump controller for T1D management.

title: Effect of Antenna Deployment on Achievable Rate in Cooperative Magnetic Induction Communication
comment: This work was published in IEEE Communications Letters, with the DOI: 10.1109/LCOMM.2019.2929790
summary: Magnetic Induction (MI) communication can be applied in some through-the-earth scenarios such as mines and underground rivers. To increase the transmission rate of MI communications, we propose a cooperative MI (CMI) scheme with an amplify-and-forward (AF) relay. Different from existing studies, we mainly focus on the relay with arbitrary antenna position and orientation (antenna deployment, AD). We derive the closed-form expression of CMI achievable data rate gain (CMG) for the relay and the closed-form expression of CMI channel bandwidth. Simulations reveal that a relay with appropriate AD could yield a significant increase in the achievable rate for MI systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a cooperative magnetic induction (CMI) scheme with an amplify-and-forward relay for through-the-earth communication, focusing on arbitrary antenna deployment, and derives closed-form expressions for achievable rate gain and channel bandwidth.
AI.motivation: To increase the transmission rate of MI communications in through-the-earth scenarios such as mines and underground rivers.
AI.method: Proposes a cooperative MI (CMI) scheme with an amplify-and-forward (AF) relay, focusing on arbitrary antenna position and orientation (antenna deployment, AD). Derives closed-form expressions for CMI achievable data rate gain (CMG) and CMI channel bandwidth.
AI.result: A relay with appropriate antenna deployment (AD) can yield a significant increase in the achievable rate for MI systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CMI scheme with proper antenna deployment can effectively boost transmission rates in through-the-earth MI communication systems.

title: MixCompress: Mixture of Experts for Variable Rate Learned Image Compression
summary: Learned image compression (LIC) is bottlenecked by the need to store independent models for each rate-distortion operating point. Existing variable bit-rate (VBR) methods aim to reduce this overhead via dense parameter modulation, but forcing a shared backbone to approximate divergent mappings causes severe feature entanglement. Specifically, low-rate smoothing gradients inherently conflict with the preservation of high-frequency textural details, leading to sub-optimal performance. To resolve this, we propose MixCompress, a unified VBR framework based on sparse structural specialization. While sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) routing successfully mitigates gradient conflict, it operates on a fixed computational budget. To address the increased representational demands of higher bit-rates we introduce a Mixture-of-Depths (MoD) extension to dynamically scale model capacity. Combined with Conditional Auxiliary Transforms (CAT) for dynamic sub-band energy modulation, our hierarchical framework effectively dynamically scales capacity. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that MixCompress not only matches individually optimized single-rate baselines but can even surpass them, establishing a new Pareto frontier for computationally efficient image coding.

AI.tldr: MixCompress introduces a unified variable bit-rate learned image compression framework using sparse Mixture-of-Experts and Mixture-of-Depths to dynamically scale model capacity and mitigate feature entanglement, outperforming single-rate baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing VBR methods suffer from severe feature entanglement because low-rate smoothing gradients conflict with high-frequency texture preservation when a shared backbone is forced to approximate divergent mappings.
AI.method: MixCompress combines sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) to resolve gradient conflict, Mixture-of-Depths (MoD) to dynamically scale model capacity for higher bit-rates, and Conditional Auxiliary Transforms (CAT) for dynamic sub-band energy modulation.
AI.result: MixCompress matches or surpasses individually optimized single-rate baselines, establishing a new Pareto frontier for computationally efficient image coding.
AI.conclusion: Sparse structural specialization with dynamic capacity scaling effectively resolves feature entanglement in variable bit-rate learned image compression, achieving superior rate-distortion performance.

title: Statistics of the Compression Ratio of a Variable-to-Variable Code: Exact Moments and Asymptotic Behavior
summary: A variable-to-variable (V2V) length code parses a source sequence into phrases of variable length and maps each phrase to a binary codeword of, generally, a different random length. After encoding $n$ phrases, the realized compression ratio $R_n=Λ_n/Σ_n$ -- total codeword length over total source-symbol count -- is the finite-sample counterpart of the code's asymptotic rate $ρ$, to which it converges only as $n\to\infty$. This paper first derives exact formulas for all integer moments of $R_n$ for a given discrete memoryless source (DMS). Specifically, we obtain a closed-form formula for every moment $\E\{R_n^k\}$ as a one-dimensional integral involving only single-phrase moment generating functions of the pair $(L,\ell)$ -- the phrase length, in source symbols, and codeword length, in bits. From these moments we derive an Edgeworth approximation to the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of $R_n$ that is substantially more accurate than the central limit theorem (CLT) approximation. Using the Laplace method of integration, we also derive explicit closed-form formulas for the bias constant $C=\lim_{n\to\infty}n(\E\{R_n\}-ρ)$ and for the variance constant $\lim_{n\to\infty}n\cdot\Var\{R_n\}$. The analysis extends to Markov sources via state-indexed matrices with a redundancy formula obtained in closed form. On the coding-theoretic side, we cast V2V length codes as finite-state encoders and apply a generalized Kraft inequality for a compression-rate lower bound, and give a structural decomposition of the bias coefficient that separates cleanly across variable-to-fixed (V2F) length codes, fixed-to-variable (F2V) length codes, and V2V length codes. Applied to the Khodak code of Bugeaud, Drmota, and Szpankowski, this decomposition shows that its improved performance is reflected in its smaller bias constant.

AI.tldr: This paper derives exact formulas for all integer moments of the finite-sample compression ratio of variable-to-variable (V2V) length codes, uses them to develop accurate Edgeworth approximations, obtains closed-form bias and variance constants via Laplace integration, extends analysis to Markov sources, and provides a coding-theoretic decomposition of bias that explains the improved performance of the Khodak code.
AI.motivation: To understand the finite-sample behavior of V2V length codes beyond asymptotic limits, providing exact moment formulas and more accurate distributional approximations than the CLT, as well as insights into code performance through bias decomposition.
AI.method: Derives closed-form moment formulas via one-dimensional integrals of single-phrase MGFs; uses Edgeworth expansion for CDF approximation; applies Laplace method for asymptotic constants; extends to Markov sources with state-indexed matrices; uses generalized Kraft inequality for lower bounds and structural bias decomposition.
AI.result: Exact formulas for all integer moments of R_n; Edgeworth approximation outperforms CLT; closed-form bias constant C and variance constant; bias decomposition across V2F, F2V, and V2V codes; Khodak code's smaller bias constant explains its superior performance.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a comprehensive finite-sample analysis framework for V2V codes, with exact moments, improved approximations, and a coding-theoretic decomposition that reveals why the Khodak code outperforms other codes.

title: ESAR: Event-Based Synthetic Aperture Reconstruction
summary:
Event cameras report asynchronous polarity events when changes in log--radiance exceed a fixed contrast threshold, producing signed temporal contrast measurements rather than conventional image frames. We formulate monocular event-based imaging as a synthetic-aperture inverse problem for a static ground-domain log--radiance field $θ\in \mathbb{R}^{N_g}$. Instead of reconstructing a latent pixel-time volume $v \in \mathbb{R}^{N_pN_t}$, we impose the geometric relation $v=Pθ$, where $P$ maps the fixed scene into motion-dependent latent views. Aggregating events over finite time intervals gives the linearized model \[
  APθ= b+η, \] where $A$ is a temporal differencing operator, $b$ contains signed binned event counts, and $η$ represents measurement and modeling errors. This decomposition exposes a synthetic-aperture structure: under near-nadir motion, successive projections are approximately shifted views of a common scene, while the composite operator $AP$ remains ill-conditioned because it combines spatial averaging with temporal differencing. We therefore use regularized inversion to recover $θ$. Numerical experiments on simulated data and real near-nadir Falcon Neuro event data show that the proposed $θ$-based formulation recovers coherent large-scale spatial structure, relative to dynamic latent-image and learned event-reconstruction baselines, while suppressing fine-scale texture.

AI.tldr: The paper formulates monocular event-based imaging as a synthetic-aperture inverse problem, recovering a static log-radiance field from event camera data via regularized inversion, showing improved large-scale spatial structure recovery on simulated and real Falcon Neuro data.
AI.motivation: Event cameras output asynchronous polarity events based on log-radiance changes, but reconstructing images from these sparse, asynchronous measurements is challenging. Existing methods often reconstruct a latent pixel-time volume, which is computationally heavy and ignores the underlying scene geometry.
AI.method: The authors impose a geometric relation v=Pθ to link the static scene θ to motion-dependent latent views, aggregate events into a linearized model APθ = b+η, and use regularized inversion to recover θ, exploiting the synthetic-aperture structure under near-nadir motion.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on simulated data and real Falcon Neuro event data show the proposed θ-based formulation recovers coherent large-scale spatial structure better than dynamic latent-image and learned event-reconstruction baselines, though fine-scale texture is suppressed.
AI.conclusion: The synthetic-aperture inverse problem formulation for event cameras is effective for recovering large-scale scene structure, with regularized inversion providing robustness despite ill-conditioning from spatial averaging and temporal differencing.

title: DiMaS: Distribution Matching for Steering Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Flow-matching-based vision-language-action (VLA) models have emerged as powerful policies for robotic manipulation, yet a critical capability remains underexplored: fine-grained behavioral control, the ability to govern how a robot performs a task by intervening on its internal representations. Representation steering is a well-established interpretability tool for language and vision-language models, where behavioral features are typically encoded as linear directions, but we show that these classic methods fall short in VLAs. We propose DiMaS, a Distribution-Matching Steering strategy tailored to flow-matching VLAs, which transports between representation distributions rather than shifting along a fixed direction, and show that it effectively controls behavior across two state-of-the-art VLAs. We further examine the generalizability of this strategy as the tasks it is learned from and evaluated on grow increasingly dissimilar, characterizing where behavioral control transfers and where it weakens. Finally, through an analysis of the representation structure of the action expert, we explain why classical linear steering falls short in the visuomotor setting: behavioral features are linearly decodable but not linearly steerable, which motivates the distribution-matching design of DiMaS. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/pegah-kh/dimas, with additional results and videos at https://pegah-kh.github.io/dimas/

AI.tldr: DiMaS is a distribution-matching steering method for flow-matching Vision-Language-Action models that enables fine-grained behavioral control by transporting between representation distributions rather than using linear steering, which fails in visuomotor settings because features are linearly decodable but not linearly steerable.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained behavioral control in VLA models is underexplored, and classical linear steering methods (effective in language/vision-language models) fall short when applied to flow-matching VLAs for robotic manipulation.
AI.method: DiMaS (Distribution-Matching Steering) - transports between representation distributions instead of shifting along a fixed linear direction, tailored specifically to flow-matching VLAs.
AI.result: DiMaS effectively controls behavior across two state-of-the-art VLAs; the paper characterizes where behavioral control transfers and weakens as tasks grow dissimilar; demonstrates that behavioral features are linearly decodable but not linearly steerable.
AI.conclusion: Classical linear steering fails in visuomotor settings due to representation structure of the action expert, motivating the distribution-matching approach of DiMaS. Code and videos are publicly available.

title: How Much of a 10-K Matters? Aggregation-Dependent Value of Full-Text versus Risk-Factor Sentiment
summary: Financial sentiment extraction has largely relied on news text and supervised extraction against return labels alone, leaving 10-K filings -- and volatility, the target risk disclosure is arguably best suited to informing -- comparatively unexplored. We extend a supervised lexicon-learning approach to 10-K filings and their Item 1A risk-factor sections, training sentiment scores against both return and volatility labels at three levels of aggregation: sector, portfolio, and individual firm. Across 1,383 filings from 94 Nasdaq-100 technology constituents (2006--2023), we evaluate the resulting twelve sentiment metrics on classification accuracy, correlation with realised market outcomes, and qualitative lexical content. Full-filing text produces more accurate sentiment at the sector and portfolio level for both targets, but this reverses at the individual-firm level, where the narrower Item 1A section performs better -- an effect we attribute to the interaction between document volume and the amount of independent training signal available at each level of aggregation. A Loughran-McDonald dictionary baseline is consistently, strongly negatively correlated with price at every level tested, underscoring the value of a supervised approach for regulatory disclosure text. These findings, and the design choices they motivate, establish the sentiment-generation methodology underlying a subsequent, larger-scale, multi-source system.

AI.tldr: This paper extends supervised lexicon-learning to 10-K filings, training sentiment scores against return and volatility labels at sector, portfolio, and individual-firm levels. Full-filing text works better at higher aggregation levels, but Item 1A risk sections outperform at the individual-firm level. A Loughran-McDonald dictionary baseline shows strong negative correlation with price, validating the supervised approach.
AI.motivation: Financial sentiment extraction has largely focused on news text and return labels, leaving 10-K filings and volatility as a target for risk disclosure unexplored.
AI.method: Extended a supervised lexicon-learning approach to 10-K filings (full text and Item 1A sections), training sentiment scores against both return and volatility labels at three aggregation levels (sector, portfolio, individual firm).
AI.result: Full-filing text is more accurate at sector/portfolio levels; Item 1A is better at individual-firm level. The Loughran-McDonald baseline is strongly negatively correlated with price at all levels.
AI.conclusion: Design choices for sentiment generation from regulatory text depend on aggregation level and target label, establishing methodology for a larger multi-source system.

title: Human AI Construction of Bayesian Networks for Operational Decision Support -- A Virtual Survey Approach
summary: Bayesian Belief Networks (BBNs) are powerful tools for decision-making under uncertainty. However, building their structures and estimating parameters are difficult. Currently, researchers must choose between relying on expert judgement or using large datasets to learn the structure and parameters of the network. We propose a new methodology using Large Language Models to bridge the gap between expert opinion and data-driven learning. This approach uses a panel of AI agents to estimate probabilities based on specific personas and context. We then apply a trimmed-mean rule to remove noise from these responses. We develop a six step BBN framework and illustrate it to model customer intention to consult a doctor in an alternative healthcare system. The model reveals that while self efficacy appears to be a major factor, its actual causal impact is small. In contrast, subjective norms have a much stronger effect in modelling customers' intention. The most effective strategy is to improve both confidence and community norms simultaneously.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new methodology that uses Large Language Models (LLMs) as a panel of AI agents to estimate probabilities for Bayesian Belief Networks, bridging the gap between expert opinion and data-driven learning. A six-step framework is developed and applied to model customer intention in an alternative healthcare system.
AI.motivation: Building Bayesian Belief Networks (BBNs) requires either expert judgment or large datasets, both of which have limitations. The authors aim to bridge this gap by proposing a methodology that leverages LLMs to provide probabilistic estimates without relying solely on human experts or extensive data.
AI.method: The method uses a panel of AI agents (LLMs) with specific personas and context to estimate probabilities. A trimmed-mean rule is applied to remove noise from responses. This is formalized into a six-step BBN framework.
AI.result: Applied to customer intention in alternative healthcare, the model shows that self-efficacy has a small actual causal impact despite appearing as a major factor. Subjective norms have a much stronger effect. The most effective strategy combines improving confidence and community norms simultaneously.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LLM-based approach effectively substitutes for expert panels in BBN construction, revealing non-obvious causal insights (e.g., self-efficacy vs. subjective norms) and identifying combined strategies as most effective.

title: Quantifying the complexity of trajectory ensembles with clustering-weighted multivariate multiscale sample entropy
summary: Across the physical and life sciences, data increasingly appear as ensembles of trajectories, from chaotic flows and satellite constellations to clinical cohorts. Established sample-entropy measures characterize individual time series, while averaging across an ensemble discards population structure and cannot distinguish redundancy from diversity. We introduce clustering-weighted multivariate multiscale sample entropy (CWMMSE), which groups trajectories into behavioral patterns and weights each by its dynamical complexity. CWMMSE is a weighted entropy of the population's pattern distribution. Its empirical plug-in estimator is strongly consistent for a fixed finite partition, and it separates two components that can diverge in real data: individual complexity and population diversity. Both are essential. Averaging ignores diversity, whereas spread alone can mistake a varied but predictable population for a complex one. Across eleven physical, environmental, engineering, and biomedical systems, CWMMSE ranks a calm ocean region above an energetic but individually more complex one, identifies a major earthquake as a collapse in system complexity, and reverses the conclusion from averaging in cardiac cohorts, where disease reduces population diversity. Supported by an open, reproducible implementation, these results show that population complexity should be measured rather than averaged.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CWMMSE, a method that measures population complexity by clustering trajectories into patterns and weighting them by dynamical complexity, revealing that population diversity and individual complexity are distinct and both essential—demonstrated across 11 real-world systems where averaging alone misleads.
AI.motivation: Existing sample-entropy measures only characterize individual time series, and averaging across an ensemble discards population structure, failing to distinguish redundancy from diversity.
AI.method: Clustering-weighted multivariate multiscale sample entropy (CWMMSE) groups trajectories into behavioral patterns and weights each by its dynamical complexity, computing a weighted entropy of the population's pattern distribution.
AI.result: Across eleven systems, CWMMSE ranks a calm ocean region above an energetic one, identifies an earthquake as a collapse in system complexity, and reverses the conclusion from averaging in cardiac cohorts where disease reduces population diversity.
AI.conclusion: Population complexity should be measured rather than averaged, as individual complexity and population diversity are both essential and can diverge in real data.

title: Beyond scalar losses: calibrating segmentation models via gradient vector field surgery
summary: Region-based loss functions, such as the Dice loss, have established themselves as the de facto standard for highly class- and region-imbalanced segmentation tasks. However, models trained using region-based loss functions are notoriously miscalibrated and typically yield over-confident predictions. In medical imaging applications, such as defining tumor resection margins, this miscalibration is hindering clinical adoption. In this work, we outline a novel gradient perspective on this overconfidence and show how it affects region-based loss functions. We propose a "surgery" on the gradient vector field as a simple, yet effective intervention to mitigate calibration issues. This surgery adds a factor to the loss's partial derivative, scaling the gradient's magnitude linearly with the prediction error. In empirical evaluations across 2D and 3D medical segmentation tasks, we demonstrate the effectiveness of this intervention while maintaining high prediction accuracy when used in conjunction with any region-based loss function.

AI.tldr: Region-based loss functions like Dice loss cause overconfident predictions in medical segmentation. The authors propose a gradient "surgery" that scales gradients linearly with prediction error to fix miscalibration without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Region-based loss functions (e.g., Dice loss) cause miscalibrated, overconfident predictions, hindering clinical adoption in medical imaging (e.g., tumor resection margin definition).
AI.method: A gradient "surgery" that adds a factor to the partial derivative, scaling gradient magnitude linearly with prediction error, applied to any region-based loss function.
AI.result: Effective calibration improvement across 2D and 3D medical segmentation tasks while maintaining high prediction accuracy with any region-based loss function.
AI.conclusion: The proposed gradient surgery is a simple yet effective intervention to mitigate overconfidence in region-based loss functions for medical segmentation, improving calibration without compromising accuracy.

title: Random Spherical Codes at High SNR: Error Transitions, Fixed-Error Data Rates, and Converse Gaps
summary: This paper characterizes random spherical codebooks over the real additive white Gaussian noise channel in the high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regime when the blocklength is fixed and the codebook size grows with SNR. In this regime, the random spherical ensemble exhibits a sharp error-probability transition governed by the intrinsic dimension of the sphere and the codebook-growth scale. Below the critical codebook-growth scale, the ensemble-average error probability vanishes; at the critical scale, it converges to a nontrivial limit; and above that scale, it approaches one. By inverting this transition law, we obtain the high-SNR expansion of the ensemble-achievable data rate for a prescribed error probability. This rate has the same leading high-SNR growth as the corresponding converse rate bound, while reliability enters through the constant-order terms. Consequently, the ratio of the ensemble-achievable rate to the converse rate bound tends to one as the SNR increases. Their additive difference, however, generally approaches a positive blocklength- and reliability-dependent limit. We characterize this limiting rate-bound gap jointly as a function of blocklength and error probability. For every fixed error probability, the gap vanishes as the blocklength increases. We further identify the reliability scalings under which a decreasing error probability prevents the additive gap from vanishing in the large-blocklength limit.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes random spherical codebooks in the high-SNR regime with fixed blocklength and growing codebook size, revealing a sharp error-probability transition governed by sphere dimension and codebook-growth scale. It derives the high-SNR expansion of achievable rate and characterizes the rate gap relative to the converse bound as a function of blocklength and error probability.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental limits of random spherical coding in the high-SNR regime where blocklength is fixed but codebook size grows with SNR, and to characterize the relationship between achievable rates and converse bounds.
AI.method: The paper analyzes the random spherical ensemble over the real AWGN channel, studying its error-probability transition behavior as a function of codebook-growth scale at fixed blocklength. It inverts this transition law to derive high-SNR expansions and examines the asymptotic rate gap.
AI.result: The ensemble exhibits a sharp error-probability transition with a critical codebook-growth scale. The achievable rate has the same leading high-SNR growth as the converse bound, but with a positive additive gap that depends on blocklength and reliability. The gap vanishes as blocklength increases for fixed error probability, but can persist under certain reliability scalings.
AI.conclusion: The additive rate gap between ensemble-achievable rate and converse bound approaches a positive blocklength- and reliability-dependent limit, and its behavior depends on the interplay between blocklength scaling and error probability requirements.

title: Beyond Visual Grasping: Benchmarking Complex Grasping from Detection to Execution
summary: Robust robotic grasping remains a fundamental challenge for complex real-world applications. Recent advances in large-scale models demonstrate promising capabilities for reasoning in robotic tasks. However, existing benchmarks for grasping primarily focus on isolated, visual-based grasp pose detection, failing to capture the complexity of grasping tasks that require multi-step reasoning and semantic understanding during execution. To address this gap, we propose GCA-Bench, a benchmark featuring challenging \textit{grasping with complex action} scenarios that involve both scene-level reasoning and semantic constraints. GCA-Bench enables the evaluation of recent large foundation models under the same settings. To demonstrate the effectiveness of our new benchmark, we implement a diverse set of baselines, ranging from traditional grasp detection pipelines to end-to-end learning methods. Empirical studies achieve success rates below 70\% on complex grasping scenarios, underscoring critical limitations. In addition, we propose new evaluation metrics, analyze critical failure models, and provide insights to guide the development of more robust and generalizable grasping strategies.

AI.tldr: GCA-Bench is a new benchmark for evaluating robotic grasping performance in complex scenarios requiring multi-step reasoning and semantic understanding, where current methods achieve below 70% success rates.
AI.motivation: Existing grasping benchmarks focus on isolated visual-based grasp pose detection and fail to capture complexity requiring multi-step reasoning and semantic understanding during execution.
AI.method: Proposes GCA-Bench, a benchmark with "grasping with complex action" scenarios involving scene-level reasoning and semantic constraints, enabling evaluation of large foundation models under same settings.
AI.result: Empirical studies show success rates below 70% on complex grasping scenarios across diverse baselines.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark reveals critical limitations in current methods; new evaluation metrics, failure mode analysis, and insights are provided to guide more robust and generalizable grasping strategies.

title: Low-Latency Relay Selection in NR-V2X Vehicular Communications via Graph Isomorphism Networks with Edge Features
summary: Reliable, low-latency uplink connectivity is a key requirement for C-V2X networks in dense urban environments, where fast channel variations and blockages often degrade direct vehicle-to-infrastructure links. Multi-hop relaying can restore coverage, but relay-link activation under radio, capacity, and routing constraints results in an NP-hard optimisation problem, typically solved via Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP), whose runtime scales poorly with graph size. This paper introduces an edge-aware Learning-to-Optimise framework for real-time relay selection. Each V2X snapshot is modelled as a directed graph: node features encode vehicle state and traffic demand, while edge features capture radio-link capacity. An offline MILP oracle generates optimal relay configurations that supervise a Graph Isomorphism Network with Edge Features (GINE), enabling edge-level relay activation through a single forward pass, with tightly bounded inference latency. To bridge learning and exact optimisation, we also propose a hybrid GINE-Pruned MILP (GP-MILP) strategy in which GINE predictions prune the MILP search space. Experiments on a large-scale dataset generated via an OSM-SUMO-GEMV$^2$ pipeline show that GINE closely matches MILP decisions at the link level (accuracy 0.9589), F1-score (0.9544) on validation) and yields consistent end-to-end connectivity gains over a 1-hop MILP baseline (up to 9.2% with four RSUs and 12% with two RSUs). Inference latency remains tightly bounded, with all evaluated instances completing within 5~ms. Moreover, GP-MILP preserves MILP-equivalent solutions (same objective value) while achieving solver runtimes below 30~ms for more than 98%) of the graph instances, making MILP-grade optimisation compatible with stringent NR-V2X latency budgets.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a GINE-based Learning-to-Optimise framework for real-time relay selection in C-V2X networks, achieving near-MILP accuracy (0.9589) with inference latency under 5ms, and a hybrid GP-MILP strategy that preserves MILP-equivalent solutions while reducing solver runtime below 30ms for 98% of instances.
AI.motivation: Multi-hop relaying can restore coverage in dense urban C-V2X networks, but relay-link activation under constraints is NP-hard (solved via MILP with poor scalability). Real-time relay selection is needed for stringent NR-V2X latency budgets.
AI.method: Models each V2X snapshot as a directed graph with node/edge features; uses offline MILP oracle to supervise training of a Graph Isomorphism Network with Edge Features (GINE) for edge-level relay activation. Also proposes hybrid GP-MILP where GINE predictions prune MILP search space.
AI.result: GINE achieves link-level accuracy 0.9589, F1-score 0.9544, consistent end-to-end connectivity gains (up to 9.2%/12%), inference latency under 5ms. GP-MILP preserves MILP-equivalent solutions with runtime below 30ms for 98%+ instances.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework makes MILP-grade optimisation compatible with stringent NR-V2X latency budgets, enabling practical real-time relay selection in dense urban environments.

title: Capability from Access Structure, Not Scale: Lower Bounds and Pre-Registered Tests for Hybrid Sequence Models
comment: 41 pages, 16 figures. Pre-registered small-scale experiments (scorecard: 11 supported, 7 partial, 1 failed). Information-theoretic lower bounds plus controlled trained witnesses. Jianlin Chen and Ziyao Lin contributed equally. Corresponding author: Chi Man Vong (cmvong@um.edu.mo)
summary: The Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH) holds that as models scale, representations of heterogeneous networks converge toward a shared model of reality. We propose its sequel and boundary, the Capability Convergence Hypothesis (CCH): under a fixed per-token inference budget, representational convergence does not entail capability convergence. Capability instead converges toward a class, the access-complete hybrid: any architecture holding both a compressive O(1)-state channel and a scalable verbatim-index channel. We anchor it on a witness task, the Newton's-apple problem in an infinite stream, and name three resource walls: a Shannon wall barring any o(Nb)-state architecture, a horizon wall barring any fixed window, and a circuit wall barring fixed-depth attention-only composition (conditional on TC0 != NC1). Under an explicit separability assumption a hybrid crosses all three by paying each wall's price, so capability is strictly super-additive under composition. We separate what we prove from what we conjecture: the access-completeness principle rests on information-theoretic lower bounds and pre-registered experiments, while the field-level convergence trend is an economics-motivated conjecture. We report the first pre-registered small-scale tests under criteria frozen before the data: the predicted scissors gap is measured (exact-retrieval error 0.994 vs. 0.000 once a 64-scalar state gains one global-attention layer), the state-tracking bifurcation lands at the registered boundary, and a conjunction witness shows an irreducibly two-channel solution; one prediction failed with its direction reversed and is reported as such. Representational convergence is given freely by scale; capability convergence must be purchased by access structure.

AI.tldr: The Capability Convergence Hypothesis (CCH) argues that as AI models scale, their representations converge (per PRH) but their capabilities do not. Capability converges only to a "hybrid" architecture combining a compressive O(1)-state channel with a scalable verbatim-index channel. Three "resource walls" (Shannon, horizon, circuit) prevent pure architectures from solving the Newton's-apple problem. Pre-registered experiments confirm predicted gaps, with one failed prediction reported transparently.
AI.motivation: To challenge the Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH) by showing that representational convergence does not imply capability convergence, and to identify the architectural conditions necessary for capability convergence under fixed inference budgets.
AI.method: Formalizes the Capability Convergence Hypothesis (CCH) with three resource walls (Shannon, horizon, circuit) grounded in information theory and computational complexity (TC0 ≠ NC1). Introduces a "hybrid" architecture (compressive state + verbatim index) as the necessary condition. Tests via pre-registered small-scale experiments on a Newton's-apple witness task.
AI.result: Pre-registered experiments show: (1) a scissors gap (exact-retrieval error 0.994 vs. 0.000 with one global-attention layer), (2) state-tracking bifurcation at the registered boundary, (3) an irreducibly two-channel solution. One prediction failed with reversed direction and is reported as such.
AI.conclusion: Representational convergence is a free byproduct of scale, but capability convergence requires specific access structure (hybrid architecture). Capability is strictly super-additive under composition across the three walls.

title: Elliptic Range-Doppler Mapping for OFDM-ISAC under IQ Imbalance
comment: Submitted to IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
summary: Receiver in-phase/quadrature imbalance (IQI) couples each OFDM subcarrier with its mirror counterpart, creating ghost targets and degrading range-Doppler recovery in orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) integrated sensing and communication (ISAC). Instead of first compensating for IQI and then applying conventional processing, this letter exploits the structure of the IQI-impaired observation directly. We show that each physical target induces coupled direct and mirror components linked through the target coefficient and its conjugate, which motivates an elliptic atom group representation for each candidate delay-Doppler cell. Based on this model, we propose an elliptic group orthogonal matching pursuit detector that performs sparse recovery directly on the received OFDM grid. The required correlations are computed efficiently through two weighted two-dimensional fast Fourier transforms (FFTs) followed by local group projections. Numerical results show that the proposed method improves exact support recovery and weak-target detection compared to corresponding benchmarks, especially under moderate and strong receiver IQI.
