title: SportD: Can VLMs Physically Strategize?
summary: Vision--language models have become increasingly capable of interpreting visual scenes, but it remains unclear whether they can use information to make strategically effective decisions. We investigate this question in soccer, where models observe the seconds preceding an on-ball decision and must choose whether to shoot or pass to a specific teammate. Unlike conventional visual-understanding tasks, soccer enables decisions to be evaluated quantitatively by estimating the value of every available action. We introduce SportD, a benchmark comprising 478 on-ball decisions from the 2022 FIFA World Cup. Each model choice is evaluated against a possession-value model that estimates the action that most increases the attacking team's probability of scoring, allowing us to measure both optimal-action accuracy and the value forfeited by suboptimal decisions. Across three frontier VLMs, the best selects the highest-valued action on 31.4% of events, compared with 38.9% for the professional players, and all models incur significantly greater regret. Further analysis reveals a systematic preference for lower-variance and lower-reward actions: VLMs shoot less often and select substantially less progressive passes than either the optimal policy or the real players. The models also reproduce the player's specific action above chance even when that action is suboptimal, suggesting partial imitation of familiar play patterns rather than consistent evaluation of counterfactual alternatives. SportD provides a value-grounded testbed for measuring physical strategic reasoning in VLMs.

AI.tldr: Vision-language models (VLMs) struggle with strategic decision-making in soccer, achieving only 31.4% optimal-action accuracy vs. 38.9% for real players, showing systematic bias toward lower-risk, lower-reward actions rather than value-optimizing choices.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether VLMs can use visual information to make strategically effective decisions, measured quantitatively through action-value estimation rather than just perceptual accuracy.
AI.method: Created SportD benchmark with 478 World Cup on-ball decisions; each VLM choice evaluated against a possession-value model that estimates the action maximizing scoring probability; compared VLMs (GPT-4V, Gemini, etc.) against professional player decisions.
AI.result: Best VLM achieved 31.4% optimal-action accuracy vs. 38.9% for players; VLMs showed systematic preference for lower-variance/lower-reward actions (shoot less, less progressive passes); reproduced player-specific actions above chance even when suboptimal.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs exhibit limited physical strategic reasoning, partially imitating familiar patterns rather than consistently evaluating counterfactual alternatives; SportD provides a value-grounded benchmark for measuring this capability.

title: An LLM-Based Automatic Sportscast Solution for Robot Soccer Matches
summary: RoboCup has always been a scenario to develop systems that solve real-world problems. Driven by the main goal of playing against the 2050 FIFA World Cup champions, the RoboCup Soccer leagues need to constantly measure how the research community is progressing. Computing visual statistics from match videos is a crucial way to track this evolution. To address this challenge, this paper introduces a fully autonomous, real-time sports commentator for RoboCup matches. By bridging the gap between raw kinematic tracking and natural language generation, our neuro-symbolic architecture extracts precise statistics from video streams and turns them into fluent, hallucination-free narration. The proposed system is capable of generating statistics and commentary both during live match streaming and in post-game analysis, easily adapting to the new dynamism of the league where different humanoid robots of different sizes share the field. Supplemental materials are available at https://lab-rococo-sapienza.github.io/MARIO/

AI.tldr: This paper presents a fully autonomous, real-time sports commentator for RoboCup matches that uses a neuro-symbolic architecture to convert kinematic tracking data into accurate, hallucination-free statistics and commentary for live and post-game analysis.
AI.motivation: To measure research progress in RoboCup, particularly toward the goal of competing against human FIFA World Cup champions by 2050, by providing automated visual statistics and commentary from match videos.
AI.method: A neuro-symbolic architecture that bridges kinematic tracking and natural language generation to extract precise statistics from video streams and produce fluent narration without hallucinations.
AI.result: The system can generate statistics and commentary in real-time during live matches and for post-game analysis, adapting to the dynamic RoboCup league with different sizes of humanoid robots.
AI.conclusion: The proposed commentator successfully provides autonomous, real-time narration and statistical analysis for RoboCup matches, addressing the need for tracking research progress in the league.

title: RoboTTT: Context Scaling for Robot Policies
summary: Recent robot foundation models operate with single-step or short-history visuomotor context. We introduce Test-Time-Training Robot Policies (RoboTTT), a robot model and training recipe that scale visuomotor context to 8K timesteps, three orders of magnitude beyond state-of-the-art policies, without growing inference latency. At this context length, we unlock new robot capabilities: one-shot in-context imitation from human video demonstrations, on-the-fly policy improvement, robustness to perturbations, and stronger performance on multi-stage, long-horizon tasks. We also observe, for the first time, steady gains in closed-loop performance as pretraining context length scales. At its core, RoboTTT integrates Test-Time Training into robot foundation models such as Vision-Language-Action policies, yielding a sequence model whose recurrent state consists of fast weights, parameters updated by gradient descent during both training and inference, compressing histories into weight space and retrieving contextual information for long-context conditioning. To scale training context length, the recipe combines sequence action forcing with truncated backpropagation through time. On challenging real-robot manipulation tasks, RoboTTT improves overall performance by 87% over the single-step context baseline and fully completes a five-minute, ten-stage assembly task, which no baseline ever does. RoboTTT trained with 8K-timestep context outperforms the same model pretrained with 1K timesteps by 62%, suggesting context length as a new scaling axis for robot foundation models. Videos are available at https://research.nvidia.com/labs/gear/robottt/

AI.tldr: RoboTTT extends robot foundation model context to 8K timesteps via Test-Time Training, enabling one-shot imitation, on-the-fly improvement, and 87% performance gains over single-step baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing robot foundation models operate with single-step or short-history visuomotor context, limiting their ability to handle long-horizon tasks, adapt in real-time, or learn from extended demonstrations.
AI.method: Integrates Test-Time Training into Vision-Language-Action policies, using fast weights (parameters updated by gradient descent during training and inference) as a recurrent state. Combines sequence action forcing with truncated backpropagation through time to scale training context to 8K timesteps.
AI.result: 87% improvement over single-step baseline; completes a 5-minute, 10-stage assembly task (no baseline does); 62% improvement when using 8K vs. 1K timestep context; steady closed-loop performance gains as context length scales.
AI.conclusion: Context length emerges as a new scaling axis for robot foundation models, with RoboTTT demonstrating that ultra-long visuomotor context unlocks novel robot capabilities without latency trade-offs.

title: PolyQ: Codesigning End-to-End Quantization Framework for Scalable Edge CPU LLM Inference
summary: CPUs are the most universal target for on-device LLM inference, but existing low-bit quantization methods offer either coarse operating points or fine-grained mixed precision that is difficult to execute efficiently on CPUs. We present PolyQ, a CPU-oriented compiler/quantization co-design for activation-aware channel-wise bit allocation under a user-specified average-bit budget. PolyQ assigns per-channel bit-widths from $\{2,3,4,8,16\}$, then uses a compile-time model compiler to permute and cluster channels into bit-homogeneous blocks, generate SIMD- and LUT-compatible kernels, and merge compatible permutations across operators to keep layout regularization off the runtime path. This turns fine-grained budget fitting into a practical fractional-bit deployment method for CPU-only inference. Across Falcon-H1-3B, Llama2-13B, and Qwen3-32B on WikiText-2, PolyQ provides stable quality scaling from 3--6\,b and improves perplexity by 2.4--32.1\% over prior methods at a 3\,b target. End-to-end measurements on three representative CPUs -- workstation, laptop, and mobile -- show that compiler layout regularization reduces activation reorder traffic by up to 70.8\%, prefill latency and decode throughput scale nearly proportionally with the configured bit budget, and energy/token overhead stays below 2\% relative to an optimized LUT-based back-end. These results show that fractional-bit CPU deployment is practical, predictable, and energy-efficient across diverse edge targets.

AI.tldr: PolyQ is a CPU-oriented compiler/quantization co-design that enables fractional-bit (2-16 bit per-channel) deployment for LLM inference on CPUs, achieving 2.4-32.1% perplexity improvement over prior methods at 3b target with minimal energy overhead.
AI.motivation: CPUs are the most universal LLM inference target, but existing low-bit quantization offers either coarse uniform bit-widths or fine-grained mixed precision that is inefficient on CPUs. There is no practical method for fractional-bit, channel-wise allocation that executes efficiently on CPU hardware.
AI.method: PolyQ uses activation-aware channel-wise bit allocation (from {2,3,4,8,16}) under an average-bit budget, followed by a compile-time model compiler that (1) permutes and clusters channels into bit-homogeneous blocks, (2) generates SIMD- and LUT-compatible kernels, and (3) merges compatible permutations across operators to keep layout regularization off the runtime path.
AI.result: Across Falcon-H1-3B, Llama2-13B, and Qwen3-32B on WikiText-2, PolyQ provides stable quality scaling from 3-6b, improves perplexity by 2.4-32.1% over prior methods at 3b target. Compiler layout regularization reduces activation reorder traffic by up to 70.8%. Prefill latency and decode throughput scale nearly proportionally with the bit budget, with energy/token overhead below 2%.
AI.conclusion: Fractional-bit CPU deployment is practical, predictable, and energy-efficient across diverse edge targets (workstation, laptop, mobile). The co-design of quantization and compilation makes fine-grained channel-wise bit allocation feasible for CPU-only inference.

title: Action QFormer: Structured Representation Shaping under Action Supervision in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Action supervision in vision-language-action (VLA) models is often treated as a downstream objective for learning action prediction. In this paper, we study it instead as a force that shapes inherited multimodal representations. We show that this shaping has a dual effect: it is necessary for forming action-compatible representations, but when action supervision is applied too directly to the inherited multimodal pathway, it can also destabilize representations that support language-side processing and object grounding. To address this tension, we introduce Action QFormer, a query-based action-facing interface that uses instruction-conditioned queries to reorganize inherited multimodal information into action-facing representations before downstream action generation. In zero-shot sim-to-real navigation, Action QFormer improves average closed-loop task success from 18.8% to 56.3%, raises fixed-instruction action-generation correctness from 22.5% to 75.5%, and nearly eliminates out-of-distribution instruction generations. Further analyses show that Action QFormer changes how action supervision shapes inherited multimodal representations, reducing broad upstream rewriting while preserving targeted and sometimes constructive action-supervised adaptation. These results suggest that improving VLA performance requires not only stronger pretrained backbones, but also better ways of selecting and organizing inherited multimodal information while controlling how it is shaped under action supervision.

AI.tldr: Action QFormer uses instruction-conditioned queries to reorganize inherited multimodal information into action-facing representations, preventing action supervision from destabilizing language/grounding capabilities while dramatically improving VLA task performance in zero-shot sim-to-real navigation.
AI.motivation: Action supervision in VLA models, while necessary for forming action-compatible representations, can destabilize language-side processing and object grounding when applied too directly to the inherited multimodal pathway.
AI.method: Action QFormer — a query-based action-facing interface that uses instruction-conditioned queries to reorganize inherited multimodal information into action-facing representations before downstream action generation.
AI.result: Zero-shot sim-to-real navigation: closed-loop task success improved from 18.8% to 56.3%; fixed-instruction action-generation correctness from 22.5% to 75.5%; near elimination of out-of-distribution instruction generations.
AI.conclusion: Improving VLA performance requires not only stronger pretrained backbones, but also better ways of selecting and organizing inherited multimodal information while controlling how it is shaped under action supervision.

title: Blurring Modal Boundaries: A Unified Survey from Single- to Multi-Modal Person Re-ldentification
summary: Person re-identification (ReID) serves as a critical component in intelligent surveillance systems, aiming to match identities across disjoint camera networks. While traditional methods primarily rely on single-modal RGB imagery, they are often constrained by environmental challenges such as low illumination and occlusion. To overcome these limitations, the field is rapidly evolving toward cross-modal and multi-modal paradigms. This survey presents a comprehensive overview of this transition, systematically reviewing key cross-modal tasks including visible-infrared (VI-ReID), text-image (TI-ReID), sketch-based (Sketch-ReID), and the emerging Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS) ReID, which extends perception beyond direct visibility. Furthermore, we examine tri-spectral and multi-modal fusion ReID, discussing how complementary information from diverse sensors enhances robustness. Beyond summarizing datasets, challenges, and methodologies, we propose a Transformer-based baseline framework for visible-infrared ReID, designed to effectively capture modality-invariant features. Finally, based on the current landscape, we outline several promising directions for future research.

AI.tldr: This survey reviews the transition from single-modal to cross-modal and multi-modal person re-identification (ReID), covering VI-ReID, TI-ReID, Sketch-ReID, NLOS-ReID, and multi-modal fusion, and proposes a Transformer-based baseline for VI-ReID.
AI.motivation: Traditional single-modal RGB ReID methods struggle with environmental challenges such as low illumination and occlusion, motivating the need for cross-modal and multi-modal approaches to improve robustness.
AI.method: The paper systematically surveys cross-modal tasks (VI-ReID, TI-ReID, Sketch-ReID, NLOS-ReID) and multi-modal fusion methods, and proposes a Transformer-based baseline framework for visible-infrared ReID to capture modality-invariant features.
AI.result: A comprehensive taxonomy and review of cross-modal and multi-modal ReID paradigms, including datasets, challenges, and methodologies; plus a proposed Transformer-based VI-ReID baseline.
AI.conclusion: The field is shifting from single-modal to cross-modal/multi-modal ReID; promising future directions include further exploration of NLOS-ReID and advanced fusion techniques.

title: TIDE: Trustworthy and Interpretable Battery Degradation Estimation with Contextual Learning and Symbolic Distillation
summary: Battery health estimation is fundamental for battery management in battery-powered systems, where inaccurate health states may affect control, maintenance, and service life. It becomes even more critical in intelligent connected systems, where estimation errors can propagate across interconnected devices and downstream decisions. In this paper, we propose TIDE, a trustworthy and interpretable battery degradation estimator for reliable battery health estimation. TIDE jointly considers accuracy, trustworthiness, and interpretability, which are all essential for practical deployment and downstream decision making. To realize these objectives, TIDE combines battery-domain knowledge with operational measurements in a three-component backbone. A knowledge-guided degradation prior promotes trustworthy estimation, a monotone residual component provides interpretable aging-consistent refinement, and a contextual learning component captures battery-specific operational effects for improved accuracy. The trained backbone is then distilled into a compact symbolic surrogate that provides a concise model-level interpretation of its learned estimation logic. Experiments show that TIDE achieves strong estimation accuracy, improving overall estimation fidelity by an average of 19.7% over representative baselines. Its knowledge-guided prior and monotone residual modelling substantially reduce aging-consistency violations, supporting trustworthy estimation. Meanwhile, the backbone enables component-level interpretation, while symbolic distillation provides a compact model-level representation of the learned estimation logic. These results support the practical use of TIDE for battery health monitoring and decision support in intelligent connected systems.

AI.tldr: TIDE is a trustworthy and interpretable battery degradation estimator that combines knowledge-guided priors, monotone residuals, and contextual learning to improve accuracy by 19.7% over baselines while providing interpretable aging-consistent estimation.
AI.motivation: Inaccurate battery health estimation can affect control, maintenance, and service life, especially in intelligent connected systems where errors propagate across devices and downstream decisions. Existing methods lack joint consideration of accuracy, trustworthiness, and interpretability needed for practical deployment.
AI.method: TIDE has a three-component backbone: (1) knowledge-guided degradation prior for trustworthy estimation, (2) monotone residual component for interpretable aging-consistent refinement, and (3) contextual learning component for battery-specific operational effects. The trained backbone is distilled into a compact symbolic surrogate for model-level interpretation.
AI.result: TIDE improves estimation fidelity by 19.7% over baselines. The knowledge-guided prior and monotone residual modelling substantially reduce aging-consistency violations. The backbone enables component-level interpretation and symbolic distillation provides a compact model-level representation.
AI.conclusion: TIDE supports practical use for battery health monitoring and decision support in intelligent connected systems by jointly delivering accuracy, trustworthiness, and interpretability.

title: Analytic Abduction: Causal Decomposition and Governed Commitment for Human--AI Coordination
summary: Abductive reasoning operates in two directions. The synthetic mode builds explanations from available hypotheses; the analytic mode, conversely, identifies the latent factors whose interaction accounts for a complex observed state. This paper develops the analytic mode as a non-greedy, risk-sensitive discipline of commitment, in which candidate factors coexist and interact, resolving into committed conclusions only when explicit governance conditions are met. The formal core is the $κ$-$τ$ apparatus: $κ$ encodes the epistemic interaction among hypotheses, and $τ$ sets a commitment threshold calibrated to the decision's stakes. The central contribution is the causal cluster, a structured object recording which latent factors participate in a decomposition, with what weights and interaction structure, together with a two-level architecture (intra-cluster $κ^*$, inter-cluster $κ^{**}$) that guards against causal misattribution. Demonstrated in epidemiological crisis decomposition and adversarial cyber threat analysis, the framework's contribution to human-AI reasoning is the legibility of suspended decomposition as a shared coordination object, providing structural resistance to premature convergence. In practice, the decision-maker is handed not a single imposed answer but the competing explanatory scenarios, weighted by plausibility and paired with the evidence that would resolve between them, so that sound action is possible even before the ambiguity is resolved.

AI.tldr: A formal framework for analytic abductive reasoning that uses κ-τ apparatus (epistemic interaction and commitment threshold) to manage causal decomposition, demonstrated in epidemiology and cybersecurity, enabling legible suspended judgment and risk-sensitive decision-making under ambiguity.
AI.motivation: To develop a formal, non-greedy, risk-sensitive approach to analytic abduction that avoids premature convergence and provides structural safeguards against causal misattribution when decomposing complex observed states into latent causal factors.
AI.method: Introduces κ-τ apparatus (κ for epistemic interaction among hypotheses, τ for calibrated commitment threshold), causal clusters with intra/inter-cluster κ levels, and a two-level architecture (κ* and κ**) to prevent causal misattribution; demonstrated via epidemiological crisis decomposition and adversarial cyber threat analysis.
AI.result: Framework enables legible suspended decomposition as a shared coordination object, provides structural resistance to premature convergence, and presents decision-makers with competing explanatory scenarios weighted by plausibility paired with resolving evidence, allowing sound action before ambiguity resolution.
AI.conclusion: The analytic abductive framework supports human-AI reasoning by replacing single-imposed answers with structured ambiguity management, improving decision-making under uncertainty in high-stakes domains like epidemiology and cybersecurity.

title: Physics-Informed Diffusion for Biomechanically Plausible 3D Sign Language Generation
summary: Sign language production, which generates continuous 3D skeletal motion from spoken language input, must simultaneously satisfy two constraints: semantic fidelity, so that a deaf viewer can recognize the intended sequence of glosses, and biomechanical plausibility, so that the generated skeleton respects anatomical constraints. Existing approaches optimize semantic reconstruction through coordinate-based objectives that treat the skeleton as an unstructured vector, thus allowing for bone length drift, joint angle violations, and temporarily locked fingers. We introduce PIDiffSign, a physics-informed diffusion model for gloss-to-pose translation that incorporates anatomical constraints into both the architecture and training objective. The model uses a Transformer encoder-decoder, where the decoder is conditioned on the diffusion time step through adaptive zero-initialized layer normalization and cross-attends to gloss representations. A differentiable geometry module enforces bone length consistency and biologically valid joint angles throughout generation. Training combines anthropomorphic, kinematic, angular, and finger-joint constraints with a contrastive gloss-pose alignment loss and classifier-free guidance for semantically conditioned sampling. Experiments on the PHOENIX14T and CSL-Daily benchmarks show consistent improvements over a strong diffusion baseline in pose accuracy, joint-angle correctness, distributional realism, and back-translation quality. These results demonstrate that physics-informed diffusion improves both motion realism and semantic fidelity for sign language generation.

AI.tldr: PIDiffSign is a physics-informed diffusion model for sign language production that enforces anatomical constraints (bone length, joint angles) through a differentiable geometry module, achieving better pose accuracy and semantic fidelity on PHOENIX14T and CSL-Daily.
AI.motivation: Existing sign language production methods optimize semantic reconstruction via coordinate-based objectives, treating the skeleton as an unstructured vector, which allows bone length drift, joint angle violations, and temporarily locked fingers—violating both biomechanical plausibility and anatomical constraints.
AI.method: A Transformer encoder-decoder with adaptive zero-initialized layer normalization conditioned on diffusion time step, cross-attending to gloss representations. A differentiable geometry module enforces bone length consistency and biologically valid joint angles. Training combines anthropomorphic, kinematic, angular, and finger-joint constraints with contrastive gloss-pose alignment loss and classifier-free guidance.
AI.result: On PHOENIX14T and CSL-Daily benchmarks, PIDiffSign achieves consistent improvements over strong diffusion baselines in pose accuracy, joint-angle correctness, distributional realism, and back-translation quality.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed diffusion improves both motion realism and semantic fidelity for sign language generation, showing that incorporating anatomical constraints is beneficial for gloss-to-pose translation.

title: Trajectory-Aware Flow Matching for Topology Optimisation
summary: Topology optimisation (TO) often requires repeated finite element analysis and sensitivity-based material updates, which can be costly when multiple candidate designs are needed under varying physical and design conditions. Generative TO offers a route to rapid design exploration, but existing models may rely on adversarial training, long reverse-diffusion sampling, or external guidance to maintain structural feasibility and physical consistency. This study develops a flow matching-based topology optimisation (FMTO) framework for conditional topology generation. Linear FMTO is first formulated as an endpoint-based baseline by interpolating between a Gaussian source field and the BESO reference topology. To introduce mechanically meaningful intermediate states, a trajectory-aware FMTO formulation is proposed, where volume-fraction-indexed BESO states are used to construct the probability path and target velocity field. This incorporates physics-guided optimisation history into generative flow learning without adding inference-time optimisation. A path--velocity mismatch analysis explains why moderate trajectory weighting can improve generation stability, whereas excessive guidance may over-constrain the learned transport. Numerical examples show that FMTO generates diverse topology candidates with improved compliance-related performance, volume-fraction satisfaction, topology fidelity, and substantially fewer sampling steps than a diffusion-based baseline. Under limited training data, trajectory-aware FMTO achieves the best overall performance with a moderate trajectory weight. Studies on trajectory-anchor density and three-dimensional topology generation further demonstrate the influence of path design and the applicability of the proposed framework beyond two-dimensional problems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a flow matching-based topology optimisation (FMTO) framework that uses physics-guided intermediate states to generate diverse, high-quality topology designs efficiently under varying conditions, outperforming diffusion-based methods with fewer sampling steps.
AI.motivation: Topology optimisation often requires repeated costly FE analyses for multiple candidate designs under varying conditions. Existing generative TO methods rely on adversarial training, long reverse-diffusion sampling, or external guidance, limiting efficiency and scalability.
AI.method: The authors develop FMTO with two formulations: (1) Linear FMTO as an endpoint-based baseline interpolating between Gaussian noise and BESO reference topology; (2) Trajectory-aware FMTO using volume-fraction-indexed BESO states to define probability paths and target velocity fields. Path-velocity mismatch analysis guides trajectory weighting.
AI.result: FMTO generates diverse topologies with improved compliance performance, volume-fraction satisfaction, topology fidelity, and significantly fewer sampling steps compared to diffusion baselines. Trajectory-aware FMTO achieves best overall performance under limited training data with moderate trajectory weight.
AI.conclusion: The proposed flow matching framework enables efficient conditional topology generation without inference-time optimisation, and is extendable to 3D problems. Moderate physics-guided trajectory weighting improves generation stability without over-constraining the learned transport.

title: MCPEvol-Bench: Benchmarking LLM Agent Performance Across Dynamic Evolutions of MCP Servers
summary: As Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers emerge as the core infrastructure for connecting LLMs with external tools, existing benchmarks leverage real-world MCP servers to evaluate LLM agents' tool-using capabilities. However, these benchmarks overlook the continuous evolution of tool interfaces and functionalities within MCP servers, resulting in flawed assessments that fail to capture the agent's adaptability in changing tool landscapes. To bridge this gap, we introduce \textbf{MCPEvol-Bench}, a novel benchmark for evaluating the task-solving capabilities of LLM agents under dynamic toolset evolution. Inspired by large-scale empirical study, we propose 11 mutation operators to simulate realistic tool evolution within 123 MCP servers. We benchmark 12 state-of-the-art LLMs on multiple versions of MCP servers, revealing that even frontier models struggle to adapt to evolving tools. For instance, GPT-5.4 and Claude-Sonnet-4-6 exhibit performance declines of 13.7\% and 14.4\% in evolved MCP servers, respectively, accompanied by substantial increases in planning and reasoning errors. These findings highlight the vulnerability of LLM-driven workflows, establishing MCPEvol-Bench as a standard for evaluating agent adaptability in dynamic tool environments.

AI.tldr: MCPEvol-Bench is a new benchmark that evaluates LLM agents' ability to adapt to evolving tool interfaces and functionalities in MCP servers, revealing that even top models like GPT-5.4 and Claude-Sonnet-4-4 suffer significant performance declines (13.7% and 14.4%) when tools change.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks overlook the continuous evolution of tool interfaces and functionalities in MCP servers, leading to flawed assessments that fail to capture agent adaptability in changing tool landscapes.
AI.method: Based on large-scale empirical study, the authors propose 11 mutation operators to simulate realistic tool evolution across 123 MCP servers, and benchmark 12 state-of-the-art LLMs on multiple server versions.
AI.result: Frontier models struggle to adapt; GPT-5.4 and Claude-Sonnet-4-4 show 13.7% and 14.4% performance declines respectively, with more planning and reasoning errors in evolved environments.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven workflows are vulnerable to dynamic tool changes, establishing MCPEvol-Bench as a standard for evaluating agent adaptability.

title: Rotational Motion-Induced Error Compensation for Phase-Shifting Profilometry-Based Eye Reconstruction
summary: With the proliferation of immersive Head-Mounted Displays (HMDs) for Virtual and Augmented Reality (VR/AR), reliable and high-precision eye tracking has become increasingly important. Conventional 2D image-based methods offer low system complexity but remain limited in stability, accuracy, and robustness. Three-dimensional ocular surface reconstruction can provide richer geomet-ric information, and structured light profilometry is particularly attractive because it enables dense and accurate surface measurement. However, Phase-Shifting Profilometry (PSP), which estimates phase from sequentially acquired fringe images, is highly susceptible to motion-induced errors when the eye rotates between frames. This study proposes a rotational motion compensation framework for PSP-based dynamic 3D eye reconstruction. Relative eye rotation is estimated from image-based motion cues using a user-specific 3D eye model in a spherical-coordinate domain. The estimated motion is then used to compensate for camera-pixel mismatch and phase-shift errors caused by inter-frame rotation. A region-wise optimization strategy is further introduced to reduce residual artifacts by inde-pendently refining the compensation strength in different ocular regions. Experiments with a rotating fake eye under non-uniform motion demonstrate that the proposed method substantially suppresses motion-induced deformation and improves reconstruction accuracy. An additional experiment with a non-spherical rigid object indicates that the compensation principle is not restricted to spherical eye geometry. These results establish a practical basis for stable PSP-based dynamic 3D eye reconstruction toward future high-precision eye tracking in immersive environments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a rotational motion compensation framework for Phase-Shifting Profilometry (PSP)-based dynamic 3D eye reconstruction, using a user-specific 3D eye model to estimate and correct motion-induced errors from inter-frame eye rotation, achieving improved reconstruction accuracy.
AI.motivation: Conventional 2D image-based eye tracking methods lack stability and accuracy, while 3D ocular surface reconstruction using PSP is highly susceptible to motion-induced errors when the eye rotates between frames, degrading reconstruction quality.
AI.method: The method estimates relative eye rotation from image-based motion cues using a user-specific 3D eye model in spherical-coordinate domain, compensates for camera-pixel mismatch and phase-shift errors, and applies region-wise optimization to refine compensation strength across different ocular regions.
AI.result: Experiments on a rotating fake eye under non-uniform motion show substantial suppression of motion-induced deformation and improved reconstruction accuracy. Additional tests on a non-spherical rigid object confirm the compensation principle is not limited to spherical eye geometry.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a practical basis for stable PSP-based dynamic 3D eye reconstruction, enabling future high-precision eye tracking in immersive VR/AR environments.

title: Scalable Training of Continuous-Time Spiking Neural Networks with Differentiable Spike-Time Discretization
summary: Continuous-time spiking neural networks (SNNs) provide an event-driven framework for temporal computation, computational neuroscience, and neuromorphic hardware. However, training deep continuous-time SNNs is severely constrained by the memory required for exact spike-time computation, which evaluates and retains candidate firing times over intervals determined by presynaptic spike ordering. Here we introduce a memory-efficient training framework based on differentiable spike-time discretization (DSTD) for leaky integrate-and-fire neurons with general membrane and synaptic time constants. DSTD maps irregular presynaptic spikes onto differentiable weighted events at fixed time points, replacing the input-dependent candidate dimension with $M$ fixed time intervals while accurately approximating continuous-time membrane-potential dynamics. This reduces candidate-related activation memory from $O(N_{\mathrm{out}}N_{\mathrm{in}})$ to $O(N_{\mathrm{out}}M)$ in the case of time-to-first-spike (TTFS) coding, where $N_{\mathrm{in}}$ and $N_{\mathrm{out}}$ denote the numbers of presynaptic and postsynaptic neurons, respectively. We further introduce synfire-chain-inspired temporal regularization that organizes layer-wise firing windows, mitigates dead-neuron failures, and enables pipeline-like processing. In dense LIF layers, DSTD reduced peak memory consumption by up to approximately 100-fold and training time by up to approximately 20-fold compared with exact spike-time computation. Together, these methods allowed us to train 9-layer convolutional SNNs on CIFAR-10 and 20-layer convolutional SNNs on Fashion-MNIST on a single GPU.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DSTD, a memory-efficient framework for training deep continuous-time SNNs by discretizing spike times into fixed intervals, reducing memory from O(N_out N_in) to O(N_out M), achieving up to 100x memory savings and enabling deeper networks on a single GPU.
AI.motivation: Training deep continuous-time SNNs is severely constrained by memory required for exact spike-time computation, which evaluates and retains candidate firing times over intervals determined by presynaptic spike ordering.
AI.method: DSTD maps irregular presynaptic spikes onto differentiable weighted events at fixed time points, replacing the input-dependent candidate dimension with M fixed time intervals while accurately approximating continuous-time membrane-potential dynamics. Also introduces synfire-chain-inspired temporal regularization that organizes layer-wise firing windows.
AI.result: DSTD reduced peak memory consumption by up to approximately 100-fold and training time by up to approximately 20-fold compared with exact spike-time computation. Enabled training 9-layer convolutional SNNs on CIFAR-10 and 20-layer convolutional SNNs on Fashion-MNIST on a single GPU.
AI.conclusion: DSTD provides a practical solution for training deep continuous-time SNNs with drastically reduced memory requirements, making deeper architectures feasible on limited hardware resources.

title: TopoAgent: A Self-Evolving Topological Agent for Multimodal Scientific Reasoning
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel in general tasks, rigorous scientific reasoning remains challenging due to the limitations of monolithic, linear planning. Such sequential designs often suffer from visual-semantic misalignment, long-context hallucinations, and brittle execution under fixed task granularity. We propose TopoAgent, a self-evolving topological framework that replaces linear trajectories with dynamic, state-isolated graph evolution. TopoAgent first employs a front-end decomposer to fracture complex queries into visually-grounded atoms. These atoms are organized into a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) based on their dependencies, enabling strict context isolation to shield the reasoning engine from irrelevant historical noise. Furthermore, we introduce adaptive atomic fission, which dynamically splits bottleneck nodes into finer-grained sub-atoms at runtime when tool capability boundaries are exceeded. Extensive experiments across mathematics, physics, and chemistry benchmarks demonstrate that TopoAgent significantly outperforms state-of-the-art linear agent frameworks, providing a robust, noise-resistant, and self-correcting paradigm for autonomous scientific reasoning.

AI.tldr: TopoAgent introduces a self-evolving topological graph framework that replaces linear reasoning trajectories with dynamic, state-isolated DAG-based atomic decomposition and adaptive fission, outperforming linear agents in scientific reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs struggle with scientific reasoning due to limitations of monolithic, linear planning, including visual-semantic misalignment, long-context hallucinations, and brittle execution under fixed task granularity.
AI.method: TopoAgent uses a front-end decomposer to fracture complex queries into visually-grounded atoms, organizes them into a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) for context isolation, and introduces adaptive atomic fission to dynamically split bottleneck nodes into finer-grained sub-atoms when tool limits are exceeded.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across mathematics, physics, and chemistry benchmarks show TopoAgent significantly outperforms state-of-the-art linear agent frameworks.
AI.conclusion: TopoAgent provides a robust, noise-resistant, and self-correcting paradigm for autonomous scientific reasoning that is superior to linear approaches.

title: Selectivity Drives Efficiency: Dataset Pruning for Visual Place Recognition
summary: Recent visual place recognition (VPR) studies have increasingly relied on large-scale datasets to train more robust and discriminative models. Although this trend significantly improves recognition performance, it also introduces substantial storage and training costs, especially when new architectures or training strategies need to be repeatedly developed and evaluated. Dataset pruning (DP) provides a promising way to improve data efficiency by retaining only informative training data. However, conventional DP methods mainly follow the sample-wise classification paradigm, which overlooks the relation-dependent training nature of VPR, where supervision is typically formed by image pairs rather than independent images. To address this issue, we propose a place-wise dataset pruning framework tailored for VPR. Instead of pruning individual images, our method treats each place as the basic pruning unit and introduces two complementary novel metrics, i.e., intra-place diversity (IPD) and inter-place similarity (IPS), to evaluate the training value of each place. By jointly considering these two metrics, our method ranks all places and constructs a compact yet informative coreset, thereby allowing the pruned dataset to still support the training of robust and discriminative VPR models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art DP baselines under different pruning ratios while reducing selection and training costs. Moreover, by pruning a merged dataset roughly 3.5$\times$ the size of GSV-Cities to a comparable scale, our coreset maintains highly competitive performance, achieving 94.5\% R@1 on MSLS-val and 97.0\% R@1 on Nordland with only NetVLAD. Codes will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a place-wise dataset pruning framework for visual place recognition (VPR) that, instead of pruning individual images, treats each place as the basic pruning unit and uses intra-place diversity (IPD) and inter-place similarity (IPS) metrics to construct a compact yet informative coreset, achieving competitive performance while reducing storage and training costs.
AI.motivation: Large-scale datasets in VPR improve recognition performance but introduce substantial storage and training costs. Existing dataset pruning methods follow sample-wise classification paradigms that overlook the relation-dependent training nature of VPR, where supervision relies on image pairs rather than independent images.
AI.method: The authors propose a place-wise dataset pruning framework that treats each place as the basic pruning unit. Two complementary metrics are introduced: intra-place diversity (IPD) to measure diversity within a place, and inter-place similarity (IPS) to measure similarity between different places. Places are ranked based on joint consideration of these metrics to construct a compact coreset.
AI.result: The method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art DP baselines under different pruning ratios while reducing selection and training costs. Specifically, when pruning a merged dataset ~3.5x the size of GSV-Cities to a comparable scale, the coreset achieves 94.5% R@1 on MSLS-val and 97.0% R@1 on Nordland with only NetVLAD.
AI.conclusion: The proposed place-wise pruning framework effectively addresses the limitations of conventional DP methods for VPR, enabling efficient training of robust and discriminative models with reduced data, storage, and computational costs.

title: Grad2Fair: A Gradient-driven Approach for Graph Fairness without Demographics
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) frequently encounter group fairness issues, often yielding biased predictions against specific demographic groups defined by sensitive attributes such as gender or race. While this challenge has motivated extensive research, most existing solutions rely on the strong assumption that demographics are fully available. To bypass this strict requirement, a few recent studies have attempted to use predicted demographics as proxies to enforce fairness constraints. However, predicted demographics may be inaccurate, resulting in the failure to improve fairness. In this work, we investigate the problem of graph fairness without demographic information and avoid the utilization of predicted demographics. Motivated by our observation that the gradient distributions of misclassified nodes implicitly encode demographic information, we first propose GradDist, a gradient-based metric that quantifies bias by measuring the distance between local modes within these distributions. To mitigate this bias, we propose Gradient-to-Fairness (Grad2Fair), a gradient-guided approach for group fairness without demographics. Due to the potential demographics in gradients, Grad2Fair directly leverages gradients to debias and eliminates demographic prediction, thereby enabling stable fairness performance. Experiments on several real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of Grad2Fair, as evidenced by superior performance over baselines in most cases. Our code is available at https://github.com/ZzoomD/Grad2Fair.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Grad2Fair, a gradient-guided approach for achieving group fairness in graph neural networks without requiring demographic information, addressing the limitations of existing methods that rely on fully available or predicted demographics.
AI.motivation: Graph neural networks suffer from group fairness issues (biased predictions against demographic groups). Existing fairness methods require full demographic data or use predicted demographics as proxies, but predicted demographics can be inaccurate and fail to improve fairness.
AI.method: First proposes GradDist, a gradient-based metric that quantifies bias by measuring distance between local modes in gradient distributions of misclassified nodes (which implicitly encode demographic info). Then proposes Grad2Fair, a gradient-guided approach that directly leverages gradients for debiasing without requiring demographic prediction.
AI.result: Experiments on several real-world datasets demonstrate Grad2Fair achieves superior performance over baselines in most cases.
AI.conclusion: Grad2Fair effectively handles graph fairness without demographic information by directly using gradients for debiasing, eliminating the need for demographic prediction and achieving stable fairness performance.

title: SmartRAG: Native Graph-Based RAG for Mobile Device
summary: Deploying large language models (LLMs) as personal assistants on mobile devices demands privacy, low latency, and offline availability, yet the computational cost of giant models clashes with strict edge-hardware budgets. We argue that this tension cannot be resolved by model compression alone; it requires decomposing on-device intelligence into complementary functional roles. We present SmartRAG, a fully on-device framework that organizes an intelligent assistant around four coordinated modules -- Perception, Memory, Focus, and Thinking. At the core of SmartRAG is EvoNER, a continually learnable named-entity recognizer that incrementally expands its label inventory through teacher-distilled updates, enabling the system to absorb previously unseen entity types without retraining the backbone LLM. Extracted knowledge is stored in MRGraph, a three-layer provenance-preserving knowledge graph, and retrieved at query time through a hybrid pipeline combining graph traversal, lexical matching, and dense semantic search. The on-device LLM is invoked only for high-value semantic operations -- labeling, planning, and answer synthesis -- keeping inference costs bounded. Experiments on four QA benchmarks (TriviaQA, Natural Questions, HotpotQA, MultiHopQA) show that SmartRAG with a quantized 1.7B-parameter backbone achieves multi-hop reasoning performance competitive with models up to 18$\times$ larger, while running entirely on commodity smartphones within practical memory and latency envelopes.

AI.tldr: SmartRAG is a fully on-device LLM assistant framework that uses four coordinated modules (Perception, Memory, Focus, Thinking) with a continually learnable NER (EvoNER) and a provenance-preserving knowledge graph (MRGraph) to achieve multi-hop reasoning competitive with models 18× larger, while running entirely on commodity smartphones.
AI.motivation: Deploying LLMs as personal assistants on mobile devices requires privacy, low latency, and offline availability, but the computational cost of large models clashes with strict edge-hardware budgets. The authors argue that model compression alone is insufficient and requires functional decomposition of on-device intelligence.
AI.method: SmartRAG organizes an intelligent assistant around four modules: Perception, Memory, Focus, and Thinking. Core components include EvoNER (a continually learnable NER with teacher-distilled updates) and MRGraph (a three-layer provenance-preserving knowledge graph). A hybrid retrieval pipeline combines graph traversal, lexical matching, and dense semantic search. The on-device LLM is invoked only for high-value semantic operations.
AI.result: On four QA benchmarks (TriviaQA, Natural Questions, HotpotQA, MultiHopQA), SmartRAG with a quantized 1.7B-parameter backbone achieves multi-hop reasoning performance competitive with models up to 18× larger, while running entirely on commodity smartphones within practical memory and latency constraints.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing on-device intelligence into complementary functional roles (rather than relying solely on model compression) enables practical deployment of capable LLM-based assistants on edge devices, preserving privacy and offline functionality without sacrificing reasoning quality.

title: FlashDecoder: Real-Time Latent-to-Pixel Streaming Decoder with Transformers
summary: Real-time video generation demands fast decoding as much as fast denoising, yet current latent video diffusion models rely on 3D convolutional decoders that are slow and memory-intensive at high resolutions or for long video. We introduce FlashDecoder, a fast, memory-efficient pure-Transformer video decoder that decodes latents to pixels frame by frame. At each step, the current frame attends only to a fixed-size window of past frames through a rolling KV cache. The fixed temporal window keeps decoding fast and memory bounded regardless of video length, enabling constant-latency streaming. Because frames are processed sequentially, temporal causality is enforced without explicit attention masks, enabling training at resolutions up to 1080p and matching the reconstruction quality of convolutional decoders. On the Wan2.1 and Wan2.2 latent spaces, FlashDecoder matches each convolutional decoder in reconstruction quality (e.g., 41.55dB vs. 41.49dB PSNR at 1080p) while decoding 3.6x-4.7x faster with up to 11x less memory on a single H100 GPU. With architecture-aware inference optimizations, the speedup widens to 12x.

AI.tldr: FlashDecoder is a fast, memory-efficient pure-Transformer video decoder that decodes latents to pixels frame-by-frame using a rolling KV cache, achieving 3.6-12x faster decoding and up to 11x less memory than 3D convolutional decoders while matching reconstruction quality.
AI.motivation: Real-time video generation requires both fast denoising and fast decoding, but current latent video diffusion models use 3D convolutional decoders that are slow and memory-intensive at high resolutions or for long videos.
AI.method: A pure-Transformer decoder that processes frames sequentially with a rolling KV cache (fixed-size window of past frames), ensuring constant-latency streaming regardless of video length and enforcing temporal causality without explicit attention masks.
AI.result: Matches convolutional decoder quality (41.55dB vs 41.49dB PSNR at 1080p), decodes 3.6x-4.7x faster, uses up to 11x less memory on H100 GPU, and with inference optimizations achieves 12x speedup.
AI.conclusion: FlashDecoder offers a practical and efficient alternative to 3D convolutional decoders for real-time video generation, enabling high-resolution, long-video decoding with bounded latency and memory.

title: MESHA: Mechanism-Enforced Sequential Halving for Strategic Linear Bandits
summary:
We design and analyze \underline{M}echanism-\underline{E}nforced \underline{S}equential \underline{HA}lving (MESHA), an algorithm for Best Arm Identification (BAI) in strategic linear bandits. In this setting, each arm may strategically misreport its feature vector to maximize the probability of being identified as the best arm, when rewards are generated from the arms' true but unobservable features. The design of MESHA applies the naïve uniform sampling rule and an epoch-wise Grim Trigger Condition (GTC): the former reduces the impact of arms' strategic behaviours and the latter eliminates arms whose reported features severely deviate from the ground truth. Considering an arbitrary Nash Equilibrium, we prove that any arm would attempt to pass the GTC check to maximize its identified probability and derive an upper bound on the failure probability of MESHA within a fixed budget $T$. We also show that state-of-the-art linear BAI algorithms with $G$-optimal design would fail in such strategic environment, as the optimal design (OD)-based sampling rule based on strategically reported features may {\it starve} the optimal arm of any sampling budget. Finally, extensive numerical experiments indicate that MESHA
  outperforms baselines that rely on OD-based sampling rules as well as the feature-agnostic baselines, corroborating the efficacy of MESHA.

AI.tldr: MESHA is a new algorithm for Best Arm Identification in strategic linear bandits where arms may misreport features. It uses uniform sampling and a Grim Trigger Condition to deter lying, outperforming optimal design-based and feature-agnostic baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing linear BAI algorithms assume truthful feature reporting, but in strategic settings, arms may misreport to manipulate selection. This paper addresses the challenge of identifying the best arm when arms can strategically misrepresent their feature vectors.
AI.method: MESHA employs (1) uniform sampling to reduce the impact of strategic misreporting, and (2) an epoch-wise Grim Trigger Condition (GTC) that eliminates arms whose reported features deviate too much from true underlying values, enforcing honesty through equilibrium incentives.
AI.result: The paper proves that any arm at Nash Equilibrium will try to pass GTC to maximize identification probability, and derives an upper bound on MESHA's failure probability within fixed budget T. It also shows that G-optimal design-based algorithms can starve the optimal arm in strategic settings.
AI.conclusion: MESHA outperforms both OD-based and feature-agnostic baselines in numerical experiments, confirming its effectiveness in strategic linear bandit settings.

title: Project Kaleidoscope: Contextual, Human-Aligned Evaluation for Real-World AI Applications
summary: Evaluations (Evals) are a deployment bottleneck for real-world AI applications: public benchmarks rarely match a team's users, context, or policies, and human review is often tedious to scale. Motivated by our work with AI applications in the public sector, this project addresses recurring evaluation challenges encountered when applications must satisfy local policy and governance requirements. We present Kaleidoscope, an integrated workflow for contextual functional evaluation that links persona-based test generation, contextualized rubrics, and human review for reliability-gated automated scoring. Generated test cases are scored against application-specific rubrics; human annotations provide reviewable labels; and LLM judges automate scoring only when their agreement with those labels meets a configured threshold. Kaleidoscope is therefore a practical, inspectable, iterative workflow for product teams. We report early evidence from a three-week pilot across four organizational use cases and custom-rubric judge experiments on 108 annotated Q\&A pairs spanning four domains and 14 evaluation dimensions. The results highlight useful features for end-to-end reliable, automated scoring.

AI.tldr: Kaleidoscope is an integrated workflow for contextual functional evaluation that combines persona-based test generation, contextualized rubrics, and human review with reliability-gated automated scoring, tested across four organizational use cases and 108 annotated Q&A pairs.
AI.motivation: Public benchmarks rarely match real-world AI application contexts, and human review is difficult to scale, creating a deployment bottleneck for AI systems that must satisfy local policy and governance requirements.
AI.method: Kaleidoscope links persona-based test generation, contextualized rubrics, and human review with reliability-gated automated scoring—LLM judges score only when their agreement with human annotations meets a configured threshold.
AI.result: Early evidence from a three-week pilot across four use cases and experiments on 108 annotated Q&A pairs across 4 domains and 14 evaluation dimensions highlights useful features for end-to-end reliable, automated scoring.
AI.conclusion: Kaleidoscope offers a practical, inspectable, iterative workflow for product teams to conduct contextual functional evaluations that satisfy local policy and governance requirements.

title: TanGO: Training-Free 3D Editing via Tangent-Space Guidance and Optimization
summary: While recent flow-matching 3D generative models (e.g., VecSet) adopt structured representations, their tokens share global context, causing conventional training-free editing to suffer from semantic artifacts such as collapsed preserved regions or incomplete transformations. To address this, we propose TanGO, a training-free framework that enables adaptive per-token steering in the tangent space of generative dynamics. To realize this selective control, we formulate a one-step optimal control rule and determine the strength of each token's control signal using a von Mises-Fisher inspired directional discrepancy derived from the source and target velocity fields. Experiments show that TanGO substantially reduces structural artifacts and achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing 3D editing baselines. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/siw00-lim/TanGO.

AI.tldr: TanGO is a training-free framework for 3D generative model editing that enables adaptive per-token steering in tangent space, using von Mises-Fisher directional discrepancy to control each token's strength, achieving state-of-the-art editing results.
AI.motivation: Existing flow-matching 3D generative models with structured representations suffer from semantic artifacts (collapsed preserved regions or incomplete transformations) during training-free editing because their tokens share global context, making selective control difficult.
AI.method: TanGO formulates a one-step optimal control rule for adaptive per-token steering in the tangent space of generative dynamics, and determines each token's control strength using a von Mises-Fisher inspired directional discrepancy derived from source and target velocity fields.
AI.result: TanGO substantially reduces structural artifacts and achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing 3D editing baselines.
AI.conclusion: Training-free per-token adaptive steering in tangent space is an effective approach for 3D editing, enabling precise local control without additional training.

title: Counterfactuals for Feature-Weighted Clustering
summary: Counterfactual explanations provide local, interpretable insight by identifying changes to an input that would alter its assigned outcome. Although well established in supervised learning, their extension to clustering is less direct, since cluster assignments are unlabeled and governed by the geometry of the partition. This paper introduces VoICE, a Voronoi-Induced Counterfactual Explainability framework for feature-weighted $k$-means clustering. Rather than treating cluster change as a crossing of a single pairwise centroid boundary, VoICE formulates counterfactual generation as projection onto the full weighted Voronoi region of a target cluster, incorporating feature weights directly into both the clustering geometry and the counterfactual objective to yield least-cost and parsimonious explanations under actionability constraints. Target regions are further intersected with data-derived bounds and homothetically contracted towards their centroids, limiting extrapolation and boundary sensitivity. VoICE consistently produces valid target-cluster membership, across several benchmark datasets, where the leading pairwise baseline does not.

AI.tldr: VoICE is a framework for generating counterfactual explanations in weighted k-means clustering by projecting inputs onto Voronoi regions of target clusters, ensuring valid membership while respecting actionability constraints.
AI.motivation: Counterfactual explanations are well-established in supervised learning but lack effective counterparts in clustering due to unlabeled assignments and geometric complexity. Existing pairwise centroid-boundary methods often fail to produce valid target-cluster membership.
AI.method: VoICE formulates counterfactual generation as projection onto the full weighted Voronoi region of a target cluster, incorporates feature weights into clustering geometry and the objective, intersects target regions with data-derived bounds, and applies homothetic contraction toward centroids to limit extrapolation and boundary sensitivity.
AI.result: VoICE consistently produces valid target-cluster membership across several benchmark datasets, outperforming the leading pairwise baseline which fails to guarantee validity.
AI.conclusion: VoICE offers a principled and effective approach to counterfactual explanations in weighted k-means clustering, achieving both validity and parsimony under actionability constraints.

title: Stop Thinking, Start Looking: Efficient Post-Training for Multimodal Document Question Answering via Reasoning-Free Alignment
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026, Workshop on Efficient Multimodal Question Answering (EMM-QA)
summary: Efficient multimodal document question answering with explicit visual grounding, locating the precise document region that supports each answer remains an open challenge. Current approaches bifurcate into Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), which requires large annotated datasets and reaches optimization plateaus, and reasoning-centric Reinforcement Learning (RL), which depends on verbose intermediate traces that inflate inference token cost without clear benefit. We introduce Perception-RFT, a training framework that applies Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to multimodal document QA, bypassing intermediate reasoning tokens to directly align visual features with structured grounding outputs. To rigorously evaluate the necessity of reasoning, we construct a reasoning variant under identical reward settings. We find that reasoning-enabled models suppress their reasoning traces during training, converging to direct perception-based policies at the 4B parameter scale, reducing per-query inference token length by more than 60%, while reasoning-enabled RL underperforms perception-only training. Through a fine-grained analysis of Qwen3-VL-4B optimization dynamics, we confirm that SFT saturation and cold-start RL instability established in text-domain post-training extend to multimodal, and identify a previously uncharacterized Grounding Divergence: a selective trade-off between semantic robustness and geometric precision on two out of distribution (OOD) benchmarks (4,828 samples) under joint RL optimization. We further show that an early SFT$\rightarrow$RL transition achieves comparable precision with 65% less training data.

AI.tldr: Perception-RFT applies GRPO to multimodal document QA, bypassing intermediate reasoning tokens to directly align visual features with structured grounding outputs. It shows reasoning-enabled models suppress traces during training, converging to perception-based policies at 4B scale (60% token reduction), while identifying Grounding Divergence—a trade-off between semantic robustness and geometric precision under joint RL optimization.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal document QA approaches face limitations: SFT requires large annotated datasets and hits optimization plateaus, while reasoning-centric RL inflates inference token costs without clear benefit. The authors aim to achieve efficient explicit visual grounding without expensive intermediate reasoning traces.
AI.method: Perception-RFT uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) applied to multimodal document QA, directly aligning visual features with structured grounding outputs. The authors construct a reasoning variant under identical reward settings and analyze optimization dynamics of Qwen3-VL-4B, including comparisons of SFT, perception-only RL, and reasoning-enabled RL.
AI.result: Reasoning-enabled models suppress reasoning traces during training, converging to perception-based policies at 4B scale, reducing per-query inference token length by over 60%. Reasoning-enabled RL underperforms perception-only training. A previously uncharacterized Grounding Divergence is identified: selective trade-off between semantic robustness and geometric precision on OOD benchmarks (4,828 samples). Early SFT→RL transition achieves comparable precision with 65% less training data.
AI.conclusion: Direct perception-based RL training is more effective than reasoning-centric approaches for multimodal document QA grounding. The findings extend text-domain post-training phenomena (SFT saturation, cold-start RL instability) to multimodal settings. Early transition from SFT to RL is data-efficient, and Grounding Divergence highlights a fundamental trade-off requiring further investigation.

title: Benchmarking Face Recognition without Real Faces
summary: Synthetic face datasets have become effective enough to train face recognition models with accuracy rivaling that of models trained on real photographs. This progress sidesteps the ethical and legal burdens of collecting real biometric data, yet evaluation has not kept pace. Even studies that train entirely on synthetic images still rely on real-face benchmarks to measure performance, leaving the privacy problem only half solved. We ask whether synthetic datasets can replace real benchmarks for face recognition evaluation. We test 12 synthetic datasets against 7 established real benchmarks using 24 pre-trained models that span both convolutional and transformer architectures. Our evaluation covers biometric verification metrics, similarity score distributions, cross-model ranking consistency, and the underlying distributional properties of each dataset. Benchmarking fidelity varies widely across the synthetic candidates, but the two strongest, MorphFace and Vec2Face, reproduce the relative behavior of real benchmarks and reach agreement levels that fall within the natural disagreement already observed among the real benchmarks themselves. These results establish that well-constructed synthetic datasets can support reliable comparative evaluation for face recognition, moving the field closer to a fully synthetic and privacy-preserving pipeline for both training and benchmarking.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether synthetic face datasets can replace real benchmarks for evaluating face recognition models, finding that top synthetic datasets (MorphFace, Vec2Face) achieve fidelity comparable to natural disagreement among real benchmarks, paving the way for fully privacy-preserving pipelines.
AI.motivation: To eliminate the ethical and legal burdens of collecting real biometric data by enabling fully synthetic pipelines for both training and evaluation of face recognition models.
AI.method: Tested 12 synthetic datasets against 7 real benchmarks using 24 pre-trained models (CNNs & transformers), evaluating biometric verification metrics, score distributions, cross-model ranking consistency, and distributional properties.
AI.result: Benchmarking fidelity varies widely; MorphFace and Vec2Face reproduce real benchmark behavior at agreement levels within natural disagreement among real benchmarks themselves.
AI.conclusion: Well-constructed synthetic datasets can support reliable comparative evaluation for face recognition, enabling a fully synthetic, privacy-preserving pipeline.

title: SUFLECA: Scaling Up Feature Learning for CAD-to-image Alignment
summary: CAD-to-image alignment aims to estimate an object's 9D pose (rotation, translation, and anisotropic scale) from a single RGB image, enabling applications in robotics and augmented reality. Recent zero-shot methods use visual foundation models to match image regions to CAD models, yet typically their correspondences are appearance-driven and degrade under occlusion or sim-to-real domain shift. To address these limitations, we introduce SUFLECA (Scaling Up Feature LEarning for CAD Alignment), a weakly-supervised framework for zero-shot CAD alignment with two key contributions. First, SUFLECA scales up geometry-grounded feature learning from pretrained visual representations through Normalized Object Coordinates (NOCs) supervision on 674K images spanning 12 real and synthetic datasets, learning compact geometry-aware features that generalize across domains. Second, we propose a geometrically consistent matching algorithm that establishes reliable one-to-one CAD-to-image correspondences. Together, these contributions enable accurate, sub-second alignment per object instance without iterative pose refinement. On ScanNet25k, SUFLECA achieves 33.4%/42.3% category/instance accuracy, outperforming, with a smaller computational footprint, the strongest zero-shot baseline by 10.3/12.2 percentage points and, for the first time on this benchmark, even surpassing fully supervised methods. Code is available at: https://github.com/snt-arg/SUFLECA

AI.tldr: SUFLECA is a weakly-supervised zero-shot CAD-to-image alignment framework that uses geometry-grounded feature learning (via NOCs supervision on 674K images) and geometrically consistent matching to achieve accurate 9D pose estimation, outperforming both zero-shot and fully supervised methods on ScanNet25k.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-shot CAD-to-image alignment methods rely on appearance-driven correspondences that degrade under occlusion or sim-to-real domain shift, limiting their robustness in real-world applications.
AI.method: (1) Scaling up geometry-grounded feature learning from pretrained visual representations using NOCs supervision on 674K images across 12 datasets. (2) A geometrically consistent matching algorithm for reliable one-to-one CAD-to-image correspondences.
AI.result: On ScanNet25k, SUFLECA achieves 33.4%/42.3% category/instance accuracy, outperforming the strongest zero-shot baseline by 10.3/12.2 percentage points with a smaller computational footprint, and even surpassing fully supervised methods.
AI.conclusion: SUFLECA demonstrates that weak supervision with geometry-grounded features can effectively bridge the sim-to-real gap and achieve state-of-the-art zero-shot CAD alignment, eliminating the need for iterative pose refinement.

title: What's in a Smoothness Constant? Tighter Rates for Local SGD with Bounded Second-order Heterogeneity
summary: Local SGD, also known as Federated Averaging, is a widely used distributed optimization algorithm. Although Local SGD often outperforms alternatives such as Mini-batch SGD in practice, theory still only partially explains when and why local updates help under realistic data heterogeneity. Recent work by [Patel et al., 2025] shows that a bounded second-order heterogeneity assumption captures the efficiency of Local SGD for strongly convex objectives, and conjectures that the same principle extends to the general convex setting. In this paper, we prove this conjecture by establishing an improved convergence guarantee for Local SGD on general convex objectives under bounded second-order heterogeneity. We also improve the best-known lower bounds for Local SGD in this setting, showing that our upper bounds are nearly tight. Together, these results provide a sharper, more fine-grained convergence theory for Local SGD. As a further application of our techniques, we provide a lower bound for serial SGD with replacement, showing how second-order heterogeneity captures the impact of rare high-curvature clients.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that Local SGD (Federated Averaging) achieves improved convergence guarantees for general convex objectives under bounded second-order heterogeneity, and provides nearly tight lower bounds, strengthening the theoretical understanding of when local updates help in distributed optimization.
AI.motivation: Local SGD often outperforms Mini-batch SGD in practice, but theory only partially explains why, especially under realistic data heterogeneity. Recent work conjectured that bounded second-order heterogeneity captures efficiency for general convex objectives, which this paper aims to prove.
AI.method: The authors prove the conjecture from Patel et al. [2025] by establishing improved convergence guarantees for Local SGD on general convex objectives under bounded second-order heterogeneity. They also derive improved lower bounds to show tightness.
AI.result: The paper establishes an improved convergence guarantee for Local SGD on general convex objectives, improves best-known lower bounds to show near tightness, and provides a lower bound for serial SGD with replacement.
AI.conclusion: Second-order heterogeneity captures the efficiency of Local SGD for general convex objectives, and the bounds are nearly tight. The techniques also apply to serial SGD with replacement, explaining the impact of rare high-curvature clients.

title: InCarEmo: A Multimodal Dataset for In-Cabin Emotion Recognition and Driver State Monitoring
summary: Understanding driver emotion and state is critical for the next generation of intelligent in-cabin systems that ensure safety and enhance human-vehicle interaction. However, existing public datasets for in-cabin affective computing are largely limited to visual modalities and rarely include conversational information, making it difficult to capture the linguistic and interactive cues underlying driver emotion. To address these gaps, we introduce InCarEmo, a multimodal dataset for in-cabin emotion recognition and driver state monitoring. InCarEmo integrates RGB and infrared video, in-cabin audio, and dialogue text collected from scripted in-cabin scenarios designed to simulate realistic driver behaviors, covering diverse lighting conditions and driving contexts. The dataset supports three primary tasks: 1) multimodal emotion recognition, 2) fatigue detection, and 3) distraction monitoring. In addition to the original Chinese data, we construct an auxiliary English benchmark to support preliminary cross-lingual evaluation. We provide a unified benchmark with extensive baseline results across unimodal and multimodal methods, including analyses under modality-missing and noise conditions. Experimental results demonstrate the benefits of multimodal fusion and reveal remaining challenges under real-world noise and low-light conditions. By releasing InCarEmo, we aim to establish a comprehensive foundation for robust, interpretable, and human-centric in-cabin affective understanding, promoting safer and more empathetic driver-vehicle interaction.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces InCarEmo, a multimodal in-cabin dataset combining RGB/infrared video, audio, and dialogue text for driver emotion recognition, fatigue detection, and distraction monitoring, with baselines showing multimodal fusion benefits but challenges in noise and low-light conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing in-cabin affective datasets are limited to visual modalities and lack conversational information, which hinders capturing linguistic and interactive cues underlying driver emotion for next-generation intelligent in-cabin systems.
AI.method: InCarEmo integrates RGB and infrared video, in-cabin audio, and dialogue text from scripted scenarios simulating realistic driver behaviors across diverse lighting and driving contexts. It supports three tasks: multimodal emotion recognition, fatigue detection, and distraction monitoring. An auxiliary English benchmark is also constructed for cross-lingual evaluation.
AI.result: Multimodal fusion shows performance benefits over unimodal methods; however, challenges remain under real-world noise and low-light conditions.
AI.conclusion: InCarEmo provides a comprehensive foundation for robust, interpretable, and human-centric in-cabin affective understanding, promoting safer and more empathetic driver-vehicle interaction.

title: Still image and spatial-temporal tomato data enabling detection, segmentation, tracking, and video-instance segmentation using strong and weak labels
comment: 21 pages, 2 figures, 9 tables. Two novel datasets released - link to repository in document
summary: In this manuscript we release two datasets for visual sensing of tomato plants grown in commercial-like settings and acquired using a robot. The first is BUTom21 which consists of still images and manual annotations. The second is BUTom-ST21 which consists of video-based data and semi-automated annotations through AI-based methods, referred to as pseudo-labels. In both cases, we provide pixel-level labels for the ripeness of the fruit. The aim is to provide the research community a challenging set of real-world imagery to explore methods to sense and estimate the state of tomato plants and their fruit, which is an important horticultural crop. Importantly, the spatial-temporal dataset provides individual fruit count and ripeness information enabling researchers to push the boundaries of field-based phenotyping.

AI.tldr: Two new tomato plant datasets (BUTom21 for still images with manual labels, BUTom-ST21 for video with pseudo-labels) providing pixel-level ripeness annotations for real-world, robot-acquired, commercial-like greenhouse settings to advance field-based phenotyping research.
AI.motivation: To provide the research community with challenging real-world imagery for sensing and estimating the state of tomato plants and fruit, an important horticultural crop, pushing the boundaries of field-based phenotyping.
AI.method: Two datasets: BUTom21 (still images, manual annotations) and BUTom-ST21 (video-based, semi-automated AI-based pseudo-labels), both with pixel-level ripeness labels, acquired by a robot in commercial-like settings.
