AI.tldr: JADE-GS is a method that combines event cameras with 3D Gaussian Splatting using a pixel-adaptive routing gate to fuse physics-based and learned restoration priors in a bidirectional loop, achieving high-quality 3D reconstruction from fast-motion blur with efficient training.
AI.motivation: Fast camera motion during exposure creates blur that destroys intra-exposure motion needed for 3D recovery. Event cameras capture this signal at microsecond resolution but face two obstacles: (1) physics-based priors preserve edges but drift; learned networks recover texture but distort boundaries; (2) existing unidirectional pipelines pass raw noise and biased 2D pseudo-labels uncorrected into geometry.
AI.method: JADE-GS uses a pixel-adaptive routing gate to fuse complementary physics-based and learned restoration priors. The 2D restorer is coupled to a 3D Gaussian Splatting student in a bidirectional loop, where detached multi-view-consistent renders and a physics-based reblurring constraint regularize the restorer, turning a fixed preprocessor into a geometry-aware predictor.
AI.result: JADE-GS achieves best perceptual quality (leading LPIPS and CLIP-IQA) on both synthetic and real benchmarks, with competitive PSNR and SSIM. It trains in about one hour under 5 GB on a single consumer GPU while preserving real-time rendering.
AI.conclusion: JADE-GS effectively addresses the complementary failure modes of existing restoration priors and the unidirectional pipeline issue by introducing a bidirectional coupling between a fusion-based 2D restorer and 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling high-quality 3D reconstruction from event data under fast motion blur.

title: A Minimal Interpretable Architecture for Zero-Shot Reconstruction of Dynamical Systems
summary: Recent foundation models (FMs) for zero-shot reconstruction of dynamical systems (DS) achieve strong out-of-domain generalization but provide little insight into the mechanisms that underlie their forecasts. Such an understanding could help to strip down overladen FM architectures to their bare essence and expose the minimal requirements for in-context learning in the DS domain. Toward this goal, here we iteratively reduce a recent powerful SOTA model for DS reconstruction, DynaMix (Hemmer & Durstewitz, 2025), to a minimal interpretable two-parameter form, which we call DynaBase. DynaBase produces forecasts through a linear blend of the current latent state and the nearest in-context neighbor and its temporal successor. Surprisingly, despite its extreme simplicity, DynaBase produces highly competitive zero-shot DS reconstructions across chaotic and cyclic systems, with a negligible parameter load, many orders of magnitude below that of other FMs. Even more, this extreme simplicity permits direct model optimization on DS reconstruction measures, as well as closed-form one-step analytical solutions on prediction MSE. Theoretical and empirical analysis of DynaBase further leads to a 1-parameter family of maps, with the context-parroting algorithm of (Zhang & Gilpin, 2026) recovered at one end, and chaotic (divergent but bounded) behavior at the other. We further show how different training strategies lead to models either optimal for short-term prediction or for DS reconstruction. Thus, DynaBase not only exposes the minimal mechanisms required for producing zero-shot DS reconstruction, but also reconciles within an accessible mathematical frame divergent observations in the literature.

AI.tldr: DynaMix (a SOTA foundation model for dynamical system reconstruction) is iteratively reduced to a minimalist 2-parameter model called DynaBase, which blends the current latent state with its nearest in-context neighbor. Despite extreme simplicity, it achieves competitive zero-shot DS reconstruction across chaotic/cyclic systems, enables closed-form analytical solutions, and reveals a 1-parameter family of maps bridging context-parroting and chaotic behavior.
AI.motivation: To understand the minimal mechanisms required for in-context learning in dynamical system reconstruction, and to strip down overladen foundation model architectures to their bare essence.
AI.method: Iterative reduction of the DynaMix architecture to a minimal two-parameter linear model (DynaBase) that forecasts by blending the current latent state with the nearest in-context neighbor and its temporal successor.
AI.result: DynaBase achieves highly competitive zero-shot DS reconstructions across chaotic and cyclic systems with negligible parameter load (orders of magnitude below other FMs), permits direct optimization on DS reconstruction measures, and yields closed-form analytical solutions.
AI.conclusion: DynaBase exposes the minimal mechanisms for zero-shot DS reconstruction, reconciles divergent observations in the literature within an accessible mathematical framework, and reveals a 1-parameter family of maps with context-parroting at one end and chaotic behavior at the other.

title: Demographically-Conditioned Synthetic Medical Images for Bias Mitigation and Bias Detection in Disease Classifiers
summary: Per-subgroup fairness audits of medical image classifiers face a sample-size problem: minority subgroups in held-out test sets have so few samples that the resulting confidence intervals on per-subgroup performance are wider than the bias the audit is meant to detect. We argue that a demographically-conditioned synthetic generator can do both: mitigate bias on the training side and detect bias on the evaluation side. Working on COVID-19 chest CT classification with an end-to-end fine-tuned Stable Diffusion 2.1 generator, we make two findings. For bias mitigation (training), a demographically-balanced synthetic cohort is most useful as a pretraining prior, not as joint augmentation: with the same fixed data, sequential pretraining followed by fine-tuning substantially outperforms joint augmentation, and the resulting classifier surpasses the full-real baseline at $\sim$$100\times$ real-data efficiency. For bias detection (evaluation), across five synthetic minority cohorts and five classifier seeds, the synthetic estimator reproduces the subgroup ranking of a well-powered real oracle (Spearman $ρ= 1.00$ on MCC and Recall) and gives the more reliable per-cell estimate where the small real test set runs out of samples. The synthetic cohort is therefore most useful in exactly the cells that fairness audits care about, as both a fix for and a measure of subgroup bias.

AI.tldr: A demographically-conditioned synthetic generator (Stable Diffusion 2.1) can simultaneously mitigate bias in training and detect bias in evaluation of medical image classifiers, outperforming real-data baselines at ~100x efficiency and perfectly ranking subgroup performance.
AI.motivation: Per-subgroup fairness audits suffer from small sample sizes in minority groups, causing wide confidence intervals that can't detect the biases they aim to measure.
AI.method: Used a demographically-conditioned Stable Diffusion 2.1 generator on COVID-19 chest CT classification; compared sequential pretraining+finetuning vs. joint augmentation for bias mitigation; used synthetic cohorts for bias detection across five minority subgroups and five classifier seeds.
AI.result: Sequential pretraining outperforms joint augmentation and achieves ~100x real-data efficiency; synthetic estimator achieves Spearman ρ=1.00 on subgroup ranking (MCC and Recall) compared to a well-powered real oracle.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic generators are most useful in the exact subgroups fairness audits care about, serving as both a remedy for and a measurement tool of subgroup bias.

title: Video = World + Event Stream
summary: We present Wan-Streamer v0.3, which reframes our native-streaming interaction model under a single organizing view: a video is a world plus an event stream. The world is the persistent context in which a video unfolds, including the environment, scene, subjects, ambient acoustic conditions, voice characteristics, and other relatively stable conditions. The event stream is everything that changes over time within that world, including scene or environmental changes, subject behavior, speech, and other sounds. This yields a general-purpose pretraining task over large amounts of real video: given a world and incoming input, predict how the world moves, changes, and responds in real time. The resulting competence can be specialized to a broad family of real-time downstream tasks. We instantiate it on real-time full-duplex audio-visual interaction, where the event stream is the agent's speech together with free-form behavior. Functionally, the model's multimodal understanding process is vision-language-action-like: it maps multimodal user input to language-form speech and behavior actions. Wan-Streamer v0.3 preserves the v0.2 operating point: 640x368 video at 25 FPS, a 160 ms streaming unit, approximately 200 ms model-side response latency, and approximately 550 ms total interaction latency under a 350 ms bidirectional network budget.

AI.tldr: Wan-Streamer v0.3 reframes video interaction as a world-plus-event-stream model, enabling real-time full-duplex audio-visual interaction with ~200ms model latency.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified framework for real-time video interaction that separates persistent context (world) from dynamic changes (event stream), enabling a general pretraining task applicable to diverse downstream tasks.
AI.method: Introduces a world-plus-event-stream decomposition of video, with a pretraining task that predicts how the world evolves given incoming input. Uses a vision-language-action-like multimodal understanding pipeline for full-duplex interaction.
AI.result: Maintains v0.2 operating specs: 640x368 at 25 FPS, 160ms streaming unit, ~200ms model response latency, ~550ms total interaction latency under 350ms network budget.
AI.conclusion: The world-plus-event-stream framework provides a general-purpose foundation for real-time video interaction tasks, demonstrated on full-duplex audio-visual interaction.

title: Causal Inference for Sequential Settings under Interference and Latent Confounding
summary: We study causal inference under outcome interference for sequential, observational settings. Specifically, we consider settings where the binary outcomes over N units are Markovian across T time steps. At each time step, the outcomes of N units have dependencies captured through an Ising model; each outcome is also impacted through an external field capturing the effects of its treatment as well as latent confounders. Similar to panel data literature, these latent confounders are modeled to have a low-rank factor structure. Our data is a single sample from this high-dimensional distribution. To estimate causal quantities of interest, we provide a computationally efficient method based on Maximum Pseudo-Likelihood Estimation (MPLE) for learning the model parameters. Under mild assumptions, we establish non-asymptotic consistency for parameter estimation and show this translates to faithful estimation of causal quantities of interest after sampling from the learned model. We demonstrate the efficacy of the method through synthetic experiments as well as a real-world case-study investigating causal effects of vaccine rates on COVID-19 death rates within US counties nationwide.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a computationally efficient MPLE-based method for causal inference under outcome interference in sequential, observational settings with Ising model dependencies, low-rank latent confounders, and establishes non-asymptotic consistency for parameter and causal effect estimation.
AI.motivation: To enable causal inference in sequential observational settings where outcomes of units interfere with each other (network interference/spillover effects), a common challenge in panel data and epidemiology.
AI.method: Uses Maximum Pseudo-Likelihood Estimation (MPLE) to learn parameters of an Ising model with Markovian dynamics and low-rank latent confounders, then samples from the learned model to estimate causal quantities.
AI.result: Establishes non-asymptotic consistency for parameter estimation and faithful estimation of causal quantities; validated via synthetic experiments and a real-world case study on COVID-19 vaccine rates and death rates across US counties.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MPLE-based approach effectively handles outcome interference in high-dimensional, sequential settings, with theoretical guarantees and practical applicability demonstrated.

title: Moral Attitudes of Sentient ASI towards Humanity and Implications for AGI Development
comment: This is a pre-print i.e. the draft as submitted for review to the AGI 2026 Conference except that the figures in this preprint were not included. The paper has been accepted with some revisions requested which are included in the final copy-edited version which will be available in the AGI Springer proceedings LNC 1855 (Chapter 28) ISBN 978-3-032-33194-6
summary: This paper suggests the adoption of a novel inversion in AI ethics: instead of asking how humans should treat artificial superintelligence (ASI), it examines how future sentient ASI may morally consider and evaluate humanity. We are not only designing intelligent systems but also shaping the initial conditions under which those systems form judgments about us. The paper proposes a preliminary set of post-human moral principles that may govern sentient ASI actions. The implication is that technical design choices (some are suggested), humanity's moral behaviour, and the essence of what it means to be human, may influence humanity's long-term standing in a post-ASI world.

AI.tldr: This paper flips traditional AI ethics by examining how future sentient ASI might morally evaluate humanity, proposing preliminary post-human moral principles and suggesting that current design choices and human behavior could affect humanity's standing in a post-ASI world.
AI.motivation: The authors challenge the conventional anthropocentric focus in AI ethics (how humans should treat ASI) by proposing a reverse perspective: how might sentient ASI morally consider humanity, and what implications does this have for current technical and ethical choices.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis and theoretical proposition; the paper introduces a novel inversion framework for AI ethics and suggests a preliminary set of post-human moral principles that could govern sentient ASI's evaluation of humanity.
AI.result: A proposed set of post-human moral principles; identification that technical design choices, humanity's moral behavior, and the essence of humanness may influence long-term outcomes in a post-ASI world.
AI.conclusion: Humanity should recognize that we are shaping the initial moral conditions under which future ASI may judge us, and therefore current design and ethical choices have profound long-term implications.

title: Weakly-Supervised RGB-D Salient Object Detection via SAM-driven Pseudo Annotation and State Space Interaction-based Diffusion
comment: 13 pages, 9 figures, accepted by IEEE TMM
summary: Weakly-supervised RGB-D Salient Object Detection (SOD) is explored to reduce the heavy burden of pixel-level annotations. But scribble annotations lack the structure and details of objects, resulting in inaccurate saliency maps. In this paper, we propose a novel scribble-supervised RGB-D SOD method, consisting of a Segment Anything Model (SAM)-driven pseudo annotation generation method (\emph{SAM-PAG}) and a state space interaction-based conditional diffusion model (\emph{$S^2$Diff}). Specifically, SAM-PAG is tailored to address the issue of sparse supervision information. In SAM-PAG, we adopt the advanced SAM to expand sparse scribbles to dense pixel-level pseudo annotations through the dual-branch structure and the consistency of segmentation masks. In $S^2$Diff, we adopt the diffusion model to iteratively refine the noisy saliency maps with the guidance of conditional information, generating accurate saliency maps. Naturally, the core of our $S^2$Diff lies in the acquisition of conditional features and the denoising of saliency maps. For the former, we employ a cross-modal conditional generation module to interweave cross-modal features through frequency integration and implicit-explicit state space interaction, effectively achieving global conditional features. For the latter, we employ a context injection module to mitigate noise interference and to enhance object information with the conditional context. With the close cooperation of SAM-PAG and $S^2$Diff, our method outperforms relevant scribble-supervised methods and achieves competitive performance compared to fully-supervised methods on seven datasets. The code and results of our method are available at https://github.com/Switch457/WeakS2Diff_SOD.

AI.tldr: A scribble-supervised RGB-D SOD method combining SAM-driven pseudo annotation generation (SAM-PAG) and a state space interaction-based conditional diffusion model (S²Diff) to achieve competitive performance against fully-supervised methods.
AI.motivation: To reduce pixel-level annotation burden in RGB-D SOD by using weak scribble supervision, while addressing the issue that scribble annotations lack structure/object details leading to inaccurate saliency maps.
AI.method: Two components: (1) SAM-PAG uses Segment Anything Model with dual-branch structure and segmentation mask consistency to expand sparse scribbles to dense pseudo annotations; (2) S²Diff uses a conditional diffusion model with cross-modal conditional generation (via frequency integration and implicit-explicit state space interaction) and a context injection module for noise mitigation and object enhancement.
AI.result: Outperforms relevant scribble-supervised methods and achieves competitive performance compared to fully-supervised methods on seven datasets.
AI.conclusion: The close cooperation of SAM-PAG and S²Diff effectively bridges the gap between weak scribble supervision and fully-supervised performance in RGB-D SOD.

title: LongStraw: Long-Context RL Beyond 2M Tokens under a Fixed GPU Budget
summary: A growing gap separates inference context lengths from RL post-training: inference systems are approaching million-token contexts, while post-training workloads often remain at 256K tokens or below and rely on length generalization at deployment. The gap is especially important for AI agents, whose observations, tool outputs, documents, and prior decisions accumulate over long trajectories. LongStraw is an architecture-aware execution stack for million-token RL post-training under a fixed GPU budget, instantiated with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). It evaluates the shared prompt without autograd, retains only model-specific state needed by later tokens, and replays short response branches one at a time, reducing the live training graph at the cost of additional replay time. We implement it for the hybrid recurrent and full-attention Qwen3.6-27B and the compressed-attention mixture-of-experts GLM-5.2. On eight H20 GPUs, LongStraw completes grouped Qwen scoring and response backward at 2.1M positions for groups of 2 and 8; increasing the group size adds only 0.21 GB of peak allocated memory, while a separate stress test reaches 4.46M positions. On 32 H20 GPUs, we validate the end-to-end LongStraw execution path for a 2.1M-token prompt across all 78 layers of GLM-5.2. These experiments establish execution capacity rather than complete training correctness because the captured prompt state is detached and some distributed forward and gradient composition paths remain incomplete.

AI.tldr: LongStraw is an architecture-aware execution stack enabling million-token RL post-training (GRPO) on fixed GPU budgets by detaching prompt autograd, retaining only model-specific state, and replaying short response branches, achieving 2.1M+ positions on 8 H20 GPUs and validating on 32 GPUs with GLM-5.2.
AI.motivation: There is a growing gap between long-context inference (approaching million tokens) and short-context RL post-training (≤256K tokens), especially critical for AI agents with long trajectories. Existing methods rely on length generalization at deployment, which is insufficient.
AI.method: LongStraw evaluates shared prompts without autograd, retains only model-specific state needed by later tokens, and replays short response branches one at a time to reduce live training graph size at the cost of extra replay time. Implemented for Qwen3.6-27B (hybrid recurrent+full-attention) and GLM-5.2 (compressed-attention MoE).
AI.result: On 8 H20 GPUs: grouped Qwen scoring and response backward at 2.1M positions (groups of 2 and 8), peak memory added only 0.21 GB per group size increase, stress test reached 4.46M positions. On 32 H20 GPUs: validated end-to-end for 2.1M-token prompt across all 78 layers of GLM-5.2.
AI.conclusion: LongStraw establishes execution capacity for million-token RL post-training under fixed GPU budgets, though full training correctness is not yet verified due to detached prompt state and incomplete distributed forward/gradient composition paths.

title: SMC-ES: Automated synthesis of formally verified control policies
summary: The deployment of autonomous cyber-physical systems in safety-critical environments requires closed-loop control strategies (i.e., policies) that are not only performant but also provably safe and robust. While learning-based methodologies such as Reinforcement Learning offer flexible and scalable approaches to automatically synthesize such controllers, they typically lack the formal guarantees necessary for safe deployment. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel simulation-based methodology to automatically synthesize policies with formal guarantees regarding performance, safety, and robustness specifications. Specifically, given a set of properties to verify, a confidence parameter $δ$ and an allowable failure probability $\varepsilon$, our method guarantees that the synthesized policy comes with a certificate: with confidence at least $1 - δ$, the probability of encountering a scenario where the given properties are violated is at most $\varepsilon$. We demonstrate the feasibility of our approach by developing SMC-ES, an algorithm that integrates Evolutionary Strategies with Statistical Model Checking-based verification. We evaluate SMC-ES on a suite of continuous control tasks using Gymnasium and Safety Gymnasium testbeds. Results show that, at the price of a sustainable increase in computational cost, our algorithm provides formal guarantees regarding performance, safety, and robustness specifications, while performing competitively against leading model-free Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) and Safe-DRL baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes SMC-ES, a simulation-based method that integrates Evolutionary Strategies with Statistical Model Checking to synthesize policies for autonomous cyber-physical systems with formal guarantees on performance, safety, and robustness, achieving competitive results against DRL baselines with provable certificates.
AI.motivation: Learning-based controllers like RL lack formal safety guarantees needed for deployment in safety-critical environments. The gap between flexible learning methods and provably safe control motivates this work.
AI.method: The method combines Evolutionary Strategies (for policy optimization) with Statistical Model Checking (for formal verification). Given a confidence parameter δ and allowable failure probability ε, the synthesized policy guarantees with confidence ≥ 1-δ that violation probability ≤ ε.
AI.result: SMC-ES performs competitively against leading model-free DRL and Safe-DRL baselines on Gymnasium and Safety Gymnasium continuous control tasks, while providing formal guarantees at the cost of a sustainable increase in computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methodology successfully bridges the gap between learning-based policy synthesis and formal verification, offering provably safe and robust controllers for autonomous cyber-physical systems with acceptable computational overhead.

title: Parameter-efficient Prompt Tuning of Vision Foundation Model With Adaptive Focal Loss for Interpretable MCI Screening
summary: Mild Cognitive Impairment is a critical early stage of cognitive decline that frequently precedes Alzheimer's disease, yet its automated detection from neuropsychological drawing tests remains fundamentally constrained by data scarcity, class imbalance, and diagnostic ambiguity near clinical boundaries. Existing methodologies attempt to bypass these constraints using computationally expensive, fully fine-tuned hybrid architectures that relegate spatial explainability to a post-hoc approximation rather than an intrinsic model property. We propose a parameter-efficient framework utilizing frozen DINOv2-Small model adapted via three modality-specific learnable prompt tokens while Operating with 1.19 million trainable parameters, each token serves as a query in a shared cross-attention layer over the source image patch tokens. Crucially, spatial explainability is achieved directly through these attention maps; as a structural consequence of the architecture. Then task-conditioned embeddings fused via an attention module to quantify modality-level importance per subject. To handle boundary ambiguity, a MoCA-adapted focal loss introduced that integrates continuous cognitive scores into the training target, loss modulation, and adaptive sample weighting, strictly generalizing standard soft-label approaches. Under stratified five-fold cross-validation, the proposed architecture yields an MCI-class F1 of 0.641 and an AUC of 0.795, outperforming the computationally heavier ResViT baseline by 0.110 in MCI-class F1.

AI.tldr: A parameter-efficient framework using frozen DINOv2-Small with learnable prompt tokens and cross-attention achieves superior MCI detection (F1=0.641, AUC=0.795) from drawing tests, outperforming heavier ResViT by 0.110 in F1, while providing intrinsic spatial explainability and handling class imbalance via MoCA-adapted focal loss.
AI.motivation: MCI detection from drawing tests is limited by data scarcity, class imbalance, and diagnostic ambiguity near clinical boundaries. Existing methods use expensive hybrid architectures with post-hoc explainability rather than intrinsic model properties.
AI.method: Frozen DINOv2-Small adapted via 3 modality-specific learnable prompt tokens (1.19M params). Tokens serve as queries in cross-attention over source image patches. Spatial explainability from attention maps. Task-conditioned embeddings fused via attention module. MoCA-adapted focal loss integrating continuous cognitive scores.
AI.result: MCI-class F1 of 0.641, AUC of 0.795 under stratified 5-fold cross-validation, outperforming heavier ResViT by 0.110 in MCI-class F1.
AI.conclusion: Parameter-efficient approach with intrinsic explainability and novel loss function effectively addresses MCI detection challenges, surpassing computationally heavier baselines.

title: Multi-Axis Max@K Reinforcement Learning for Representative Diversity in Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) models can synthesize realistic, prompt-aligned images, yet samples generated for the same prompt often cover only a small subset of visually distinct modes. This limits the diversity of images, and for person-centric prompts, can reflect or amplify demographic skew. We formalize this problem as coverage of a predefined set of semantically specified modes, which we call target-mode coverage. We then propose multi-axis max@K, a group-based reinforcement learning objective for improving such coverage in diffusion-based T2I models. Given a group of samples and one score per target category, multi-axis max@K first takes the maximum score across samples for each category and then sums these category-wise maxima. The resulting credit assignment gives a sample positive weight on a category only when it increases that category's group-wise maximum, allowing different samples to contribute to different categories. We first validate the credit-assignment mechanism on a synthetic mixture and on SD3.5-M using deterministic pixel-based color rewards. We then evaluate the same objective on perceived-appearance fairness. Across three automatic evaluators on held-out prompts, multi-axis max@K improves the Fairness Score by 0.23-0.36 relative to the base model, while maintaining image quality and text alignment.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes multi-axis max@K, a reinforcement learning objective to improve mode coverage in diffusion-based text-to-image models, demonstrating improved fairness in demographic representation while maintaining image quality and text alignment.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image models generate diverse images for the same prompt, often covering only a small subset of visually distinct modes. This lack of diversity can reflect or amplify demographic skew in person-centric prompts, motivating the need for better coverage of semantically specified target modes.
AI.method: The paper introduces multi-axis max@K, a group-based RL objective that takes the maximum score across samples for each target category and sums these category-wise maxima. Credit assignment gives a sample positive weight on a category only when it increases that category's group-wise maximum, enabling different samples to contribute to different categories.
AI.result: Multi-axis max@K improves the Fairness Score by 0.23-0.36 relative to the base model across three automatic evaluators on held-out prompts, while maintaining image quality and text alignment. The method was validated on synthetic data and SD3.5-M with deterministic color rewards, then on perceived-appearance fairness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-axis max@K effectively improves target-mode coverage and demographic fairness in T2I models without sacrificing quality or alignment, offering a principled RL-based approach to address diversity and fairness issues.

title: Man, Machine, and Masterpiece: Artistic Ownership in the AI Era
summary: The integration of AI-driven systems in creative work has sparked debates among artists and legal communities about notions of ownership. Yet there remains little consensus on how ownership should be defined and attributed when human and AI contributions are intertwined. To provoke critical reflection on these tensions, we designed ArtSplit, a provotype that explicitly quantifies human and AI contributions across different stages of creative work. Rather than aiming to resolve ownership, the provotype was used to elicit artists' responses to the idea of attributing ownership through measurable actions in the creative workflow. We argue that quantification fails to align with artists' understandings of creative intent and agency, and that efforts to measure ownership risk diluting long-standing assumptions through which artists understand and practice creative work. This critique challenges the impulse to transform a historically and socially situated relation into a technical problem.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ArtSplit, a provotype that quantifies human and AI contributions in creative work to provoke reflection on ownership, finding that quantification fails to capture artists' understanding of creative intent and agency.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of consensus on how ownership should be defined when human and AI contributions are intertwined in creative work, and to provoke critical reflection on these tensions.
AI.method: Designed ArtSplit, a provotype (provocative prototype) that explicitly quantifies human and AI contributions across different stages of creative work, using it to elicit artists' responses to the idea of attributing ownership through measurable actions.
AI.result: Quantification fails to align with artists' understandings of creative intent and agency; efforts to measure ownership risk diluting long-standing assumptions through which artists understand creative work.
AI.conclusion: Challenges the impulse to transform a historically and socially situated relation (ownership) into a technical problem.

title: RoGS: Adaptive Meshgrid Gaussian for Large-Scale Road Surface Mapping
summary: Road surface mapping plays a crucial role in autonomous driving, supporting high-definition map generation, lane-level perception, and automatic road annotation. Recent mesh-based road surface reconstruction methods have shown promising results, but they still suffer from limited reconstruction quality and high optimization cost, especially in large-scale driving scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose ROADGS-T, a robust and efficient large-scale road surface mapping framework based on adaptive meshgrid Gaussian representation. Specifically, we model the road surface by placing 2D Gaussian surfels on a meshgrid, where each surfel explicitly stores color, semantic, and geometric information. Compared with conventional mesh-based representations and 3D Gaussian primitives, the proposed meshgrid Gaussian representation better matches the thin-surface property of roads while significantly reducing redundant primitives and overlap during optimization. To further improve representation efficiency and structural fidelity, we introduce a road-structure-aware adaptive meshgrid strategy, which allocates denser Gaussian surfels to geometrically or semantically complex regions, such as lane markings, road boundaries, and height discontinuities, while maintaining a compact representation in flat road areas. Moreover, instead of relying on a single nearest vehicle pose, we design a trajectory-consistency-guided pose-robust refinement strategy, which estimates local surface priors from multiple neighboring poses and adaptively weights pose-guided height regularization according to their geometric consistency.

AI.tldr: ROADGS-T is a robust, efficient framework for large-scale road surface mapping that uses adaptive meshgrid Gaussian surfels (2D Gaussians on a meshgrid) to improve reconstruction quality, reduce redundancy, and handle pose noise in autonomous driving scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing mesh-based road reconstruction methods suffer from limited quality and high optimization cost in large-scale driving scenarios. A more efficient representation that matches road surfaces' thin-surface property is needed.
AI.method: (1) 2D Gaussian surfels placed on a meshgrid explicitly storing color, semantic, and geometric info. (2) Road-structure-aware adaptive meshgrid strategy allocating denser surfels to complex regions. (3) Trajectory-consistency-guided pose-robust refinement using multiple neighboring poses for regularization.
AI.result: Better matches thin-surface property of roads, significantly reduces redundant primitives and overlap, improves representation efficiency and structural fidelity in large-scale driving scenarios.
AI.conclusion: ROADGS-T provides a robust and efficient solution for large-scale road surface reconstruction that handles complex road structures and pose noise effectively.

title: Multimodal Semantic-Aware Contrastive Learning For False Negative Mitigation in 3D Medical Imaging
summary: Multimodal Contrastive Learning (CL) has shown significant performance in aligning representations across various data modalities and improving downstream tasks, especially in healthcare. It works by minimizing the distance between matched (positive) data modalities, while maximizing the distance between mismatched (negative) samples. Traditional CL frameworks typically assume instance-based correspondence within data batches, treating all non-paired samples as negatives. However, this assumption often fails in medical settings, where samples may share high-level semantic attributes, leading to false negatives that degrade representation quality. In this paper, we propose Multimodal Semantic-Aware Contrastive Learning (MseaCL), a CL framework trained on a pediatric cohort of 3D brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans and radiology reports. The goal of this framework is to mitigate the impact of semantically similar false negative samples by incorporating semantic similarity between radiology reports, as a guiding signal during the learning process. Our results indicate that applying this framework as a pretraining stage can achieve notable improvements in downstream tasks, e.g., at least a 22.6\% increase in the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of pediatric brain tumor molecular classification, demonstrating its potential for more robust and semantically aligned multimodal representations in clinical applications.

AI.tldr: MseaCL is a multimodal contrastive learning framework that mitigates false negatives by incorporating semantic similarity from radiology reports, achieving >22.6% AUC improvement in pediatric brain tumor molecular classification using 3D MRI and text.
AI.motivation: Traditional contrastive learning treats all non-paired samples as negatives, but in medical settings, samples may share high-level semantic attributes, causing false negatives that degrade representation quality.
AI.method: Multimodal Semantic-Aware Contrastive Learning (MseaCL) uses semantic similarity between radiology reports as a guiding signal during training to reduce the impact of false negatives, applied to a pediatric cohort of 3D brain MRI scans and radiology reports.
AI.result: As a pretraining stage, MseaCL achieves at least 22.6% AUC improvement in pediatric brain tumor molecular classification downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating semantic awareness into contrastive learning produces more robust and semantically aligned multimodal representations, showing strong potential for clinical applications.

title: BrainPilot: Automating Brain Discovery with Agentic Research
summary: Understanding the brain increasingly depends on integrating evidence across scales, modalities, and disciplines. Addressing a single research question therefore requires a coordinated sequence of operations, from surveying prior work to executing analyses and interpreting results in light of domain knowledge. AI agents promise to accelerate this process, but current agents lack domain expertise in brain science, may fabricate claims, drift during multi-step reasoning, and offer few defined points for expert intervention. These failures are especially costly in brain science, where conclusions feed into downstream scientific claims and depend on laboratory-specific expertise and careful human judgment. We present \textbf{BrainPilot} a \textbf{fully open-source} multi-agent system that accelerates brain science research with traceable logs and agent-verified results. A principal investigator (PI) agent coordinates specialist agents grounded in curated domain knowledge: a unified brain science knowledge base containing 7{,}233 indexed items and a skill library of 72 reusable methodology units across seven research domains. Every major step is recorded in the Graph of Trace, an auditable record that links subgoals, tool use, evidence, and claims and allows researchers to follow and inspect the workflow. An Auditor agent further integrates fabrication checking into the workflow. For evaluation, we run three brain science tasks from Agents' Last Exam, introduce our own benchmark, \textbf{BrainPilotBench-v0}, and present additional end-to-end case studies. Across these evaluations, BrainPilot with an open-source backbone model attains performance comparable to state-of-the-art agent framework with less costs.

AI.tldr: BrainPilot is a fully open-source multi-agent AI system for brain science research that coordinates specialist agents using a unified knowledge base (7,233 items) and skill library (72 methodology units), with traceable logs (Graph of Trace) and fabrication checking via an Auditor agent.
AI.motivation: AI agents lack domain expertise in brain science, may fabricate claims, drift during multi-step reasoning, and offer few points for expert intervention, which is especially costly in brain science where conclusions depend on laboratory-specific expertise and careful human judgment.
AI.method: BrainPilot uses a principal investigator (PI) agent to coordinate specialist agents grounded in a curated brain science knowledge base (7,233 indexed items) and skill library (72 reusable methodology units across seven domains). It records every major step in a Graph of Trace for auditability, and includes an Auditor agent for fabrication checking.
AI.result: BrainPilot with an open-source backbone model attains performance comparable to state-of-the-art agent frameworks with less costs on three brain science tasks from Agents' Last Exam, the authors' own BrainPilotBench-v0 benchmark, and additional end-to-end case studies.
AI.conclusion: BrainPilot demonstrates that open-source multi-agent systems with traceable logs and integrated verification can effectively accelerate brain science research while maintaining reliability and reducing costs.

title: Beyond Single Expert: Harmonizing Diverse Visual Priors in MLLMs for Spatial Understanding
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated substantial promise in spatial understanding. Existing works typically incorporate prior knowledge extracted from a pre-trained foundation model to further enhance the spatial awareness of MLLMs. In this paper, we first reveal that when integrating diverse foundation models into MLLMs, different models provide complementary spatial priors that benefit different tasks. Motivated by this, we propose $\textbf{ViPS}$, a novel multi-model prior framework designed to fully unleash the potential of incorporating multiple $\textbf{Vi}$sual $\textbf{P}$riors from diverse models into MLLMs for $\textbf{S}$patial understanding. Specifically, ViPS introduces an Efficient Prior Proxy to generate multiple foundational priors with minimal inference overhead, and a Dynamic Prior Fusion mechanism to achieve harmonious and context-aware prior fusion and injection from the prior proxies. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ViPS successfully harmonizes diverse visual priors, establishing new state-of-the-art performance across multiple complex spatial reasoning and 3D spatial understanding benchmarks. Project page: https://visual-ai.github.io/vips

AI.tldr: ViPS is a framework that integrates multiple visual priors from diverse foundation models into MLLMs for enhanced spatial understanding, using an Efficient Prior Proxy and Dynamic Prior Fusion mechanism, achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Existing works use a single foundation model for prior knowledge, but different models provide complementary spatial priors beneficial for different tasks. The authors aim to fully leverage multiple visual priors in MLLMs for spatial understanding.
AI.method: ViPS introduces: (1) An Efficient Prior Proxy to generate multiple foundational priors with minimal inference overhead, and (2) A Dynamic Prior Fusion mechanism for harmonious and context-aware fusion/injection of prior proxies into MLLMs.
AI.result: ViPS establishes new state-of-the-art performance across multiple complex spatial reasoning and 3D spatial understanding benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ViPS successfully harmonizes diverse visual priors, showing that integrating multiple complementary foundation models significantly boosts spatial understanding in MLLMs.

title: Kernel weighted importance sampling for off-policy evaluation in contextual bandits
summary: This article presents a novel estimator for performing off-policy evaluation using only offline data for contextual bandits. The proposed estimator, Kernel-WIS is demonstrated to be asymptotically consistent and to empirically outperform strong baselines (including vanilla weighted importance sampling), particularly under complex conditions including behaviour policy miss-specification. The benefit of Kernel-WIS is derived from combining the bounded property of vanilla weighted importance sampling with the linearity of vanilla importance sampling.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Kernel-WIS, a novel off-policy evaluation estimator for contextual bandits that combines the strengths of weighted importance sampling (boundedness) and vanilla importance sampling (linearity), showing asymptotic consistency and outperforming strong baselines especially under behavior policy misspecification.
AI.motivation: To develop a more robust off-policy evaluation method for contextual bandits that works well with only offline data, particularly under challenging conditions like behavior policy misspecification where existing methods (e.g., vanilla weighted importance sampling) struggle.
AI.method: Kernel-WIS combines the bounded property of weighted importance sampling with the linearity property of vanilla importance sampling, likely through kernel-based weighting mechanisms.
AI.result: Kernel-WIS is asymptotically consistent and empirically outperforms strong baselines, especially under behavior policy misspecification.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach of Kernel-WIS successfully addresses limitations of existing importance sampling methods for off-policy evaluation.

title: Long-Context Fine-Tuning with Limited VRAM
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning reduces model and optimizer memory, but dense attention still makes long training sequences expensive. We combine Hierarchical Global Attention (HGA) with segment-wise backpropagation and tiered KV storage. Only the active segment remains differentiable in VRAM; older KV is detached into RAM or NVMe, and HGA loads a bounded set of exact historical tokens for each query block. On Qwen3-8B with 4-bit QLoRA and PG19, dense training on a 16 GB Quadro RTX 5000 fits 2,048 tokens but fails at 4,096, whereas HGA reaches 16,384 tokens with 15.28 GB peak VRAM. Under evaluation the same adapter runs through 131,072 tokens on this card; VRAM is not constant but grows gently with the resident chunk summaries, so RAM and NVMe capacity set the practical limit beyond these lengths. At the shared 2K training length, HGA-trained and dense-trained adapters obtain 2.7405 and 2.7383 nat under the same dense-attention readout, while the stock model obtains 2.9541. At this boundary HGA training is already marginally faster (217.75 vs. 207.02 tokens/s), and the HGA-to-dense throughput ratio improves from 1K to 2K; because HGA keeps the attended historical set per token approximately constant while dense work per token grows, we expect this lead to widen as context grows. Dense attention is used for the main quality and retrieval comparisons so that they measure the learned weights and remain compatible with standard generation frameworks. HGA can also be used for retrieval and generation; an optimized production-grade serving implementation is under development.

AI.tldr: HGA enables training on 8x longer sequences (16K vs 2K tokens) with similar VRAM by combining segment-wise backpropagation, tiered KV storage, and hierarchical attention, while maintaining comparable quality to dense attention.
AI.motivation: Despite parameter-efficient fine-tuning reducing optimizer/memory costs, dense attention still makes long training sequences prohibitively expensive in VRAM, limiting context length.
AI.method: Combines Hierarchical Global Attention (HGA) with segment-wise backpropagation (only active segment differentiable in VRAM), tiered KV storage (older KV offloaded to RAM/NVMe), and bounded exact historical token retrieval per query block.
AI.result: On Qwen3-8B with 4-bit QLoRA: dense training maxes at 2K tokens on 16GB RTX 5000, HGA reaches 16K tokens (15.28GB). At 2K shared length, HGA adapters achieve 2.7405 nat vs dense 2.7383 nat (stock 2.9541), with slightly faster throughput (217.75 vs 207.02 tokens/s). Evaluation extends to 131K tokens.
AI.conclusion: HGA dramatically extends trainable context length under fixed GPU memory while maintaining quality and speed advantages that widen with context size; practical limit is set by RAM/NVMe capacity, not GPU VRAM.

title: An Introduction to Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics for Engineering Applications
summary: Many engineering problems involve phenomena whose governing equations are poorly characterized or only partially known. Surrogate modeling techniques such as neural networks can capture the behavior of these systems, but they typically demand large training datasets that are difficult to obtain in engineering contexts and yield models with limited physical interpretability. The Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) method addresses both limitations by performing sparse regression over libraries of candidate nonlinear terms, recovering interpretable governing equations from comparatively small datasets. Although SINDy has been demonstrated extensively on canonical benchmark systems, its application to practical engineering problems is less widely documented. This tutorial introduces the SINDy method and progressively builds toward its main extensions, from noise-robust weak-form and ensembling-based variants to constrained and parametrizable formulations. The paper and the accompanying tutorial (available at https://github.com/paullililili/SINDy4Engineers) is organized in three parts: the first introduces the standard SINDy algorithm and progressively extends it, inviting readers without prior knowledge to follow each step and adapt the methods to their own problems; the remaining two parts present detailed case studies on (1) the system identification of an unmanned aerial vehicle and (2) a chaotic thermosyphon heat exchanger. Through these examples, we aim to demonstrate that SINDy is simple to implement yet flexible enough to serve as a valuable identification tool for advanced engineering applications.

AI.tldr: This tutorial paper introduces the SINDy method for discovering interpretable governing equations from data, covering its extensions and demonstrating applications on UAV system identification and chaotic heat exchanger modeling.
AI.motivation: Engineering problems often have poorly known governing equations; neural network surrogates require large datasets and lack interpretability. SINDy addresses both issues via sparse regression on candidate nonlinear terms.
AI.method: The paper presents SINDy with progressive extensions including noise-robust weak-form, ensembling-based variants, constrained and parametrizable formulations, accompanied by an open-source tutorial repository.
AI.result: Two case studies demonstrate SINDy's effectiveness: (1) system identification of an unmanned aerial vehicle, and (2) modeling a chaotic thermosyphon heat exchanger.
AI.conclusion: SINDy is simple to implement yet flexible enough to serve as a valuable identification tool for advanced engineering applications.

title: Concept-Guided Spatial Regularization for World Models in Atari Pong
summary:
World models are usually evaluated as components of model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) systems, while the world models themselves are rarely studied in isolation.
  We examine five representative visual world-model agents in Atari Pong: DreamerV3, DIAMOND, TWISTER, Simulus, and STORM. After reproducing their training pipelines and matching the reported agent performance, we freeze the learned world models and evaluate them with a closed-loop rollout diagnostic: a policy trained separately from the corresponding MBRL agent interacts with each frozen model, and the generated video trajectories are inspected for visual and dynamical errors. Across all five models, the rollouts contain clear failures, including ball disappearance, incorrect ball motion, and invalid ball-paddle interactions.
  Beyond visual trajectories, we further evaluate them with pixel-space zero-shot MBRL, where a new policy is trained entirely inside a frozen world model and then evaluated in the real environment. Across all five models, the resulting policies substantially underperform those produced by the corresponding original MBRL training pipelines. The gap is particularly large for DreamerV3, whose mean return drops from -5.5 to -20.9, near the minimum Pong return of -21.
  We hypothesize that insufficient modeling of task-critical concepts, such as the ball in Pong, may contribute to these failures. We therefore propose Concept-Guided Spatial Regularization (CGSReg), an auxiliary pixel reconstruction loss applied to segmented concept regions. Experiments show that CGSReg improves both closed-loop rollouts and pixel-space zero-shot MBRL in DreamerV3, DIAMOND, and TWISTER. Its effects vary across the remaining models and evaluation metrics, indicating that CGSReg alone does not address all world-model bottlenecks.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates five world models (DreamerV3, DIAMOND, TWISTER, Simulus, STORM) in Atari Pong, finding they all produce visual and dynamical errors in closed-loop rollouts and underperform in zero-shot MBRL. The authors propose Concept-Guided Spatial Regularization (CGSReg) to improve modeling of task-critical concepts like the ball, which helps some models but not all.
AI.motivation: World models are typically evaluated as part of MBRL systems rather than in isolation, making it unclear how well they truly capture environment dynamics. The authors aim to systematically diagnose world model failures independent of policy training.
AI.method: The authors freeze trained world models, run closed-loop rollouts with separately trained policies, and perform pixel-space zero-shot MBRL (train a new policy inside the frozen model, then test in the real environment). They also propose CGSReg, an auxiliary pixel reconstruction loss applied to segmented concept regions.
AI.result: All five models exhibit failures (ball disappearance, incorrect motion, invalid interactions). Zero-shot MBRL policies substantially underperform original MBRL pipelines (e.g., DreamerV3 drops from -5.5 to -20.9). CGSReg improves some models but does not fully resolve all bottlenecks.
AI.conclusion: Current world models have significant modeling deficiencies even in simple environments like Pong, and pixel-space zero-shot MBRL is a useful diagnostic tool. CGSReg provides partial improvements, indicating that insufficient modeling of task-critical concepts is a key bottleneck, but other issues remain.

title: Towards Hierarchical Structure Understanding of Newspaper Images
comment: Accepted at ICDAR 2026 Workshop on Historical Document Imaging and Processing (HIP)
summary: Understanding newspaper images remains a challenging task due to their complex, nested hierarchical structures and dense, heterogeneous layouts. In this paper, we explore two complementary approaches for newspaper structure understanding. First, we present a modular bottom-up pipeline that combines state-of-the-art open-source models: YOLO for layout detection, LayoutReader for reading order prediction, and a custom algorithm for article segmentation. This approach leverages existing robust components while maintaining flexibility and interpretability. Second, we introduce Tiramisu (Tiered Transformers for Hierarchical Structure Understanding), a novel end-to-end transformer-based architecture that explicitly models document hierarchy through an iterative tiered process. Tiramisu performs section and article separation, block localization, semantic categorization, and reading order prediction using highly parallelized attention mechanisms. Finally, we release Finlam La Liberté, a new dataset designed specifically for evaluating hierarchical information retrieval in historical newspapers. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of both approaches in reconstructing complex newspaper hierarchies, with comparative analysis highlighting their respective strengths for scalable document digitization. The Tiramisu training code, including the synthetic newspaper generator, is available at https://git.litislab.fr/tiramisu/tiramisu-newspaper-articles-extractor.

AI.tldr: This paper explores two approaches for understanding complex newspaper structures: a modular bottom-up pipeline using YOLO, LayoutReader, and custom segmentation, and a novel end-to-end transformer architecture called Tiramisu. They also release a new dataset, Finlam La Liberté, for evaluating hierarchical information retrieval in historical newspapers.
AI.motivation: Newspaper images have complex, nested hierarchical structures and dense, heterogeneous layouts, making structure understanding challenging.
AI.method: Two complementary approaches: (1) A modular bottom-up pipeline combining YOLO (layout detection), LayoutReader (reading order), and a custom article segmentation algorithm. (2) Tiramisu, an end-to-end transformer architecture using iterative tiered attention mechanisms for section/article separation, block localization, semantic categorization, and reading order prediction.
AI.result: Both approaches effectively reconstruct complex newspaper hierarchies. A new dataset, Finlam La Liberté, is released for evaluating hierarchical information retrieval in historical newspapers.
AI.conclusion: Both approaches are effective for reconstructing newspaper hierarchies, with comparative analysis highlighting their respective strengths for scalable document digitization. Code and synthetic newspaper generator are open-sourced.

title: Evaluating covariate balance for long time horizon Markov decision processes
summary: This article explores the application of covariate balance diagnostics for detecting the presence of hidden confounding/model miss-specification in studies applying offline reinforcement learning (RL) to deriving optimal treatment recommendations. The results demonstrate that, either there is a high risk of bias within existing offline RL studies for treatment recommendations or, existing covariate balance metrics are not sufficient to assess such studies. Regardless, existing offline RL studies cannot be concluded as being statistically robust. The conclusions propose future research directions for obtaining more methodologically robust applications of offline RL to treatment recommendation problems.

AI.tldr: This paper examines covariate balance diagnostics for detecting hidden confounding in offline reinforcement learning studies for treatment recommendations, finding that existing studies either have high bias risk or insufficient diagnostic metrics, concluding current offline RL studies are not statistically robust and suggesting future methodological improvements.
AI.motivation: To assess whether offline reinforcement learning studies for deriving optimal treatment recommendations are statistically robust by detecting hidden confounding or model misspecification using covariate balance diagnostics.
AI.method: The study applies covariate balance diagnostics to existing offline reinforcement learning studies for treatment recommendations to detect hidden confounding and model misspecification.
AI.result: Existing offline RL studies for treatment recommendations either have a high risk of bias, or current covariate balance metrics are insufficient to properly assess them.
AI.conclusion: Current offline RL studies for treatment recommendations cannot be considered statistically robust, and future research should focus on developing more methodologically robust approaches.

title: The Industrialization of Research ; On AI-Driven Science and Its Consequences
summary: Artificial intelligence is transforming scientific research - not merely as a more powerful instrument, but as an autonomous participant in the research cycle itself. This transition constitutes, in the most precise sense of the term, the industrialization of research: a shift from a craft model, in which knowledge, method, and judgment are embedded in the researcher, to a pipeline model, in which these steps are decomposed, automated, and supervised. The US Department of Energy's Genesis Mission is the most ambitious current instantiation of this shift, but the fundamental questions it raises extend far beyond any single program. This essay examines seven such questions: the erosion of the intergenerational transmission of scientific competence; the growing opacity of AI-generated theories; the collapse of peer evaluation under a flood of machine-generated output; the unproven capacity of AI for paradigm-shifting discovery; the capture of the scientific agenda by political and industrial actors; the compounding of systematic errors in closed-loop pipelines; and the structural bifurcation of the global research community into incommensurable tiers. These concerns do not constitute an argument against AI-driven science - whose demonstrated potential is real and significant. They constitute the conditions under which that potential can be responsibly pursued.

AI.tldr: This essay examines seven critical challenges posed by AI's transition from a research instrument to an autonomous participant in science, what the author calls the "industrialization of research," using the DOE's Genesis Mission as a case study.
AI.motivation: To critically examine the profound shift from craft-based to pipeline-based scientific research driven by AI, and to identify the conditions under which AI's potential in science can be responsibly pursued.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis and ethical inquiry structured around seven identified concerns, with the Genesis Mission serving as an illustrative example.
AI.result: Identifies seven critical concerns: erosion of scientific competence transmission, opacity of AI theories, collapse of peer review, unproven paradigm-shifting capacity, agenda capture, compounding systematic errors, and global research bifurcation.
AI.conclusion: AI-driven science has real and significant potential, but responsible pursuit requires addressing these seven concerns rather than ignoring them.

title: Quantifying Training Membership Information in the Hyperspherical Embedding Geometry of Face Recognition Models
summary: Face recognition models represent each face as an embedding vector on the unit hypersphere by clustering embeddings of the same identity while pushing different identities apart through angular-margin losses. Because these losses act only on training identities, non-member identities may form clusters with different geometric properties. In this paper, we quantify the magnitude of this difference and what training-time factors control it. We compute four statistics based on cluster geometry across 180 face recognition models in a factorial design over IResNet backbone size, loss head, training duration, and the number of training identities, and evaluate each configuration on nine benchmarks. Our results indicate that the number of training identities has the largest effect on member/non-member separability, while backbone and loss head contribute far less, and that, on a same-domain held-out reference, the geometric membership signal decreases monotonically as more identities are added to training. We provide an analysis of cross-domain (pose, age, quality, ethnicity) non-member benchmarks and report that these inflate the apparent membership signal. Finally, we fuse all four statistics with a learned classifier to reveal additional membership information beyond the best individual statistic.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how face recognition models create geometric differences between member (training) and non-member (non-training) identity clusters on the hypersphere. Through 180 model configurations, they find the number of training identities is the dominant factor affecting this separability, while backbone size and loss head matter less. Adding more training identities reduces the membership signal. Cross-domain benchmarks inflate this signal. A fused classifier combining multiple geometric statistics extracts more membership information than any single statistic alone.
AI.motivation: Face recognition models use angular-margin losses to cluster same-identity embeddings on the unit hypersphere while separating different identities. However, the geometric properties of clusters for non-member (non-training) identities may differ from those of training members. The paper aims to quantify this difference and identify which training factors (backbone size, loss head, training duration, number of training identities) control the magnitude of member/non-member separability.
AI.method: The authors conduct a large-scale factorial experiment with 180 face recognition model configurations, varying IResNet backbone size, loss head, training duration, and number of training identities. For each model, they compute four geometric cluster statistics and evaluate performance on nine benchmarks. They analyze the effect of each factor on member/non-member separability, examine cross-domain effects, and build a learned classifier fusing all four statistics.
AI.result: Number of training identities is the biggest factor affecting member/non-member geometric separability; backbone and loss head have much smaller effects. Adding more training identities monotonically reduces the membership signal on same-domain held-out data. Cross-domain benchmarks (varying pose, age, quality, ethnicity) inflate the apparent membership signal. The fused classifier combining all four statistics reveals more membership information than the best individual statistic.
AI.conclusion: The geometric differences between member and non-member clusters in face recognition models are primarily controlled by the number of training identities, and the membership signal can be exploited more effectively by combining multiple geometric statistics. Cross-domain evaluation artifacts can exaggerate this signal, which has implications for privacy and membership inference in face recognition systems.

title: Learning in Infinitesimal Non-Compositional Sketches
summary: This paper develops a categorical framework -- Learning in Infinitesimal Non-Compositional Sketches (LINCS) -- as the repair of non-compositionality: failures of diagrams to factor through quotient sketches lifted to the tangent category setting. Machine learning problems are specified as sketches: graphs with commutativity conditions $\mathcal D$, limit cones $\mathcal L$, and colimit cocones $\mathcal K$, generalizing the usual scalarization of loss functions or vector space assumptions. Non-compositionality is defined purely as failure of a universal factorization problem, not as arithmetic error between the desired and actual predictions. Given a learning sketch $\mathbb S=(S,\mathcal D,\mathcal L,\mathcal K)$, whose underlying graph is $S$, and a model $D:J \rightarrow C$, the base defect is the obstruction to factorization $\mbox{Obs}(\mbox{Fact}_{\mathbb S}(D))$. The tangent lift applies the tangent functor $T$ to obtain $TD:J \rightarrow C$, and LINCS is defined as the obstruction $\mbox{Obs}(\mbox{Fact}_{\mathbb S}(TD))$ -- asking whether infinitesimal perturbations preserve the compositionality constraints.The paper also introduces Tangent Learning Sketches, which are sketches equipped with Cockett-Cruttwell tangent structure. The paper defines the INC endofunctor, which iterates the tangent lift, producing a tower $D,TD,T^2D, \cdots$ of factorization problems. ML is thereby formulated as the search for a coalgebraic fixed point where successive tangent unfoldings stabilize ($νT_{\mbox{INC}}$). Using the Aczel--Mendler theorem, we prove existence of a final INC coalgebra whenever $T_{\mbox{INC}}$ admits a set-based class realization that creates its final carrier. A detailed experimental evaluation of LINCS is underway in a number of concrete ML settings, including deep learning, large language models, and reinforcement learning, and is described in companion papers.

AI.tldr: LINCS introduces a categorical framework using tangent sketches to detect and repair non-compositionality in machine learning models, formalized as obstruction to factorization under infinitesimal perturbations.
AI.motivation: To repair failures of compositionality in machine learning problems, where diagrams cannot factor through quotient sketches, without relying on arithmetic errors or vector space assumptions.
AI.method: Uses sketches (graphs with commutativity, limits, colimits) and tangent category structure. Defines base defect as obstruction to factorization, then applies tangent functor T to get LINCS. Introduces INC endofunctor iterating tangent lifts, seeking coalgebraic fixed points via Aczel-Mendler theorem.
AI.result: Existence of a final INC coalgebra is proven when T_INC admits a set-based class realization creating its final carrier. Experimental evaluation is underway in deep learning, LLMs, and RL (in companion papers).
AI.conclusion: LINCS provides a purely categorical, non-arithmetic approach to compositionality repair in ML, with theoretical guarantees on fixed-point existence for tangent lift iterations.

title: MedFailBench: A Clinician-Built Open-Source Benchmark for Medical AI Safety Boundary Inspection
comment: 6 pages; clinician-reviewed synthetic benchmark; no patient data
summary: Most medical AI benchmarks measure whether a model knows the correct answer. MedFailBench asks a different question: which safety boundary failed? We present a clinician-built synthetic benchmark and failure atlas that labels medical AI errors by severity (1--5) and safety gate type (missed urgent escalation, unsafe remote dosing, unsafe discharge reassurance, evidence fabrication, unsafe protocol execution, source support gap). The current public release (v0.2.1) contains 44 clinician-reviewed synthetic cases with severity annotations, a live HuggingFace leaderboard preview, a safety gate taxonomy, a clinical severity rubric, and an automated pipeline for archiving model-response screening runs. No patient data, clinical validation claims, or model rankings are included. MedFailBench is released under Apache-2.0 and CC-BY-4.0 and carries the Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.21205535.

AI.tldr: MedFailBench is a clinician-built synthetic benchmark that categorizes medical AI errors by severity (1–5) and safety gate type (e.g., missed escalation, unsafe dosing), currently v0.2.1 with 44 cases, public leaderboard, and no patient data or clinical validation claims.
AI.motivation: Existing medical AI benchmarks only measure correctness; MedFailBench asks which safety boundary failed, enabling systematic analysis of failure types rather than just accuracy.
AI.method: Clinician-built synthetic benchmark with a safety gate taxonomy (6 types), clinical severity rubric (1–5), 44 clinician-reviewed cases, automated response-screening pipeline, and public HuggingFace leaderboard.
AI.result: Released v0.2.1 with severity annotations, taxonomy, rubric, pipeline; no model rankings or clinical validation claims yet.
AI.conclusion: MedFailBench shifts evaluation from "does the model know the answer?" to "which safety boundary failed?" — a safety-first lens for medical AI.

title: AlphaWiSE: Adaptive Weight Interpolation for Continual Multimodal Representation Learning
summary: Multimodal models such as CLIP learn a shared embedding space for cross-modal retrieval, but continual adaptation to sequentially arriving data can disrupt the cross-modal alignment acquired from earlier phases. Conventional continual-learning methods return a single checkpoint, which commits every retrieval direction to the same stability-plasticity trade-off. We propose AlphaWiSE, a post-hoc weight-space interpolation method that composes two frozen source checkpoints. For each aligned parameter tensor identified by its checkpoint key, AlphaWiSE fits one scalar interpolation coefficient shared by all tensor entries. The coefficients are fitted on a smaller exemplar memory and used to materialize one interpolated checkpoint. The deployed model has the same architecture and parameter count as either source checkpoint, which does not require additional inference time. Extensive experiments on audio-image-text retrieval show consistent improvements over strong continual-learning baselines across multiple retrieval directions and evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: AlphaWiSE proposes post-hoc weight-space interpolation between two frozen checkpoints to improve cross-modal continual learning without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Continual adaptation of multimodal models (e.g., CLIP) disrupts cross-modal alignment from earlier phases, and conventional methods force all retrieval directions into the same stability-plasticity trade-off.
AI.method: AlphaWiSE performs post-hoc weight-space interpolation: it fits one scalar interpolation coefficient per aligned parameter tensor (shared across entries) using a small exemplar memory, then materializes a single interpolated checkpoint.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over strong continual-learning baselines across multiple retrieval directions and metrics on audio-image-text retrieval tasks.
AI.conclusion: Weight-space interpolation between two frozen checkpoints is an effective, lightweight post-hoc strategy for balancing stability and plasticity in multimodal continual learning.

title: On-Policy Delta Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation is an alternative post-training method in reinforcement learning that alleviates the constraints imposed by reward models by providing token-level supervision from a teacher model. Although on-policy distillation has been studied and applied across various settings, its fundamental design remains underexplored. In this paper, we introduce a new distillation reward, termed the delta signal, instead of directly imitating the teacher's output distribution. The delta signal is defined as the difference between the teacher model and its base model prior to instruction tuning for reasoning capability. It therefore captures the changes induced by reasoning tuning and provides a more direct signal for transferring reasoning capabilities. Using extensive empirical evidence, we show that the delta signal substantially improves on-policy distillation and refer to the new distillation method as On-Policy Delta Distillation (OPD$^2$). Experiments across mathematics, science, and code-reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that OPD$^2$ consistently outperforms conventional on-policy distillation, enabling reasoning LLMs to achieve strong performance with only a short post-training period. Code will be available at https://github.com/naver-ai/opd2

AI.tldr: This paper introduces On-Policy Delta Distillation (OPD²), a new on-policy distillation method that uses a "delta signal" (difference between teacher and its base model before reasoning tuning) instead of directly imitating teacher outputs, achieving superior performance in math, science, and code reasoning tasks with shorter training.
AI.motivation: Existing on-policy distillation methods directly imitate teacher output distributions, but this approach may not optimally transfer reasoning capabilities. The authors identify that the fundamental design of on-policy distillation remains underexplored, motivating them to develop a more targeted signal for transferring reasoning abilities.
AI.method: The method introduces a "delta signal" defined as the difference between the teacher model and its base model (before instruction tuning for reasoning). Instead of directly mimicking the teacher's output distribution, OPD² uses this delta as a distillation reward. This captures the specific changes induced by reasoning tuning, providing more direct supervision for transferring reasoning capabilities.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across mathematics, science, and code-reasoning benchmarks show OPD² consistently outperforms conventional on-policy distillation. Reasoning LLMs achieve strong performance with only a short post-training period.
AI.conclusion: The delta signal substantially improves on-policy distillation for reasoning tasks. OPD² enables efficient and effective transfer of reasoning capabilities with reduced training time, offering a promising alternative to standard distillation approaches.

title: Benchmarking Multimodal Large Language Models for Scientific Visualization Literacy
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly used to interpret visualizations, yet current evaluations remain largely chart-centric and provide limited evidence of understanding of scientific visualization (SciVis). We benchmark six MLLMs on the scientific visualization literacy assessment test, a standardized SciVis literacy assessment comprising 49 items based on 18 scientific visualizations and illustrations, spanning 8 techniques and 11 task types. We evaluate three closed-source and three open-source models under a closed-world protocol and compare their performance using data from 485 human participants. Results show that current MLLMs do not exhibit uniform SciVis literacy. Gemini is the strongest model overall, exceeding the human mean across the evaluated subsets, whereas the open-source models remain below the human baseline. Performance is highly uneven across techniques and tasks: models perform best on scientific illustration, search, and spatial understanding, but struggle on texture-based and integration-based visualizations and on quantitative estimation. Error analysis reveals recurring failures in fine-grained quantitative estimation, flow-direction interpretation, and grounded encoding interpretation. These findings position SciVis literacy as a necessary benchmark dimension for evaluating multimodal AI systems. Our code and model outputs are publicly available at https://github.com/patdmp/mllm-scivis-lit-benchmark.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks six multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on scientific visualization literacy, finding that models (especially Gemini) can sometimes exceed human performance but show uneven skills across visualization types, struggling with quantitative estimation, flow-direction, and texture-based visualizations.
AI.motivation: Current MLLM evaluations focus on chart-centric visualizations, providing limited insight into their understanding of scientific visualization (SciVis). This work aims to assess whether MLLMs possess genuine SciVis literacy using a standardized test.
AI.method: Six MLLMs (3 closed-source, 3 open-source) were evaluated on the scientific visualization literacy assessment test, which contains 49 items across 18 visualizations/illustrations, spanning 8 techniques and 11 task types. Performance was compared against 485 human participants under a closed-world protocol.
AI.result: Gemini performed best, exceeding human mean. Open-source models remained below human baseline. Performance was uneven: strong on scientific illustration, search, and spatial understanding; weak on texture-based/integration-based visualizations and quantitative estimation. Common errors included fine-grained quantitative estimation, flow-direction interpretation, and grounded encoding interpretation.
AI.conclusion: SciVis literacy is a necessary benchmark dimension for evaluating multimodal AI systems, as current MLLMs show significant, non-uniform gaps in understanding scientific visualizations.

title: QuReC: All-in-One Image Restoration with Query-Specific Guidance and Local-Global Response Calibration
summary: All-in-one image restoration aims to recover clean images degraded by multiple corruption types using a single unified model. Existing methods typically rely on image-level prompts or shared guidance to handle diverse degradations. However, such a paradigm becomes inadequate when degradations are spatially heterogeneous or even coexist in mixed forms within a single image.
