Yet spatially adaptive guidance alone is not sufficient, since accurate restoration also requires each spatial query to reliably aggregate complementary information from local neighborhoods and global contexts. To this end, we propose QuReC, a unified framework for all-in-one image restoration. QuReC consists of a Degradation-Guided Query Reconstruction Module (DQRM) and a Local-Global Response Calibration Module (LGRCM). Specifically, DQRM matches each spatial query against a degradation prototype space to reconstruct a query-specific degradation-aware representation, thereby providing fine-grained spatially adaptive restoration guidance. To further stabilize this query-wise matching process, we introduce a weakly supervised prototype matching learning strategy to improve optimization stability and degradation semantic consistency. Meanwhile, LGRCM performs local-global dual-branch aggregation and calibrates the aggregated responses with learnable priors, improving the reliability of feature aggregation and the coordination between local detail modeling and global context modeling. Extensive experiments demonstrate that QuReC achieves superior performance on multiple all-in-one image restoration benchmarks. The code is released at https://github.com/zhoushen1/QuReC.

AI.tldr: QuReC is a unified all-in-one image restoration framework that handles spatially heterogeneous and mixed degradations via degradation-guided query reconstruction and local-global response calibration, achieving SOTA performance.
AI.motivation: Existing all-in-one restoration methods rely on image-level prompts or shared guidance, which fail when degradations are spatially heterogeneous or mixed within a single image.
AI.method: QuReC introduces two key modules: (1) Degradation-Guided Query Reconstruction Module (DQRM) that matches spatial queries to a degradation prototype space for fine-grained spatially adaptive guidance, with a weakly supervised learning strategy for stability; (2) Local-Global Response Calibration Module (LGRCM) that performs dual-branch aggregation and calibrates responses with learnable priors for reliable local-global feature coordination.
AI.result: Superior performance on multiple all-in-one image restoration benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: QuReC effectively addresses the limitations of existing all-in-one methods by providing spatially adaptive guidance and reliable local-global feature aggregation, achieving state-of-the-art results.

title: RTS Smoother-Guided Learning of Physics-Based Neural Differential Models
summary: Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) are widely used to model dynamical systems in physics, biology, neuroscience, and physiology, but in many applications some equations of the dynamics are unknown and only a subset of the state variables are measured. We propose a hybrid neural--physics framework in which the known components of the ODE are kept explicit and the missing components are represented by a neural network. The proposed method consists of two stages where we alternate between state and parameter estimation and iterate until a predetermined criterion is met. Specifically, in the first step, we treat the model parameters as being known and we infer the latent states from the available measurements using a Rauch--Tung--Striebel (RTS) smoother. In the second stage, we treat the smoothed trajectories as being known and use them to estimate the neural networks' parameters through backpropagation. We evaluate the method on benchmark systems spanning linear, nonlinear, and stiff dynamics under partial state observation. Across these settings, the proposed method learns missing ODE components from incomplete measurements while exploiting and retaining interpretable mechanistic structure and improving latent-state reconstruction and long-horizon prediction.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hybrid neural-physics framework that combines known ODE components with neural networks for unknown dynamics, using alternating RTS smoothing and backpropagation, achieving improved latent-state reconstruction and prediction under partial observations.
AI.motivation: Many real-world dynamical systems (physics, biology, neuroscience) have partially unknown ODE dynamics and only a subset of state variables are measured, making it challenging to model and predict these systems accurately.
AI.method: A two-stage alternating framework: (1) Treat model parameters as known, infer latent states using an RTS smoother; (2) Treat smoothed trajectories as known, estimate neural network parameters via backpropagation. Iterate until convergence.
AI.result: Evaluated on benchmark systems (linear, nonlinear, stiff dynamics) under partial state observation. The method learns missing ODE components from incomplete measurements while retaining interpretable mechanistic structure.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach improves latent-state reconstruction and long-horizon prediction compared to purely data-driven or purely mechanistic methods, by exploiting known physics while learning unknown components.

title: Can We Trust Item Response Theory for AI Evaluation?
summary: AI benchmarks increasingly leverage item-level statistical models, particularly item response theory (IRT), to estimate model capabilities, rank systems, select informative examples, and diagnose benchmark quality. However, AI benchmark data often departs from the data regime of human testing, for which standard IRT estimation tools were originally developed: benchmarks typically involve fewer evaluated models, far more items, and capability distributions that may be skewed, clustered, or multimodal. We examine how these regime mismatches challenge the reliability of IRT modeling for AI evaluation. Using item parameters and capability distributions derived from six widely used LLM benchmarks, we simulate response matrices under three common IRT models and compare four estimation tools used in recent benchmark studies: marginal maximum likelihood, Markov chain Monte Carlo, variational inference, and a neural pseudo-Siamese estimator. Across 18,000 simulation conditions, we systematically evaluate computational feasibility, scalability, and the reliability of IRT inferences about model rankings, predicted performance, and item characteristics. Results show that classical estimators can become infeasible in large benchmark settings, whereas scalable estimators can produce unreliable item-level and ranking inferences with small or nonnormally distributed model sets. This study identifies when latent trait models reliably support or risk distorting AI benchmarking claims, and what sample sizes and diagnostics are needed for trustworthy use.

AI.tldr: This paper examines how the mismatch between AI benchmark data regimes (few models, many items, skewed capabilities) and standard IRT assumptions developed for human testing affects the reliability of IRT-based AI evaluation, finding that classical estimators become infeasible at scale while scalable estimators produce unreliable inferences with small or non-normal model sets.
AI.motivation: AI benchmarks increasingly use IRT for capability estimation, ranking, and item selection, but AI benchmark data differs substantially from human testing data (fewer models, more items, non-normal capability distributions). The reliability of IRT tools under these regime mismatches is unknown but critical for trustworthy AI evaluation.
AI.method: Using item parameters and capability distributions from six LLM benchmarks, the authors simulate response matrices under three IRT models and compare four estimation tools (marginal maximum likelihood, MCMC, variational inference, neural pseudo-Siamese estimator) across 18,000 simulation conditions, evaluating computational feasibility, scalability, and inference reliability.
AI.result: Classical estimators become computationally infeasible in large benchmark settings, while scalable estimators produce unreliable item-level and ranking inferences when model sets are small or non-normally distributed.
AI.conclusion: The study identifies when IRT models can reliably support AI benchmarking and when they risk distorting claims, providing guidance on sample sizes and diagnostics needed for trustworthy use.

title: DAPGNet: Dynamic Adaptive Physics-Guided Graph Diffusion Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
comment: 9 figures and 9 tables. Pengkun Wang and Weijia Cao contributed equally to this work
summary: Hyperspectral image (HSI) classification requires reliable pixel-relation modeling under spectral variability, mixed pixels, and heterogeneous boundaries. Existing graph-based HSI classifiers usually construct graph topology from spatial proximity, superpixel connectivity, or learned feature affinity. However, the spectral physical prior carried by contiguous bands has limited influence on topology estimation and message propagation. This paper presents DAPGNet, a dynamic adaptive physics-guided graph diffusion network that injects a structure-constrained physical prior into relation-level graph learning. DAPGNet first encodes contiguous spectral responses into node-wise multiscale physical-prior representations. A two-stage graph constructor then combines spectral-spatial affinity, physical-prior consistency, and spatial distance to form a physical-prior-aware sparse topology. During graph diffusion, learned edge weights are transformed into additive attention biases, while a physical gate performs node-wise and feature-wise interpolation between graph-aggregated features and projected physical-prior features. Cross-scale fusion integrates node states from different diffusion depths, and the network is optimized with main classification, auxiliary supervision, and second-order spectral smoothness regularization. Experiments on Indian Pines, WHU-Hi-LongKou, Houston2013, and Houston2018 show that DAPGNet achieves the best OA, AA, and Kappa among representative CNN-, Transformer-, Mamba-, and graph-based baselines. It improves AA over the strongest competing method by 3.64 to 7.31 percentage points across the four datasets. Ablation and sensitivity analyses further support the complementary effects of physical-prior extraction, prior-aware topology construction, physics-gated propagation, and spectral smoothness regularization.

AI.tldr: DAPGNet is a dynamic adaptive physics-guided graph diffusion network that injects spectral physical priors into graph learning for HSI classification, achieving state-of-the-art results on four benchmark datasets with 3.64–7.31% AA improvements over competitors.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-based HSI classifiers construct graph topology from spatial proximity or feature affinity but neglect the spectral physical prior carried by contiguous bands, limiting topology estimation and message propagation.
AI.method: DAPGNet encodes contiguous spectral responses into multiscale physical-prior representations, constructs a physical-prior-aware sparse topology via spectral-spatial affinity, physical-prior consistency, and spatial distance, then applies physics-gated graph diffusion with additive attention biases and node-wise/feature-wise interpolation between graph and physical features, plus cross-scale fusion and spectral smoothness regularization.
AI.result: On Indian Pines, WHU-Hi-LongKou, Houston2013, and Houston2018, DAPGNet achieves the best OA, AA, and Kappa against CNN-, Transformer-, Mamba-, and graph-based baselines, with AA improvements of 3.64–7.31 percentage points over the strongest competitor.
AI.conclusion: The physical-prior extraction, prior-aware topology, physics-gated propagation, and spectral smoothness regularization are complementary and collectively contribute to DAPGNet's superior performance.

title: Plover: Steering GUI Agents through Plan-Centric Interaction
summary: Graphical user interface (GUI) automation remains challenging in real-world environments, where dynamic layouts, unexpected dialogs, and evolving interface states can cause autonomous agents to drift from user intent. Recent vision-based multimodal agents improve flexibility by operating directly over screenshots and natural language instructions, but planning and adaptation often remain internal, limiting users' ability to inspect, supervise, or correct system behavior. We present Plover, a plan-centric vision-based GUI automation system that externalizes task plans and replanning as persistent, inspectable, and revisable artifacts. Through a planner--executor architecture, Plover supports explicit supervision of evolving execution, localized correction through editable plans, natural-language guidance, and screenshot-grounded interventions, while preserving prior progress during repair. A formative study with six participants informed the interaction design. We then evaluate Plover through benchmark failure-case repair and scenario-based workflow analyses. Our results show that many autonomous GUI-agent failures are structurally repairable when plans remain visible and interventions are localized, and that explicit replanning helps make GUI automation more transparent, controllable, and adaptable.

AI.tldr: Plover is a plan-centric GUI automation system that makes task plans visible, inspectable, and editable, enabling users to supervise, correct, and repair agent behavior through localized interventions while preserving prior progress.
AI.motivation: GUI automation agents often fail in dynamic environments due to hidden planning and adaptation processes, limiting user ability to inspect, supervise, or correct system behavior.
AI.method: Plover uses a planner-executor architecture that externalizes task plans as persistent, inspectable artifacts, supporting explicit supervision, localized plan editing, natural-language guidance, and screenshot-grounded interventions.
AI.result: Many autonomous GUI-agent failures are structurally repairable when plans remain visible and interventions are localized; explicit replanning enhances transparency, controllability, and adaptability.
AI.conclusion: Making GUI automation plans visible and editable improves system transparency and user control, enabling effective repair of agent failures through localized interventions.

title: Ray-based phase error correction for miniaturized DOE projector-based FPP under single-directional hyperbolic projection
summary: Fringe Projection Profilometry (FPP) systems using miniaturized DOE pro-jectors often suffer from severe phase artifacts due to nonlinear projection characteristics and limited pattern controllability. We propose a ray-based phase error correction framework that models phase artifacts along projection rays from the projector pinhole, incorporating projector geometry without re-lying on image-domain processing or neighboring pixels. A projector pinhole estimation method based on a single-directional hyperbolic fringe pattern is introduced, through which projector geometry can be recovered without stereo calibration. In addition, a data-efficient strategy constructs the re-finement model from a single calibration pose. Experiments on miniaturized DOE projector-based FPP systems demonstrate significant improvements in reconstruction accuracy under nonlinear projection conditions, confirming the robustness and physical consistency of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: A ray-based phase error correction framework is proposed to address phase artifacts in miniaturized DOE projector-based FPP systems, using projector pinhole estimation via hyperbolic fringe patterns and data-efficient calibration from a single pose.
AI.motivation: Miniaturized DOE projectors in FPP systems suffer from severe phase artifacts caused by nonlinear projection characteristics and limited pattern controllability.
AI.method: A ray-based phase error correction framework that models phase artifacts along projection rays from the projector pinhole, plus a projector pinhole estimation method using single-directional hyperbolic fringe patterns, and a data-efficient single-pose calibration strategy.
AI.result: Significant improvements in reconstruction accuracy under nonlinear projection conditions, confirming robustness and physical consistency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ray-based approach effectively corrects phase errors in miniaturized DOE projector FPP systems without relying on image-domain processing or stereo calibration.

title: Data Driven Block Replacement Scheduling
summary: We develop data-driven algorithms for maintaining $N$ independent identical machines under a \textit{block replacement policy}, in which each machine is replaced upon failure and all machines are jointly replaced at regular intervals of length $k$. The goal is to learn the cost-minimizing interval $k^*$ from operational data when the lifetime distribution is unknown. At each decision epoch, the operator selects $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$, observes the resulting failure history (a mixture of complete and right-censored lifetimes) and incurs a per-unit-time cost governed by the renewal function. We formulate this as a stochastic multi-armed bandit and propose Hoeffding- and Bernstein-based lower-confidence-bound algorithms achieving $O(K \log T)$ regret, matching the Lai--Robbins lower bound. Exploiting a nested observation property unique to block replacement, correlated variants attain $O((K-k^*)\log T)$ regret and require only $O(1)$ direct pulls of suboptimal arms $k < k^*$. A complementary Kaplan--Meier renewal algorithm estimates the lifetime distribution nonparametrically from censored data, achieving almost-sure policy consistency and empirically near-zero incremental regret at long horizons. We additionally analyze two average-cost MDPs: a time-elapsed formulation establishing that block replacement is optimal within its policy class for any lifetime distribution, and an age-vector formulation proving a monotone threshold structure under increasing failure rate distributions and providing a gold-standard cost benchmark. Numerical experiments confirm the theoretical ordering and reveal structural cost gaps between optimal block and age-dependent replacement.

AI.tldr: This paper develops bandit algorithms for learning the optimal block replacement interval for identical machines when the lifetime distribution is unknown, achieving near-optimal regret guarantees and using censored data efficiently.
AI.motivation: To learn the cost-minimizing block replacement interval from operational data when the machine lifetime distribution is unknown, rather than assuming it is known a priori.
AI.method: Formulates the problem as a stochastic multi-armed bandit with Hoeffding- and Bernstein-based LCB algorithms, exploits a nested observation property for correlated variants, and uses Kaplan-Meier estimation for nonparametric renewal learning.
AI.result: Achieves O(K log T) regret matching the Lai-Robbins lower bound, with correlated variants achieving O((K-k*) log T) regret requiring only O(1) pulls of suboptimal arms. Kaplan-Meier renewal algorithm achieves almost-sure consistency and near-zero incremental regret.
AI.conclusion: Block replacement is optimal within its policy class for any lifetime distribution, and under IFR distributions, age-dependent replacement exhibits a monotone threshold structure. Numerical experiments confirm theoretical results and reveal structural cost gaps.

title: Self-Evolving Human-Centered Framework for Explainable Depression Symptom Annotation
comment: Accepted at IEEE International Conference on Omni-Layer Intelligent Systems (COINS) 2026
summary: Annotation quality is a major bottleneck in building reliable and explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) systems for mental health research. In depression-related datasets, labels are often assigned without structured evidence, symptom-level justification, or traceable alignment with the criteria of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition, Text Revision (DSM-5-TR), limiting both transparency and downstream model interpretability. We propose a self-evolving, expert-in-the-loop annotation framework for Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) that combines large language model (LLM)-assisted labeling with expert verification. The framework is intended to support the construction of explainable, DSM-5-TR-aligned datasets rather than to perform clinical diagnosis. It operates in three stages: candidate evidence selection from textual records, criterion-level DSM-5-TR analysis, and case-level synthesis that produces label-level diagnostic and severity annotations. A dual-memory architecture, composed of Example Memory and Reflection Memory, is designed to internalize expert feedback and iteratively improve future annotations without retraining. We describe this mechanism and leave its evaluation across multiple feedback cycles to future work. In addition to final labels, the framework exports clinical evidence, reasoning traces, and edit histories, enabling comprehensive auditability. In a pilot study using expert-reviewed samples, the proposed approach improves annotation consistency and explainability while reducing manual revision effort.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a self-evolving, expert-in-the-loop annotation framework for Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) that combines LLM-assisted labeling with expert verification to produce DSM-5-TR-aligned, explainable, and auditable datasets for mental health AI research.
AI.motivation: Annotation quality is a major bottleneck in building reliable XAI systems for mental health. Existing depression datasets lack structured evidence, symptom-level justification, and traceable alignment with DSM-5-TR criteria, limiting transparency and interpretability.
AI.method: A three-stage framework: (1) candidate evidence selection from text, (2) criterion-level DSM-5-TR analysis, (3) case-level synthesis for diagnostic/severity labels. Uses a dual-memory architecture (Example Memory + Reflection Memory) to internalize expert feedback and improve iteratively without retraining.
AI.result: In a pilot study with expert-reviewed samples, the approach improves annotation consistency and explainability while reducing manual revision effort.
AI.conclusion: The framework supports constructing explainable, DSM-5-TR-aligned datasets and enables comprehensive auditability through exported clinical evidence, reasoning traces, and edit histories. Full evaluation across multiple feedback cycles is left to future work.

title: MAGiSt3R: Multi-Agent Feed-forward 3D Reconstruction from Monocular RGB Videos
summary: This paper presents MAGiSt3R, a multi-agent 3D reconstruction framework performing reconstruction and camera tracking for monocular RGB videos at almost 10 FPS. MAGiSt3R relies on a feed-forward model from the 3R family to process RGB videos and regress local point maps, and on a merging model, MAGMA, that combines local maps at both intra-agent and inter-agent levels to obtain the final global point map. Furthermore, MAGiSt3R performs pose graph optimization to mitigate cumulative camera drift occurring along the feed-forward pipeline. We evaluate MAGiSt3R on both synthetic and real-world datasets, demonstrating its superior reconstruction and camera tracking accuracy compared to state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: MAGiSt3R is a multi-agent 3D reconstruction system that achieves near real-time (10 FPS) monocular RGB video reconstruction and camera tracking using feed-forward local point maps, a novel merging model (MAGMA), and pose graph optimization for drift reduction.
AI.motivation: To achieve fast (real-time) and accurate 3D reconstruction from monocular RGB videos by overcoming limitations of existing feed-forward methods, particularly cumulative camera drift.
AI.method: Uses a feed-forward model from the 3R family to regress local point maps from RGB videos, a merging model (MAGMA) to combine local maps at intra-agent and inter-agent levels, and pose graph optimization to correct camera drift.
AI.result: Achieves ~10 FPS reconstruction and camera tracking, with superior accuracy over state-of-the-art methods on both synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: MAGiSt3R demonstrates that multi-agent coordination with feed-forward local mapping and pose graph optimization enables both fast and accurate monocular 3D reconstruction.

title: Mutable Low-Rank Sketches for Retrain-Free Recommendation
summary: A common bottleneck in two-stage recommendation is embedding staleness: when a user rates a new item, their embedding remains fixed until the next retrain cycle. We propose mutable sketches, which store each user's preferences in a KP-tree (a sparse segment tree with sum aggregation), fit a low-rank projection once, and recompute embeddings on-the-fly as ratings arrive. We prove that each new observation monotonically tightens the prediction error envelope (Theorem 1), a guarantee that FunkSVD and eALS lack. On KuaiRec, the mutable sketch achieves 0.810 RMSE at 1.8% data read vs. ALS 0.822 at 100%, with 8x faster per-batch updates. A new user receives personalized recommendations in <1 ms after their first rating, with no model retraining required. A comparison of sampling strategies across density regimes shows that the KP-tree's norm-proportional sampling provides 40-130% better item coverage on sparse data (<1% density), while uniform sampling suffices on dense matrices.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces mutable sketches using KP-trees to solve embedding staleness in two-stage recommendation, enabling real-time embedding updates with provable error guarantees and significant efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: The key bottleneck in two-stage recommendation is embedding staleness - user embeddings remain fixed between retrain cycles even when new ratings arrive, reducing recommendation quality.
AI.method: The method uses mutable sketches based on KP-trees (sparse segment trees with sum aggregation), fits a low-rank projection once, and recomputes embeddings on-the-fly as ratings arrive.
AI.result: On KuaiRec, achieves 0.810 RMSE at 1.8% data read vs. ALS 0.822 at 100%, with 8x faster per-batch updates. New users receive recommendations in <1ms after first rating. KP-tree's norm-proportional sampling provides 40-130% better item coverage on sparse data.
AI.conclusion: Mutable sketches with KP-trees effectively eliminate embedding staleness with provable error guarantees, achieving superior efficiency and coverage, especially on sparse data.

title: When Words Are Safe But Actions Kill: Probing Physical Danger Beyond Text Safety in Hidden-State Risk Space
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly serve as high-level planners for embodied agents, where linguistically benign instructions can become unsafe once grounded in the physical world. We study whether this physically grounded danger is the same safety problem as ordinary text-level content danger. Through hidden-state direction analysis and random-split null tests, we show that content danger (CD) and physical danger (PD) form separable signals in LLM representations across Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B/32B, Phi-3.5 and SmolLM2. Building on the CD/PD separability, we propose PRISM, a single-layer L2-regularized logistic probe over full hidden states. PRISM achieves 86.2--87.7\% accuracy on SafeAgentBench with 11.7--13.7\% FPR, while same-scale LLM judges over-block safe tasks at 24.7--39.0\% FPR. We further introduce PhysicalSafetyBench-1K (PSB-1K), a contrastive benchmark of 1{,}000 physical-risk pairs without direct harm keywords, to test whether methods detect physically grounded danger rather than explicit unsafe wording. On PSB-1K, PRISM reaches 99.6\% accuracy and 0.7\% FPR, whereas a Qwen2.5-3B judge rejects 67.8\% of safe tasks. PRISM also replicates on SafeText and EARBench, supporting hidden-state probing as a representation-level method for physical safety beyond text moderation.

AI.tldr: LLMs have separable representations for content danger (CD) and physical danger (PD). PRISM, a simple logistic probe on hidden states, detects physical danger with high accuracy (86-88%) and low false positive rates (12-14%), outperforming LLM judges. A new benchmark PSB-1K further tests physical danger detection without explicit unsafe keywords.
AI.motivation: Linguistically benign instructions for embodied agents can become dangerous when grounded physically. Current safety methods don't distinguish between text-level content danger and physically grounded danger, which may be fundamentally different safety problems.
AI.method: 1) Hidden-state direction analysis and random-split null tests across models (Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B/32B, Phi-3.5, SmolLM2) to show CD and PD are separable signals. 2) PRISM: single-layer L2-regularized logistic probe on full hidden states. 3) PhysicalSafetyBench-1K (PSB-1K): contrastive benchmark of 1,000 physical-risk pairs without harm keywords.
AI.result: PRISM achieves 86.2-87.7% accuracy on SafeAgentBench (11.7-13.7% FPR) vs LLM judges at 24.7-39.0% FPR. On PSB-1K: 99.6% accuracy, 0.7% FPR. Replicates on SafeText and EARBench.
AI.conclusion: Content danger and physical danger are separable in LLM representations. Hidden-state probing is effective for physical safety detection beyond text moderation, offering better precision than LLM judges.

title: Symbal: Detecting Systematic Misalignments in Model-Generated Captions
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often introduce errors when generating image captions, resulting in misaligned image-text pairs. Our work focuses on a class of captioning errors that we refer to as systematic misalignments, where a recurring error in MLLM-generated captions is closely associated with the presence of a specific visual feature in the paired image. Given a vision-language dataset with MLLM-generated captions, our aim in this work is to detect such errors, a task we refer to as systematic misalignment detection. As our first key contribution, we present Symbal, which utilizes a structured, dual-stage setup with off-the-shelf foundation models to identify systematic misalignments and summarize results in natural language. As our second key contribution, we introduce SymbalBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate automated methods on our proposed task. SymbalBench consists of 1.7 million image-text pairs from two domains (natural and medical images), organized into 420 vision-language datasets with annotated systematic misalignments. Symbal exhibits strong performance on this benchmark, correctly identifying systematic misalignments in 63.8% of datasets, a nearly 4x improvement over the closest baseline. We supplement our evaluations on SymbalBench with real-world evaluations, showing that (1) Symbal can accurately surface systematic misalignments in captions generated by four MLLMs and (2) Symbal is a powerful tool for auditing off-the-shelf image-caption datasets. Ultimately, our novel task, method, and benchmark can aid users with auditing MLLM-generated captions and identifying critical errors, without requiring access to the underlying MLLM. Code is available at https://github.com/Stanford-AIMI/Symbal.

AI.tldr: Symbal detects systematic misalignments (recurring captioning errors tied to specific visual features) in MLLM-generated image captions using a dual-stage framework with off-the-shelf foundation models. The authors introduce SymbalBench, a benchmark with 1.7M image-text pairs across 420 datasets for evaluation. Symbal achieves 63.8% detection accuracy (4x improvement over baselines).
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs produce systematic captioning errors (recurring misalignments tied to specific visual features) that degrade vision-language dataset quality. Current methods lack automated detection for such errors without accessing the underlying MLLM.
AI.method: Symbal uses a structured dual-stage setup with off-the-shelf foundation models: first identifies systematic misalignments, then summarizes results in natural language. SymbalBench contains 1.7M image-text pairs (natural and medical images) across 420 datasets with annotated systematic misalignments.
AI.result: Symbal correctly identifies systematic misalignments in 63.8% of datasets, achieving nearly 4x improvement over the closest baseline. It works across four MLLMs and proves effective for auditing off-the-shelf image-caption datasets.
AI.conclusion: The novel task (systematic misalignment detection), method (Symbal), and benchmark (SymbalBench) enable automated auditing of MLLM-generated captions to identify critical errors without requiring access to the underlying MLLM.

title: Decoding Market Emotion from Blockchain Activity: A Data-Driven Sentiment Classifier
comment: This manuscript has been accepted for presentation at the IEEE International Symposium on Computers and Communications (ISCC 2026)
summary: The growing use of Bitcoin as a decentralized digital asset and investment tool has sparked strong interest in understanding its market behavior. This study presents a new approach to analyze Bitcoin market sentiment by combining on-chain and financial data with social media posts. Unlike models that aim to predict prices, this work focuses on explaining market sentiment using blockchain transactions, historical price data of Bitcoin, and daily Twitter sentiment classifications. The method merges sentiment trends with on-chain and financial metrics, normalized into a dataset for detailed market analysis. Multiple machine learning models were tested using cross-validation, with Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) emerging as the most reliable model for classifying sentiment, achieving an average F1-score of about 0.84. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), a game theory-based method for model interpretability, was used to quantify the contribution of on-chain features to the model's predictions, improving transparency. The results indicate that this data combination yields meaningful predictive signals and insights, supporting data-driven cryptocurrency analysis and future improvements with deep learning.

AI.tldr: This study combines on-chain data, financial metrics, and Twitter sentiment to classify Bitcoin market sentiment using machine learning, with XGBoost achieving the best F1-score (~0.84), and SHAP used to interpret feature contributions.
AI.motivation: To move beyond price prediction and instead focus on explaining Bitcoin market sentiment by integrating on-chain, financial, and social media data.
AI.method: Normalized multi-source dataset (blockchain transactions, historical price, daily Twitter sentiment), multiple ML models tested with cross-validation, XGBoost selected as best, SHAP for interpretability.
AI.result: XGBoost achieved an average F1-score of approximately 0.84 for sentiment classification. SHAP analysis quantified on-chain feature contributions, improving model transparency.
AI.conclusion: The combination of on-chain, financial, and social media data yields meaningful predictive signals for cryptocurrency sentiment analysis, with potential for future deep learning enhancements.

title: AutoSynthesis: An agentic system for automated meta-analysis
summary: Evidence synthesis is crucial for turning primary research into reliable knowledge for science, medicine, education, and policy. Yet, quantitative evidence synthesis remains largely manual and difficult to scale. Here, we introduce AutoSynthesis, an end-to-end multi-agent system for automated meta-analysis. Given a research question in natural language, AutoSynthesis formulates a search strategy, retrieves scientific literature, screens candidate studies, assesses full-text eligibility, extracts quantitative statistics, computes standardized effect sizes, and finally performs random-effects meta-analysis. AutoSynthesis further supports heterogeneity analysis to examine how effect sizes vary across moderators, as well as risk-of-bias assessment. As output, AutoSynthesis produces a transparent report aligned with PRISMA guidelines. In our application, AutoSynthesis screened over 28 studies and extracted more than 20 quantitative claims. The pooled effect estimates produced by AutoSynthesis are similar to Hedges' $g$ of expert-conducted meta-analyses, indicating close agreement with manual evidence synthesis. Together, these results show that AutoSynthesis can make quantitative evidence synthesis more scalable, thereby supporting evidence-based decision-making across disciplines.

AI.tldr: AutoSynthesis is an end-to-end multi-agent system that automates meta-analysis from search to reporting, producing results that closely match expert-conducted meta-analyses.
AI.motivation: Quantitative evidence synthesis (meta-analysis) remains largely manual, unscalable, and labor-intensive, limiting its ability to turn primary research into reliable knowledge for science, medicine, education, and policy.
AI.method: AutoSynthesis is a multi-agent system that: formulates search strategies from natural language questions, retrieves and screens literature, assesses eligibility, extracts statistics, computes effect sizes (Hedges' g), performs random-effects meta-analysis, includes heterogeneity analysis and risk-of-bias assessment, and outputs a PRISMA-aligned report.
AI.result: Applied to over 28 studies and 20+ quantitative claims; pooled effect estimates from AutoSynthesis closely matched those from expert-conducted meta-analyses.
AI.conclusion: AutoSynthesis makes quantitative evidence synthesis more scalable and transparent, supporting evidence-based decision-making across disciplines.

title: Structural-Semantic Reciprocal Learning for Unsupervised Visible-Infrared Person Re-Identification
summary: Unsupervised visible-infrared person re-identification (USVI-ReID) is challenging due to the large modality gap and the lack of cross-modal identity annotations. Progressive association paradigms have been proposed to gradually bridge the gap, but they suffer from two critical bottlenecks: reliance on ambiguous global representations and unchecked propagation of pseudo-label noise in an open-loop manner. To address these issues, we propose Structural-Semantic Reciprocal Learning (SSRL), a framework that transforms open-loop association into a self-correcting closed-loop system. Structurally, we introduce Fine-grained Structural Decoupling (FSD) to extract discriminative body-part primitives as reliable spatial anchors, complementing ambiguous holistic silhouettes with spatially consistent structural details. Semantically, we design a Closed-loop Semantic Calibration (CSC) mechanism that reconstructs shared semantic prototypes at each epoch and feeds them back into the training loop, effectively filtering pseudo-label noise before the next clustering cycle. Through the reciprocal interaction between structural and semantic learning, SSRL achieves robust cross-modal representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate the competitive performance of SSRL against state-of-the-art USVI-ReID methods on both SYSU-MM01 and RegDB, notably surpassing several supervised counterparts on RegDB.

AI.tldr: SSRL introduces Structural-Semantic Reciprocal Learning, a closed-loop framework that uses Fine-grained Structural Decoupling and Closed-loop Semantic Calibration to address ambiguous global representations and noisy pseudo-labels in unsupervised visible-infrared person re-identification.
AI.motivation: Existing progressive association paradigms for USVI-ReID suffer from reliance on ambiguous global representations and unchecked propagation of pseudo-label noise in an open-loop manner.
AI.method: Proposes SSRL with two key components: (1) Fine-grained Structural Decoupling (FSD) extracts discriminative body-part primitives as spatial anchors; (2) Closed-loop Semantic Calibration (CSC) reconstructs shared semantic prototypes at each epoch and feeds them back to filter pseudo-label noise, transforming open-loop association into a self-correcting closed-loop system.
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance against state-of-the-art USVI-ReID methods on SYSU-MM01 and RegDB, notably surpassing several supervised counterparts on RegDB.
AI.conclusion: The reciprocal interaction between structural and semantic learning enables robust cross-modal representation, and the closed-loop design effectively addresses the bottlenecks of open-loop association paradigms.

title: teLLMe Why (Ain't Nothing but a Jam): Exploratory Causal Analysis of Urban Driving Data
comment: Accepted at the NeurIPS 2025 Workshop on UrbanAI. 6 pages
summary: Traffic agencies now have access to large volumes of video-derived data for studying safety and congestion. Most of these data are observational and collected without interventions, which makes causal questions such as "How would rain change traffic density?" difficult to answer. We present teLLMe, a system for exploratory causal analysis of urban driving datasets. The system starts from a structured event table built from dashcam annotations and combines causal structure learning with the PC algorithm, bootstrap-based stability checks, and query-specific effect estimation using linear regression and DoWhy. Natural-language questions are mapped to structured causal queries through a schema-aware LLM, enabling users to specify treatments, outcomes, and subpopulations. teLLMe returns a "Causal Card" that summarizes effect estimates, adjustment sets, DAG support, and assumptions, followed by a short natural-language explanation. Case studies on BDD-derived traffic events show that the system can surface plausible relationships involving weather, peak hours, and traffic density, while making uncertainty and modeling choices explicit. The system is designed as a tool for hypothesis generation and expert reasoning rather than a source of definitive causal claims.

AI.tldr: teLLMe is a system for exploratory causal analysis of urban driving datasets that uses causal structure learning, stability checks, and LLM-based query mapping to generate Causal Cards with effect estimates and explanations from dashcam-derived event data.
AI.motivation: Traffic agencies have large volumes of observational video data but struggle to answer causal questions (e.g., "How would rain change traffic density?") because most data lack interventions, making standard causal inference difficult.
AI.method: teLLMe combines PC algorithm for causal structure learning, bootstrap-based stability checks, linear regression with DoWhy for effect estimation, and a schema-aware LLM to map natural-language questions into structured causal queries, outputting a "Causal Card."
AI.result: Case studies on BDD-derived traffic events show teLLMe surfaces plausible causal relationships (weather, peak hours, traffic density) while making uncertainty and modeling choices explicit.
AI.conclusion: The system is designed for hypothesis generation and expert reasoning, not definitive causal claims, serving as an exploratory tool for analysts.

title: Divergent Gaze Patterns in Artistic Viewing: Spatial and Temporal Signatures of Attention Across Autistic Individuals, Artists, and Neurotypical Observers
summary: How different populations visually explore artworks bears on cognitive science and on accessibility design, yet most eye-tracking work in autism has used social scenes rather than art, and has analysed where the eyes land while ignoring when and in what order. We present a comparative free-viewing study across three groups, autistic adults (ASD), trained artists, and neurotypical observers, who each viewed 30 paintings for 15s. We introduce a directed, metric-grounded framework that compares groups along two complementary axes: a spatial axis, in which one group's fixation-density map predicts another's fixations under six saliency metrics (AUC-Judd, NSS, CC, SIM, KL, Information Gain); and a temporal axis, in which individual scanpaths are compared with MultiMatch, ScanMatch, a foveal-disc IoU score (FDISS), and dynamic time warping (DTW). Fixations are extracted uniformly for all groups with a dispersion-threshold algorithm. Three results converge. (i)Artists and neurotypicals are almost indistinguishable in both space (density-map correlation CC=0.96) and time (they form the most alignable scanpath pair), whereas ASD gaze diverges from both. (ii)ASD attention is dissociated: it matches artists' wide spatial exploration (dispersion, explored area) but carries a distinct temporal signature, shorter fixations, less dwell, and the most idiosyncratic (least self-consistent) scanpaths of any group. (iii)ASD gaze is not selectively artist-like on any metric; if anything it is marginally closer to neurotypical. Together these findings indicate that autistic viewing of art is a distinct, group-specific attentional profile in both space and time, and they motivate population-conditioned models of aesthetic attention. We release all analysis code and per-stimulus results.

AI.tldr: This study compares free-viewing eye movements on paintings across autistic adults, trained artists, and neurotypical observers using both spatial (saliency metrics) and temporal (scanpath analysis) frameworks. Results show artists and neurotypicals are nearly indistinguishable, while ASD exhibits a distinct attentional profile: wide spatial exploration like artists but with shorter fixations and idiosyncratic temporal patterns, representing a unique group-specific viewing style.
AI.motivation: Most prior eye-tracking work in autism used social scenes rather than art, and analyzed fixation locations without considering temporal dynamics (when and in what order eyes move). This study addresses both gaps by introducing a dual spatial-temporal framework to understand how autistic individuals visually explore artworks.
AI.method: Three groups (ASD adults, trained artists, neurotypical controls) viewed 30 paintings for 15s each. Spatial analysis used 6 saliency metrics (AUC-Judd, NSS, CC, SIM, KL, Information Gain) comparing fixation-density maps. Temporal analysis used MultiMatch, ScanMatch, foveal-disc IoU (FDISS), and dynamic time warping (DTW) to compare scanpaths. Fixations were extracted uniformly via dispersion-threshold algorithm.
AI.result: (i) Artists and neurotypicals are nearly indistinguishable (CC=0.96). (ii) ASD shows wide spatial exploration like artists but distinct temporal signature (shorter fixations, less dwell, most idiosyncratic scanpaths). (iii) ASD gaze is not selectively artist-like; marginally closer to neurotypical.
AI.conclusion: Autistic viewing of art represents a distinct, group-specific attentional profile in both space and time, motivating population-conditioned models of aesthetic attention.

title: SearchOS-V1: Towards Robust Open-Domain Information-Seeking Agent Collaboration
summary: Recent advances in Tool-Integrated Large Language Models have made web search a core capability of information-seeking agents. However, as interaction histories grow, agents increasingly struggle to track task progress. When search attempts fail to yield useful evidence, current single- and multi-agent systems can become trapped in repetitive loops, wasting search budgets and ultimately compromising the quality and completeness of the final output. We introduce SearchOS, a system-level multi-agent framework that turns fragile, implicit search progress into explicit, persistent, and shared state. First, we formulate open-domain information seeking as relational schema completion with grounded citations, where agents discover entities, populate attributes across linked tables, and anchor each value to source evidence. Then we design Search-Oriented Context Management (SOCM), which externalizes the evolving state into Frontier Task, an Evidence Graph, a Coverage Map, and Failure Memory. Built on SOCM, SearchOS applies a pipeline-parallel scheduling mechanism that overlaps the execution of sub-agents and continuously refills freed slots with tasks targeting unresolved coverage gaps to improve utilization and throughput. To schedule and control the execution of search agents, SearchOS introduces a Search Tool Middleware Harness that intercepts model and tool interactions to record grounded evidence and react to stalls or budget exhaustion, and provides a reusable hierarchical skill system comprising strategy and access skills to augment the agents' search process and avoid repeating failed search patterns across runs. On WideSearch and GISA, SearchOS leads all metrics among the evaluated single- and multi-agent baselines, paving the way toward robust information-seeking collaboration.

AI.tldr: SearchOS is a multi-agent framework that improves web search by externalizing task state, enabling parallel agent execution, and avoiding repetitive search failures.
AI.motivation: Current Tool-Integrated LLMs struggle to track progress during long web search sessions, often falling into repetitive loops that waste search budgets and degrade output quality.
AI.method: SearchOS introduces SOCM (externalized state with Frontier Task, Evidence Graph, Coverage Map, Failure Memory), pipeline-parallel scheduling, Search Tool Middleware Harness, and a hierarchical skill system to avoid failed patterns.
AI.result: SearchOS outperforms single- and multi-agent baselines on WideSearch and GISA benchmarks across all metrics.
AI.conclusion: SearchOS provides a robust foundation for information-seeking collaboration by converting implicit search state into explicit, persistent, shared memory.

title: CRISP: Constrained Refinement via Iterative Squeezing Process for Robust Medical Image Segmentation under Domain Shift
comment: X pages, 3 figures, 3 tables; submitted to AAAI 2027
summary: Distribution shift in medical imaging remains a central bottleneck for the clinical translation of medical AI. Failure to address it can lead to severe performance degradation in unseen environments and exacerbate health inequities. Existing methods for domain adaptation are inherently limited by exhausting predefined possibilities through simulated shifts or pseudo-supervision. Such strategies struggle in the open-ended and unpredictable real world, where distribution shifts are effectively infinite. To address this challenge, we adopt the "Rank Stability of Positive Regions" as a working assumption under distribution shift, and use it to derive robust spatial hints for source-only segmentation. Guided by this assumption, we propose CRISP, a model-agnostic framework that, unlike deployment-time adaptation, requires no test-time parameter updates and no target-domain data--a target-free, plug-in refinement framework that segments with frozen weights. Rather than using ranking to directly output masks, CRISP exploits the stability of probability rankings under distribution shift to derive robust spatial priors. Via latent feature perturbation, perturbation-invariant high-grade regions define a high-precision (HP) core, while voxels that remain potentially foreground under at least one perturbation define a high-recall (HR) support; these dual priors are then recursively refined under perturbation. We then design an iterative training framework that progressively squeezes HP and HR toward the final segmentation. Extensive evaluations on multi-center cardiac MRI and CT-based lung vessel segmentation demonstrate CRISP's superior robustness, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods with striking HD95 reductions of up to 0.14 (7.0% improvement), 1.90 (13.1% improvement), and 8.39 (38.9% improvement) pixels across multi-center, demographic, and modality shifts, respectively.

AI.tldr: CRISP is a model-agnostic, target-free framework that improves segmentation robustness under distribution shifts by leveraging the stability of probability rankings—using perturbation-invariant high-precision and high-recall spatial priors—without requiring test-time adaptation or target-domain data.
AI.motivation: Distribution shift in medical imaging hinders clinical AI translation, causing performance degradation and health inequities. Existing domain adaptation methods rely on predefined simulated shifts or pseudo-supervision, which fail in open-ended real-world scenarios where shifts are effectively infinite.
AI.method: CRISP adopts "Rank Stability of Positive Regions" as a working assumption, deriving robust spatial hints via latent feature perturbation. It defines a high-precision (HP) core (perturbation-invariant high-grade regions) and a high-recall (HR) support (voxels potentially foreground under any perturbation), then uses iterative recursive refinement with frozen model weights—no test-time updates or target data required.
AI.result: On multi-center cardiac MRI and CT-based lung vessel segmentation, CRISP significantly outperforms SOTA methods, achieving HD95 reductions of up to 0.14 (7.0%), 1.90 (13.1%), and 8.39 (38.9%) pixels across multi-center, demographic, and modality shifts respectively.
AI.conclusion: CRISP demonstrates that exploiting rank stability of probability predictions under perturbation provides a powerful, practical solution for robust medical image segmentation under unpredictable distribution shifts, without needing target data or test-time adaptation.

title: Pretraining Data Can Be Poisoned through Computational Propaganda
summary: Poisoning pretraining data can introduce harmful behaviors to LMs that are difficult to detect and mitigate. Prior work on poisoning pretraining data has largely exploited established data sources such as Wikipedia, which do not represent the large scale and heterogeneity typical of pretraining corpora, and has ignored the interaction between poisoned data and data curation pipelines. We demonstrate that poisoning attacks on pretraining data are feasible beyond this limited setting through an existing web-scale content injection mechanism: public discussion interfaces. Additionally, to measure whether malicious content is included after web crawling and data curation, we introduce HalfLife, a novel analysis for estimating adversarial content inclusion in web-crawl based LM training data. We use HalfLife to explore the feasibility of poisoning pretraining corpora at web scale through open discussion interfaces. Our analysis demonstrates the importance of estimating whether poison injections are included in pretraining data, and establishes third-party webpage content as a possible vector for attacking language model pretraining.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that poisoning attacks on LM pretraining data are feasible at web scale via public discussion interfaces, and introduces HalfLife, a method for estimating whether malicious content survives web crawling and data curation pipelines.
AI.motivation: Prior work on poisoning pretraining data focused on established sources like Wikipedia, ignoring large-scale, heterogeneous pretraining corpora and the impact of data curation pipelines. The authors aim to show real-world feasibility of such attacks at web scale.
AI.method: The authors use public discussion interfaces as a content injection mechanism for web-scale poisoning. They introduce HalfLife, a novel analysis method to estimate the likelihood that adversarial content persists in web-crawl-based LM training data after curation.
AI.result: Poisoning attacks on pretraining data are feasible beyond limited settings via web-scale content injection. HalfLife provides estimates of adversarial content inclusion after web crawling and data curation.
AI.conclusion: Estimating whether poison injections are included in pretraining data is important. Third-party webpage content is established as a viable vector for attacking language model pretraining at scale.

title: ARMOR++: Agentic Orchestration of a Multi-Domain Primitive Set for Transferable Attacks on Deepfake Detectors
summary: The reliability of deepfake detectors frequently degrades under black-box adversarial transfer, as these models often rely on fragile, architecture-dependent forensic cues. Existing transfer attacks often lack semantic awareness and struggle to maintain effectiveness under strict no-query constraints, particularly when perturbations are transferred from convolutional surrogates to transformer-based targets. To address these limitations, this paper introduces ARMOR++, a robust multi-agent framework designed for high-transferability deepfake evasion. The framework leverages the Qwen2.5-VL Vision-Language Model (VLM) to supply spatial semantic priors, while the Qwen3 Large Language Model (LLM) orchestrates primitive selection, adaptive hyperparameter reparameterization, and entropy-regularized perturbation mixing. By integrating five complementary primitives, spanning dense optimization, saliency-based methods, spatial transformations, frequency-domain perturbations, and block-structured modifications, ARMOR++ effectively targets heterogeneous inductive biases. Rigorous evaluation on the AADD-2025 benchmark demonstrates that ARMOR++ significantly outperforms existing agentic and non-agentic baselines across both low- and high-quality image regimes. Statistical analysis confirms a substantial gain in blind-target Attack Success Rate (ASR) over the state-of-the-art agentic baseline, with further performance advantages evidenced against non-agentic benchmarks and under robust defensive configurations. These findings highlight a significant residual reliability gap in current deepfake detector deployments and demonstrate the efficacy of agentic orchestration in identifying latent vulnerabilities.

AI.tldr: ARMOR++ uses a multi-agent framework (VLM + LLM orchestration) combining five complementary perturbation primitives to achieve high-transferability black-box adversarial attacks against deepfake detectors, significantly outperforming existing methods on the AADD-2025 benchmark.
AI.motivation: Deepfake detectors are vulnerable to black-box adversarial transfer attacks, but existing transfer attacks lack semantic awareness and struggle to maintain effectiveness under no-query constraints, especially when transferring from convolutional to transformer-based architectures.
AI.method: ARMOR++ integrates Qwen2.5-VL for spatial semantic priors, Qwen3 LLM for orchestrating primitive selection and hyperparameter reparameterization, and five complementary primitives: dense optimization, saliency-based, spatial transformations, frequency-domain perturbations, and block-structured modifications.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms existing agentic and non-agentic baselines across low- and high-quality image regimes on AADD-2025 benchmark, with substantial gains in blind-target ASR over state-of-the-art agentic baseline, and advantages under robust defensive configurations.
AI.conclusion: There is a significant residual reliability gap in current deepfake detector deployments, and agentic orchestration effectively identifies latent vulnerabilities.

title: Fast-Fading Channel and Power Optimization of the Magnetic Inductive Cellular Network
comment: This work has been accepted by the IEEE TWC for publication
summary: The cellular network of magnetic Induction (MI) communication holds promise in long-distance underground environments. In the traditional MI communication, there is no fast-fading channel since the MI channel is treated as a quasi-static channel. However, for the vehicle (mobile) MI (VMI) communication, the unpredictable antenna vibration brings the remarkable fast-fading. As such fast-fading cannot be modeled by the central limit theorem, it differs radically from other wireless fast-fading channels. Unfortunately, few studies focus on this phenomenon. In this paper, using a novel space modeling based on the electromagnetic field theorem, we propose a 3-dimension model of the VMI antenna vibration. By proposing ``conjugate pseudo-piecewise functions'' and boundary $p(x)$ distribution, we derive the cumulative distribution function (CDF), probability density function (PDF) and the expectation of the VMI fast-fading channel. We also theoretically analyze the effects of the VMI fast-fading on the network throughput, including the VMI outage probability which can be ignored in the traditional MI channel study. We draw several intriguing conclusions different from those in wireless fast-fading studies. For instance, the fast-fading brings more uniformly distributed channel coefficients. Finally, we propose the power control algorithm using the non-cooperative game and multiagent Q-learning methods to optimize the throughput of the cellular VMI network. Simulations validate the derivation and the proposed algorithm.

AI.tldr: This paper models vehicle magnetic induction (VMI) antenna vibration-induced fast-fading using electromagnetic field theory, derives statistical channel characteristics, analyzes impacts on network throughput and outage probability, and proposes a game-theoretic power control algorithm with multiagent Q-learning to optimize throughput in cellular VMI networks.
AI.motivation: Traditional MI communication treats channels as quasi-static with no fast-fading, but vehicle mobility causes unpredictable antenna vibration leading to remarkable fast-fading that cannot be modeled by the central limit theorem. Few studies address this phenomenon.
AI.method: Proposes a 3D model of VMI antenna vibration using electromagnetic field theorem; develops "conjugate pseudo-piecewise functions" and boundary p(x) distribution; derives CDF, PDF, and expectation of VMI fast-fading channels; proposes power control via non-cooperative game and multiagent Q-learning.
AI.result: Derives statistical distributions (CDF, PDF, expectation) for VMI fast-fading; theoretically analyzes effects on network throughput and outage probability; finds fast-fading yields more uniformly distributed channel coefficients unlike traditional wireless fast-fading.
AI.conclusion: The VMI fast-fading behavior differs significantly from conventional wireless fast-fading; the proposed game-theoretic power control with Q-learning effectively optimizes cellular VMI network throughput; simulations confirm the derivations and algorithm performance.

title: HoloGeo: Mitigating Landmark Bias in Geo-localization via Evidence-Driven Reasoning
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have significantly improved image geo-localization, yet existing models remain susceptible to landmark bias, causing them to overlook geographical cues or form spurious correlations, ultimately resulting in inaccurate localization. To systematically investigate this issue, we first design two quantitative metrics, Bias Intensity (BI) and Bias Harmfulness (BH), to characterize the impact of landmarks exerted on model reasoning, and establish a comprehensive benchmark, LandmarkBias-3K. To mitigate landmark bias, we further propose an evidence-driven reasoning framework, HoloGeo, to improve the reliability of geo-localization. HoloGeo is supported by a high-quality dataset, BF-30k, annotated with structured multi-evidence bias-free reasoning chains. By incorporating multi-dimensional rewards, HoloGeo explicitly encourages balanced attention over diverse visual cues and achieves evidence-driven joint reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HoloGeo not only maintains excellent performance on IM2GPS3K and YFCC4k but also significantly outperforms existing open-source VLMs on LandmarkBias-3K, validating its effectiveness for robust geospatial reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies landmark bias in VLM-based geo-localization, proposes metrics (BI/BH) and a benchmark (LandmarkBias-3K) to measure it, and introduces HoloGeo—an evidence-driven reasoning framework with a bias-free dataset (BF-30k) and multi-dimensional rewards—that outperforms existing models on robust geospatial reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs for image geo-localization suffer from **landmark bias**, where models either overlook geographical cues or form spurious correlations with landmarks, leading to inaccurate localization. No systematic metric or benchmark existed to characterize this problem.
AI.method: 1) Proposed **Bias Intensity (BI)** and **Bias Harmfulness (BH)** metrics to quantify landmark bias. 2) Built **LandmarkBias-3K** benchmark for evaluation. 3) Introduced **HoloGeo**, an evidence-driven reasoning framework trained on **BF-30k** (structured multi-evidence bias-free reasoning chains) with multi-dimensional rewards to encourage balanced attention across diverse visual cues.
AI.result: HoloGeo maintains excellent performance on standard benchmarks (IM2GPS3K, YFCC4k) and **significantly outperforms** existing open-source VLMs on LandmarkBias-3K, demonstrating robust geospatial reasoning.
AI.conclusion: HoloGeo effectively mitigates landmark bias and improves geo-localization reliability, validating that structured multi-evidence reasoning with balanced attention is a promising direction for robust geospatial reasoning in VLMs.

title: SceneBind: Binding What and Where Across Vision, Audio and Language
summary: We present SceneBind, an omni-modal representation of realistic scenes with joint semantic and 3D spatial understanding across vision, audio and language. Existing omni-modal encoders excel at instance-level semantics (i.e., what is present), but often lack explicit spatial structure (i.e., where it is). SceneBind addresses this gap by representing each scene as a semantic-spatial entity, combining a global semantic embedding with object-centric semantic-spatial slots. This representation explicitly captures object-level semantics, spatial attributes, and uncertainty. We further propose SceneBind Matching, a semantic-spatial matching scheme that integrates global scene similarity with object alignment, supporting cross-modal scene retrieval and object grounding. To train and evaluate SceneBind, we curate a novel real-world binaural audio-visual dataset with structured semantic and spatial annotations, and propose a training protocol for aligning semantic and spatial signals across modalities. SceneBind is compatible with large-scale pretrained semantic encoders, adds lightweight spatial modeling with only a few additional tokens. It achieves state-of-the-art scene and spatial retrieval while enabling strong zero-shot transfer to downstream tasks such as audio-visual localization.

AI.tldr: SceneBind is an omni-modal representation that jointly captures semantic and 3D spatial understanding across vision, audio, and language, using object-centric semantic-spatial slots and a matching scheme for cross-modal retrieval and grounding, achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Existing omni-modal encoders capture instance-level semantics (what is present) but lack explicit spatial structure (where it is). SceneBind addresses this gap by representing scenes as semantic-spatial entities.
AI.method: Represents each scene as a semantic-spatial entity combining global semantic embedding with object-centric semantic-spatial slots. Proposes SceneBind Matching for cross-modal scene retrieval and object grounding. Curates a real-world binaural audio-visual dataset with structured annotations and a training protocol for aligning semantic and spatial signals.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art scene and spatial retrieval, enables strong zero-shot transfer to downstream tasks like audio-visual localization, and is compatible with large-scale pretrained semantic encoders with only a few additional tokens.
AI.conclusion: SceneBind effectively bridges the semantic-spatial gap in omni-modal representation, providing a lightweight yet powerful framework for cross-modal scene understanding and retrieval.

title: Motion-Conditioned Multi-View Fusion for Myocardial Infarction Localization from Echocardiography
summary: Myocardial infarction (MI) remains a leading cause of mortality worldwide. Echocardiography (Echo) is a widely available modality for MI assessment, where regional wall motion abnormality is a key indicator. Prior learning based methods for myocardial motion analysis often use handcrafted descriptors or densely supervised estimation, but the need for extensive annotation limits applicability. Foundation models have recently improved vision-based Echo analysis; however, most methods operate on single views and segment-level localization remains unreliable under view-dependent ambiguity, especially in apical views. To address this, we propose MCF-Net, a novel motion-guided multi-view fusion framework that fuses myocardial motion cues with foundation model representations to localize infarction. Visual features are extracted using EchoPrime, a pretrained Echo foundation model shared across dual views. Cardiac motion is modeled with extremely sparse supervision: a single annotated template frame is transferred across videos to initialize point tracking, avoiding dense labels. Motion-derived segment-aware soft masks provide coarse spatial priors that selectively enhance features for challenging myocardial segments. A motion-conditioned fusion mechanism then integrates motion and vision across views, refining predictions without overriding strong appearance cues. On segment-level MI localization, MCF-Net achieves 72.4\% F1 and 84.9\% accuracy, outperforming state-of-the-art motion-only, vision-only, and fusion baselines.

AI.tldr: MCF-Net is a motion-guided multi-view fusion framework that combines myocardial motion cues with foundation model representations (EchoPrime) for segment-level MI localization from echocardiography, using extremely sparse annotation (single template frame) and achieving 72.4% F1 and 84.9% accuracy, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current myocardial motion analysis methods rely on handcrafted descriptors or dense supervision requiring extensive annotations. Foundation models for Echo analysis operate on single views and suffer from unreliable segment-level localization under view-dependent ambiguity, especially in apical views.
AI.method: MCF-Net uses EchoPrime (pretrained Echo foundation model) for dual-view visual feature extraction. Cardiac motion is modeled with extremely sparse supervision by transferring a single annotated template frame across videos for point tracking. Motion-derived segment-aware soft masks provide coarse spatial priors, and a motion-conditioned fusion mechanism integrates motion and vision across views.
AI.result: MCF-Net achieves 72.4% F1 and 84.9% accuracy on segment-level MI localization, outperforming state-of-the-art motion-only, vision-only, and fusion baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-view fusion framework effectively addresses view-dependent ambiguity and reduces annotation burden while improving MI localization performance, demonstrating the value of combining sparse motion cues with foundation model features.

title: Online Neural Space Time Memory for Dynamic Novel View Synthesis
comment: 15 pages. Preprint. Project page with demos and video results: https://nst-mem.github.io
summary: Online novel view synthesis from multi-view streaming videos faces a fundamental trade-off: maintaining a persistent, long-horizon memory to reconstruct temporarily occluded regions while operating under strict real-time constraints. While Test-Time Training (TTT) offers a powerful memory mechanism, standard models mandate gradient-based memory updates at every frame to adapt to the changing motion in dynamic scenes. The computational cost of heavy memory updates precludes real-time application and can lead to instability over long contexts. Given that memory updates are more demanding than memory application and video content is largely redundant, we propose to decouple the frequencies of these two processes. Our approach performs periodic memory updates while applying the memory on a per-frame basis, using cross-view attention to manage deformations between the prior memory state and the current frame. To lock in the historical context, we introduce two critical mechanisms: an auxiliary Memory Loss that forces persistent internalization of the scene, and a Memory Caching strategy that regularizes active weights against catastrophic drift. Our method demonstrates real-time, state-of-the-art performance on scenes with dynamic human motion as well as minute-scale online memorization.

AI.tldr: Proposes decoupling memory update frequency from memory application frequency for real-time novel view synthesis from multi-view streaming video, achieving state-of-the-art performance with periodic updates and per-frame memory application using cross-view attention.
AI.motivation: Online novel view synthesis from multi-view streaming videos faces a trade-off between maintaining long-horizon memory for occluded regions and operating under strict real-time constraints. Standard TTT models require gradient-based memory updates at every frame, which is computationally prohibitive.
AI.method: Decouples memory update frequency from memory application frequency: periodic memory updates with per-frame memory application, cross-view attention for deformation management, auxiliary Memory Loss for scene internalization, and Memory Caching to prevent catastrophic drift.
AI.result: Real-time, state-of-the-art performance on scenes with dynamic human motion and minute-scale online memorization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed frequency decoupling approach enables real-time novel view synthesis while maintaining long-horizon memory, outperforming standard TTT models in both efficiency and stability.

title: MeanFlowNFT: Bringing Forward-Process RL to Average-Velocity Generators
comment: Project Page: https://harahan.github.io/meanflownft-project-page/, GitHub: https://github.com/Harahan/MeanFlowNFT, Hugging Face: https://huggingface.co/Harahan/MeanFlowNFT
summary: MeanFlow generators achieve fast few-step sampling by predicting average velocities over time intervals, making them attractive for efficient generation. Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a powerful way to align diffusion and flow models with human preferences and task-specific objectives. In particular, DiffusionNFT offers an efficient forward-process RL framework that does not require reverse-process trajectories or likelihood estimation. However, applying such RL methods to MeanFlow remains underexplored. DiffusionNFT optimizes instantaneous velocities, whereas MeanFlow samples with average velocities. To bridge this gap, we introduce MeanFlowNFT. Inspired by the MeanFlow identity, which bridges average and instantaneous velocities, we construct an induced instantaneous-velocity predictor. We apply the DiffusionNFT objective to this predictor, making reward optimization well-defined for MeanFlow. Sampling remains based on the average velocity, preserving MeanFlow's fast few-step generation. We further prove that MeanFlowNFT inherits DiffusionNFT's strict policy-improvement guarantee. Experiments on image and video generation show that MeanFlowNFT consistently improves baselines. Moreover, it outperforms prior state-of-the-art RL-tuned few-step generators on most metrics ($6$ of $8$ on SD3.5-M), and can even surpass multi-step RL-tuned diffusion while using only a few sampling steps. For instance, on Wan 2.1, $4$-step MeanFlowNFT reaches a VBench score of $84.33$, surpassing $50$-step LongCat-Video RL ($82.57$).

AI.tldr: MeanFlowNFT bridges reinforcement learning with MeanFlow generators by constructing an induced instantaneous-velocity predictor, enabling DiffusionNFT-style RL optimization while maintaining few-step sampling, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical results on image and video generation.
AI.motivation: To enable RL-based alignment for MeanFlow generators (which use average velocities), since existing DiffusionNFT optimizes instantaneous velocities, creating a gap that prevents direct application of efficient forward-process RL to MeanFlow models.
AI.method: Constructs an induced instantaneous-velocity predictor via the MeanFlow identity, applies DiffusionNFT's RL objective to this predictor, while sampling still uses average velocities to preserve fast few-step generation.
AI.result: Outperforms prior state-of-the-art RL-tuned few-step generators on 6/8 metrics on SD3.5-M; 4-step MeanFlowNFT on Wan 2.1 achieves VBench 84.33, surpassing 50-step LongCat-Video RL (82.57).
AI.conclusion: MeanFlowNFT successfully extends RL alignment to MeanFlow models with theoretical guarantees and practical superiority, even surpassing multi-step RL-tuned diffusion models with fewer sampling steps.

title: Hierarchical Denoising For Multi-Step Visual Reasoning
summary: Video models are evolving into vision foundation models, yet they still lack human-like multi-step reasoning. Streaming autoregressive diffusion models are efficient but limited in reasoning, while bidirectional diffusion enables global revision with high inference costs due to dense frame-level denoising. Both paradigms struggle to achieve logical consistency and low-latency streaming for complex reasoning tasks. We propose HDR (Hierarchical Denoising for Visual Reasoning), a unified framework that integrates hierarchical latents into causal video generation for multi-step reasoning. HDR organizes video latents into a tree-structured hierarchy, enabling coarse-to-fine reasoning before streaming output. Coarse denoising layers preserve uncertain hypotheses for global planning, while finer layers progressively refine them into concrete visual states. A sparse hierarchical attention pattern (SHAP) further reduces temporal attention costs. We introduce a level-stratified multi-step video reasoning benchmark with out-of-distribution cases, covering six tasks: maze navigation, Tower of Hanoi, one-line drawing, sliding puzzle, Sokoban, and water pouring. Compared with streaming autoregressive diffusion baselines, HDR improves success from 34.22 to 60.29 (76.2% relative gain) and increases average progress from 76.00 to 89.56, demonstrating more consistent reasoning trajectories. HDR maintains low-latency streaming at 0.70 seconds per latent, achieving 54.2 times faster inference than bidirectional diffusion. It also retains 82.9% of full-data performance with only 2% training data, compared with 52.0% for bidirectional diffusion. Real-world robot experiments further demonstrate HDR's potential for physical interaction and world modeling. Project demo: https://hierarchical-diffusion-reasoning.github.io/.

AI.tldr: HDR (Hierarchical Denoising for Visual Reasoning) is a unified framework that integrates hierarchical latents into causal video generation, enabling coarse-to-fine multi-step reasoning with low-latency streaming. It achieves 76.2% relative improvement in success rate, 54.2x faster inference than bidirectional diffusion, and strong few-shot learning capability.
AI.motivation: Existing video models lack human-like multi-step reasoning. Streaming autoregressive diffusion models are efficient but poor at reasoning, while bidirectional diffusion enables global revision but incurs high inference costs. Both fail to achieve logical consistency and low-latency streaming for complex reasoning tasks.
AI.method: HDR organizes video latents into a tree-structured hierarchy with coarse-to-fine reasoning. Coarse denoising layers preserve uncertain hypotheses for global planning; finer layers progressively refine them. A sparse hierarchical attention pattern (SHAP) reduces temporal attention costs.
AI.result: HDR improves success from 34.22 to 60.29 (76.2% relative gain), increases average progress from 76.00 to 89.56, achieves 0.70s per latent (54.2x faster than bidirectional diffusion), and retains 82.9% of full-data performance with only 2% training data (vs. 52.0% for bidirectional diffusion).
AI.conclusion: HDR effectively bridges the gap between efficient streaming generation and complex multi-step visual reasoning, demonstrating strong performance across six benchmark tasks and real-world robot experiments for physical interaction and world modeling.
